title: OHVIRA syndrome: hysteroscopy and intraoperative sonography – a value addition in the management
summary: Obstructed hemivagina and ipsilateral renal agenesis (OHVIRA) syndrome is a congenital müllerian anomaly that presents with dysmenorrhea and reduced menstrual flow due to obstruction of one hemivagina. The authors demonstrate that vaginal septal incision and drainage effectively treats this condition, with vaginoscopy using a hysteroscope providing critical visualization of the vaginal anatomy both before and after the surgical intervention. Notably, spontaneous decompression of hematocolpos can occasionally occur when communication develops between the two hemivaginas through fistula or microperforation. The case report highlights the diagnostic and therapeutic value of combining hysteroscopy with intraoperative sonography in the management of OHVIRA syndrome, offering improved surgical precision and visualization compared to conventional approaches. These imaging modalities enable accurate identification of the anatomical abnormality and confirmation of successful treatment, supporting their adoption as standard adjuncts in the surgical management of complex müllerian anomalies.

title: Particles and Prejudice: Nanomedicine Approaches to Reducing Health Disparities in Endometrial Cancer
summary: This article examines endometrial cancer biology and emerging nanomedicine approaches, with particular attention to health disparities affecting Black women. Type I endometrial cancer, which comprises 80–90% of cases and is strongly associated with obesity and estrogen exposure, contrasts with the more aggressive Type II subtype characterized by TP53 mutations in approximately 90% of cases and disproportionately affecting Black women, who experience twice the mortality rate despite similar incidence. Multiple molecular pathways drive endometrial cancer development, including the PI3K-AKT pathway and mutations in CTNNB1, KRAS, and POLE, while estrogen receptor status plays a critical role in treatment selection. The article highlights several risk factors including reproductive factors (early menarche, late menopause, infertility, anovulation), hormonal imbalances (unopposed estrogen, PCOS), and hereditary syndromes (Lynch and Cowden). Emerging nanomedicine strategies show promise for improving outcomes, including carbon nanoparticles and indocyanine green for sentinel lymph node detection, surface-enhanced Raman scattering with tumor extracellular vesicles for liquid biopsy diagnostics, and porphysomes for intraoperative tumor visualization. The review underscores that early diagnosis through precision medicine approaches utilizing nanoparticle-based therapeutics could address current treatment limitations and help reduce the substantial survival disparities observed in vulnerable populations.

title: Uterine rupture of an unscarred gravid uterus at term attributed to adenomyosis
summary: This case report describes an unusual presentation of uterine rupture in an unscarred, term gravid uterus attributed to adenomyosis rather than the typical risk factor of previous caesarean section or scar tissue. Histological examination of the rupture site revealed diffuse adenomyosis, establishing a direct pathological association between the adenomyotic lesions and uterine rupture. The patient, a primigravida without prior uterine surgery, presented in cardiac arrest with massive haemoperitoneum secondary to the rupture, demonstrating the severe clinical consequences of adenomyosis-related uterine compromise. This case is notable because adenomyosis is not conventionally recognized as a primary risk factor for uterine rupture, which is typically associated with scar tissue from prior interventions. The findings suggest that severe adenomyosis may structurally weaken the myometrium sufficiently to precipitate rupture even in the absence of surgical scarring. This report expands understanding of adenomyosis pathophysiology and highlights an unexpected obstetric complication that clinicians should consider in pregnant patients with adenomyosis.

title: Spontaneous haemoperitoneum in the second trimester of pregnancy
summary:
# Summary: Spontaneous Haemoperitoneum in the Second Trimester of Pregnancy

Spontaneous haemoperitoneum in pregnancy (SHiP) is a rare but serious obstetric emergency frequently caused by vascular rupture originating from pre-existing endometriosis. Endometriotic lesions can undergo abnormal neovascularization during pregnancy, creating fragile vessels prone to spontaneous rupture and intra-abdominal bleeding. This case report documents the presentation and management of SHiP occurring in the second trimester, highlighting the previously underrecognized association between endometriosis and life-threatening hemorrhage during pregnancy. Transcatheter arterial embolization emerged as a critical therapeutic intervention, offering a minimally invasive alternative to emergency hysterectomy or laparotomy that preserves both maternal life and reproductive capacity. These findings underscore the importance of recognizing endometriosis as a significant risk factor for SHiP and demonstrate that interventional radiology techniques can provide effective, pregnancy-preserving treatment when managing this obstetric emergency.

title: Utero-cutaneous fistula following caesarean section
summary: Utero-cutaneous fistula is a rare complication that can develop following caesarean section, resulting from abnormal tract formation between the uterus and abdominal wall. The condition typically presents with cyclical bleeding from an abnormal opening at the previous caesarean scar site, as demonstrated in a case of a multiparous woman in her twenties who experienced hemorrhage from her surgical scar. The pathophysiology involves a persistent fistulous connection that allows uterine bleeding to drain externally through the cutaneous defect. Surgical management consisting of complete resection of the fistulous tract, closure of the resulting uterine defect, and placement of a supportive omental patch effectively treats this complication. This case highlights the importance of recognizing utero-cutaneous fistula as a potential post-operative sequela of caesarean delivery and the efficacy of surgical intervention in restoring normal anatomy and resolving symptoms.

title: Myomatous erythrocytosis syndrome: a uterine fibroid associated with polycythaemia
summary: Myomatous erythrocytosis syndrome (MES) is a rare condition in which uterine myomas autonomously produce erythropoietin (EPO), causing abnormal elevations in red cell count, hemoglobin, and hematocrit levels with an estimated incidence of 0.02–0.5%. The pathophysiology involves EPO produced by the myoma binding to erythropoietin receptors and activating JAK2-STAT5 signaling pathways, which promote erythropoiesis and proliferation of erythroid precursors in bone marrow. Alternative mechanisms may include compression of renal vessels or urinary tract by the myoma, leading to renal hypoperfusion or elevated parenchymal pressure that stimulates EPO secretion from the kidneys. Surgical treatment through myomectomy or hysterectomy is definitive and effectively resolves the hematological abnormalities, with patients' blood values returning to normal within weeks to months after tumor removal. Phlebotomy may provide temporary symptomatic relief in patients experiencing hyperviscosity-related symptoms such as headaches, though it is not curative. This case illustrates the importance of recognizing MES as a paraneoplastic syndrome associated with uterine fibroids, particularly in patients with unexplained polycythemia.

title: Mycoplasma hominis peritonitis after oocyte donation
summary:
# Mycoplasma hominis Peritonitis after Oocyte Donation: Clinical Summary

This case report documents a rare but serious complication of transvaginal oocyte retrieval for assisted reproduction: peritonitis caused by *Mycoplasma hominis*, a fastidious bacterium that normally colonizes the genitourinary tract in 25–67% of women. The infection likely arose when the retrieval procedure inadvertently introduced this pathogen into the peritoneal cavity, causing acute peritonitis with characteristic laboratory findings including leucocytosis, neutrophilia, and elevated C-reactive protein. The patient presented with an acute abdomen and was successfully treated with a combination of intravenous clindamycin, ampicillin, and gentamicin, followed by a 14-day course of oral doxycycline, achieving complete resolution. This case highlights that *Mycoplasma hominis* possesses intrinsic resistance to β-lactams and vancomycin due to its lack of a cell wall, requiring targeted antimicrobial therapy with agents that inhibit protein synthesis or DNA replication, such as tetracyclines, lincosamines, and fluoroquinolones. Women with risk factors including endometriosis, pelvic inflammatory disease, or pelvic adhesions warrant antibiotic prophylaxis during oocyte retrieval procedures to prevent this potentially life-threatening infection.

title: Hysteropathological Correlation in Abnormal Uterine Bleeding in a Tertiary Care Hospital in South India
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic utility of hysteroscopy combined with histopathological examination in patients presenting with abnormal uterine bleeding at a tertiary care center in South India. Hysteroscopy identified multiple characteristic intra-uterine findings including strawberry patterns, tongue-shaped projections, and polypoidal appearances, which correlated with specific histopathological diagnoses. Strawberry appearance on hysteroscopy was associated with secretory changes on histopathology, while polypoidal patterns indicated endometrial hyperplasia, and cerebroid appearance suggested endometrial carcinoma. The most common histopathological findings were secretory changes (35%) and proliferative changes (26.67%), with endometrial carcinoma accounting for 6.67% of cases. The combination of hysteroscopy with endometrial sampling significantly improved diagnostic accuracy for intrauterine pathology, particularly for detecting endometrial cancer and polyps. These findings establish hysteroscopy as a safe, reliable, and well-tolerated diagnostic procedure for comprehensively evaluating abnormal uterine bleeding across all age groups when combined with histopathological correlation.

title: Complications in spinal cord injury persons with “traditional” colostomy: a case series
summary: Spinal cord injury and disease frequently cause neurogenic bowel dysfunction, a condition that can be managed through colostomy when conservative treatments prove ineffective. However, this case series demonstrates that traditional colostomy procedures, particularly Hartmann's procedure, present significant complications in spinal cord-injured patients. The anatomical positioning of the stoma and retention of a large colon segment predispose patients to persistent constipation, which can progress to sub-occlusion or complete bowel obstruction with associated gastroparesis. Additional complications include parastomal hernias, with one patient experiencing a strangulated hernia requiring intervention. Management of these colostomy-related complications typically requires intensive laxative therapy and frequent daily stoma washes, highlighting the ongoing burden of symptom control in this population. These findings suggest that alternative surgical approaches or more careful patient selection may be warranted to improve outcomes in spinal cord-injured individuals requiring bowel diversion.

title: Route of Delivery in a Patient with Vaginal Stenosis from Stevens-Johnson Syndrome and Review of the Management of Genital Complications
summary: Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) are severe mucocutaneous reactions most commonly triggered by medications including antibiotics (cephalosporins, penicillins, sulfa derivatives), NSAIDs, antiepileptics, and antiretroviral agents. These conditions cause painful erosions and ulcerations affecting multiple mucosal surfaces, with vulvovaginal involvement occurring in 12.7% of SJS cases and up to 70% of TEN cases, potentially leading to chronic vaginal stenosis, synechiae, and erosive vaginitis. Long-term genital sequelae from SJS/TEN can result in significant reproductive morbidity including sexual dysfunction, infertility, preterm labor, and obstetric complications necessitating cesarean delivery. Management involves early initiation of topical and intravaginal steroids, vaginal estrogen formulations, menstrual suppression, and pelvic floor physical therapy to prevent progressive vulvovaginal scarring. The case presented describes a primigravida at term with severe vaginal stenosis from prior NSAID and cephalosporin-induced SJS requiring cesarean delivery due to genital scarring. Clinicians should recognize that SJS and TEN survivors of childbearing age require comprehensive gynecologic evaluation and early aggressive management of genital manifestations to minimize long-term reproductive complications.

title: The emerging field of viroimmunotherapy for pediatric brain tumors
summary: Pediatric brain tumors present significant clinical challenges due to their molecular heterogeneity and complex interactions between tumor cells and the microenvironment, which currently limit treatment efficacy. Oncolytic viruses are emerging as a promising therapeutic approach for brain tumors, operating through a dual mechanism: direct cytopathic killing of tumor cells followed by activation of antitumor immune responses. The antitumor immune response triggered by oncolytic virus infection contributes substantially to tumor mass debulking, representing a shift in understanding how virotherapy achieves its therapeutic benefits beyond simple viral cytotoxicity. A critical barrier to optimizing viroimmunotherapy remains the incomplete understanding of how tumor and microenvironment interactions influence immune activation and viral efficacy. This review highlights oncolytic viruses as a feasible and safe emerging strategy for pediatric brain tumors, with potential to overcome some limitations of conventional therapies when immunological mechanisms are properly leveraged.

title: Unusual ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome presentation: Pleural effusion without ascites. A case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes an unusual presentation of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) manifesting as isolated pleural effusion without ascites. Controlled ovarian hyperstimulation during in vitro fertilization triggered OHSS, which caused characteristic hemodynamic changes including hypotension, severe tachycardia, decreased renal perfusion, and hemoconcentration, accompanied by laboratory findings of elevated hematocrit, reduced serum osmolality and sodium, elevated D-dimer, leukocytosis, and thrombocytosis. The patient presented with dyspnea and pleural effusion, which is a rare complication occurring in approximately 80% of OHSS cases with isolated pleural effusion on the right side. Management employed a multimodal supportive approach including fluid balance management, albumin and furosemide administration, continuous pleural cavity drainage, thoracentesis, thromboembolic prophylaxis with enoxaparin, and symptomatic treatment with paracetamol and oxygen therapy. Prophylactic strategies for future cycles include utilizing anti-Müllerian hormone and antral follicle count to assess ovarian reserve, and employing GnRH agonist triggering with embryo cryopreservation to mitigate OHSS risk in high responders. The patient achieved a successful dichorionic diamniotic pregnancy that progressed without complications to term at 37 weeks.

title: Differential diagnosis of uterine vascular anomalies: Uterine pseudoaneurysm as a cause of massive hemorrhage
summary:
# Summary

Uterine artery pseudoaneurysm (UAP), an acquired vascular lesion characterized by extraluminal blood collection contained by fibrin and platelet crosslinks, represents a significant cause of massive hemorrhage in reproductive-aged women. Multiple iatrogenic and obstetric procedures including cesarean section, vaginal delivery, curettage, hysteroscopic surgery, and conization can precipitate UAP formation, with the condition also associated with deep endometriosis. Clinical presentations vary from asymptomatic findings to life-threatening complications including massive hemorrhage, haematuria, thrombosis, and secondary hydronephrosis. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities such as Doppler ultrasound (which reveals the pathognomonic to-and-fro blood flow pattern), magnetic resonance imaging, and computed tomography angiography, with digital angiography providing definitive confirmation. The article also comprehensively reviews related uterine vascular anomalies including arteriovenous malformations and fistulas, which similarly cause hemorrhagic complications and menorrhagia, with embolization established as the first-line uterine-sparing therapeutic option across these vascular pathologies.

title: Factors influencing vaginal cuff dehiscence after laparoscopic hysterectomy and the psychological state of the patients
summary: Vaginal cuff dehiscence is a serious complication following laparoscopic total hysterectomy, with multiple modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors identified through multivariate analysis. Preoperative hemoglobin levels below 90 g/L and resumption of sexual activity within three months of surgery are the two strongest independent predictors of dehiscence, suggesting that anemia impairs wound healing while premature sexual activity causes direct mechanical injury and disrupts vaginal microecology. Additional risk factors include the vaginal cuff suture method, with intermittent suturing conferring higher risk than continuous suturing, as well as postmenopausal status and conditions that increase abdominal pressure such as constipation and chronic cough. Preventive strategies include preoperative anemia correction, prolonged sexual abstinence (at least three months postoperatively), estrogen supplementation for postmenopausal women, and postoperative measures to reduce intraperitoneal pressure through diet modification, stool softeners, and treatment of conditions causing increased abdominal pressure. When dehiscence occurs, treatment options include vaginal suturing, conservative management, or emergency exploratory laparotomy depending on severity, with strengthened postoperative follow-up and timely intervention for cuff bleeding and inflammation reducing the risk of progression to complete dehiscence.

title: Menstruation: An Important Indicator for Assessing Stroke Risk and Its Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Abnormal menstruation patterns serve as an important clinical indicator of stroke risk in premenopausal women, reflecting underlying reproductive and systemic pathologies that directly impact cerebrovascular health. Endometrial and uterine abnormalities, including adenomyosis and leiomyomas, promote heavy menstrual bleeding that triggers iron-deficiency anemia and activates systemic hypercoagulability through elevated coagulation factors, fibrinogen, and platelet counts, collectively increasing thrombotic stroke risk. Conditions affecting menstruation—including endometriosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome, and immune thrombocytopenia—independently elevate stroke risk through inflammatory pathways, metabolic dysfunction, and platelet abnormalities, respectively. Protective endogenous estrogens normally promote vasodilation, cell survival, and angiogenesis to prevent non-cardioembolic ischemic stroke, but this cardioprotection is lost through hormonal contraceptive use, surgical interventions like oophorectomy and hysterectomy, and environmental exposures to xenoestrogens and nicotine. The decline in estradiol during menopause, coupled with relative androgen excess, substantially worsens stroke risk factors and cardiovascular disease incidence. Clinicians should assess menstrual patterns, heavy menstrual bleeding, and related reproductive pathologies as integrated markers of stroke vulnerability and treatment targets for stroke prevention in women.

title: Risk Factors of Complications After Uterine Artery Embolisation for Symptomatic Fibroids: A Case–Control Study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study identified multiple risk factors for complications following uterine artery embolisation (UAE), a well-established minimally invasive treatment for symptomatic fibroids. The major diameter of the fibroids emerged as the primary factor associated with both early and late complications, while the presence of an intrauterine device, multiple fibroids, and incomplete fibroid devascularisation significantly increased early complication risk. Early complications consisted primarily of infection (30.4%), including sepsis cases, along with pain-related hospitalisations and occasional fibroid expulsion, whereas late complications included symptom recurrence and were associated with immunosuppression and chronic medication use. Non-devascularised fibroids represented a critical procedural failure point, occurring in approximately one-third of complicated cases compared to 12% of controls. The findings suggest that careful patient selection based on fibroid size, thorough devascularisation technique, and consideration of IUD removal prior to treatment may help reduce the substantial morbidity associated with UAE, with hysterectomy remaining an option for severe complications.

title: Infrared Spectroscopy Coupled with Machine Learning
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated vascular dysfunction induced by estrogen deficiency using ovariectomized rats as a model for postmenopausal conditions. Ovariectomy caused multiple physiological changes including increased body weight, decreased uterine weight, and compromised vascular function characterized by increased oxidative stress and reduced nitric oxide bioavailability, both of which elevated vascular resistance. Female sex hormones normally regulate vascular function through nitric oxide and endothelium-dependent hyperpolarization mechanisms that control smooth muscle tone; their loss disrupts these pathways and impairs coronary artery vasodilation. The study employed attenuated total reflectance Fourier-transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy combined with machine learning algorithms, including principal component analysis (PCA) and partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA), to detect spectroscopic signatures of vascular dysfunction. These findings demonstrate that infrared spectroscopy coupled with chemometric analysis can serve as a diagnostic tool for identifying vascular pathology associated with estrogen deficiency and related conditions such as atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease.

title: A rare but troublesome complication of cesarean section: the uterocutaneous fistula. Report of two cases and review of literature
summary:
# Summary

Uterocutaneous fistula (UCF) is a rare but serious complication of cesarean section, typically arising from post-operative surgical site infections caused by bacteria such as Escherichia coli and Enterococcus faecalis, which trigger fever and systemic inflammation. Multiple etiological factors can precipitate fistula formation, including incomplete uterine closure, placental remnants, endometriosis, tuberculosis, and prolonged drain placement, with patients presenting with characteristic symptoms of hyperpyrexia, pain, and abnormal vaginal discharge. Diagnostic imaging combining transabdominal and transvaginal ultrasound with CT fistulography and MRI effectively characterizes the fistula's location and anatomy, while hydrogen peroxide testing serves as an inexpensive confirmatory method. Treatment approaches are multimodal, utilizing appropriate antibiotic therapy to minimize tissue damage and facilitate subsequent reconstructive surgery to preserve fertility, though hysterectomy may be necessary when concomitant uterine pathology such as fibromatosis or salpingitis is present. Antibiotic prophylaxis significantly reduces the incidence of post-cesarean wound infections and represents an important preventive measure against this devastating complication.

title: Successful treatment of large adenomyosis with transcervical radiofrequency ablation in a high-risk patient: A case report and short review
summary: Adenomyosis, characterized by endometrial invasion into the myometrium, is a prevalent gynecological condition in women of reproductive age that causes heavy bleeding, severe dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and impaired fertility. While medical therapies including combined oral contraceptives, GnRH modulators, and progestins provide symptomatic relief, transcervical radiofrequency ablation (RFA) has emerged as an effective minimally invasive alternative, particularly for patients unsuitable for or resistant to medical management. In comparative studies, transcervical RFA demonstrated superior outcomes compared to high-intensity focused ultrasound and percutaneous microwave ablation, achieving greater reductions in uterine and adenomyosis volumes (46.8% versus 33.6%) with a significantly lower minor adverse event rate of 3.6%. This case report demonstrates successful treatment of large adenomyosis in a high-risk patient, with the patient achieving marked symptom improvement including resolution of dysmenorrhea and hypermenorrhea by six months and enhanced fertility outcomes, with transcervical RFA showing a 91% pregnancy rate in patients with fertility desires. Although RFA carries risks including uterine adhesions (2.5% postoperative incidence) and postoperative symptoms such as abdominal discomfort and vaginal discharge, it represents a uterus-sparing option that preserves reproductive potential while avoiding the definitive nature of hysterectomy.

title: Successful Management of Complications Due to a Cesarean Section in a Patient With a Complex Obstetric History: A Case Report and Review of Preventive Strategies
summary: This case report examines post-cesarean section complications in a patient with a complex obstetric history involving multiple C-sections and myomectomy. Cesarean delivery and tissue disruption during surgery are major risk factors for intra-abdominal adhesion formation, with adhesion rates ranging from 24-73% depending on surgical technique, and multiple prior cesarean sections significantly increasing adhesion severity. These adhesions cause substantial morbidity, manifesting as chronic pelvic pain, nausea, vomiting, dyspareunia, and accounting for 15-20% of female infertility cases and 80% of chronic postoperative pain. Prevention strategies include barrier methods, hyaluronic acid gels, oxidized regenerated cellulose, and careful surgical techniques such as closed peritoneal closure and cystoinflation to prevent bladder injury. Diagnostic approaches employ laparoscopy as the gold standard, with non-invasive alternatives like transvaginal ultrasound and the "sliding sign" helping identify patients at high adhesion risk. Treatment through lysis of adhesions successfully relieved this patient's dysmenorrhea and anemia, demonstrating the clinical benefit of surgical intervention in symptomatic cases.

title: Out-of-Pocket Expenditure (OOPE) on Selected Surgeries in the Obstetrics and Gynaecology Department incurred by Ayushman Bharat Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya  Yojana (AB-PMJAY), Private Health Insurance and Uninsured Patients in a Tertiary Care Teaching Hospital in Karnataka state of India.
summary: This study evaluates the financial impact of health insurance coverage on out-of-pocket expenditures for gynecological surgeries in India, examining outcomes across the government-funded AB-PMJAY scheme, private insurance, and uninsured patients. The AB-PMJAY program substantially reduces out-of-pocket expenditure and enhances financial risk protection for patients undergoing common obstetric and gynecological procedures, while private health insurance also reduces costs, though considerable financial burden remains. Several surgical interventions treat major reproductive conditions: laparoscopic myomectomy addresses uterine fibroids, which cause abnormal bleeding, pelvic pain, and infertility; laparoscopic cystectomy treats ovarian cysts associated with pelvic pain and irregular cycles; and both LAVH and laparoscopic hysterectomy manage uterine prolapse, menorrhagia, fibroids, and endometriosis. The study demonstrates that high out-of-pocket expenses create barriers to timely healthcare access among low-income populations, contributing to unfavorable outcomes and elevated maternal mortality rates, whereas health insurance possession correlates with increased healthcare utilization. By preventing catastrophic health expenditure and reducing poverty-related medical debt, government insurance schemes like AB-PMJAY play a critical role in improving financial protection and health equity in reproductive care.

title: Now is the time to introduce new innovative assisted reproduction methods to implement accessible, affordable, and demonstrably successful advanced infertility services in resource-poor countries
summary:
# Summary

This article addresses infertility in resource-poor countries by identifying key causative factors and promoting accessible treatment innovations. Multiple female factors contribute to infertility, including endometriosis, adenomyosis, diminished ovarian reserve, chronic anovulation, and tubal factor infertility, while sexually transmitted infections and unsafe abortions represent major preventable causes of tubal blockages and secondary infertility in low-resource settings. For treatment, the article advocates affordable and effective options including mild ovarian stimulation protocols with agents like clomiphene citrate, gonadotrophins, and letrozole; intrauterine insemination for unexplained and moderate male infertility; and endoscopic surgery for conditions such as endometriosis and intrauterine adhesions. A simplified IVF culture system (SCS) is presented as a cost-effective innovation that achieves successful outcomes comparable to intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI), with added benefits of lower preterm birth and low birthweight rates. The Walking Egg Project exemplifies efforts to make advanced reproductive technologies accessible and affordable in resource-limited settings. Collectively, these evidence-based approaches demonstrate how innovation combined with simplified protocols can deliver demonstrably successful infertility services to underserved populations globally.

title: Anatomical, Pathophysiological, and Clinical Aspects of Extra-Pelvic Varicose Veins of Pelvic Origin
summary:
# Summary

Extra-pelvic varicose veins of pelvic origin result from pelvic vein incompetence and venous hypertension that transmit reflux to perineal and lower extremity vessels through anatomical "escape points." Pregnancy represents a major risk factor, with elevated estrogen, progesterone, and relaxin weakening vein walls, while the gravid uterus causes direct compression and increased blood volume expands venous capacity. Additional mechanisms include anatomical compressions—such as May-Thurner syndrome affecting the left common iliac vein and left renal vein compression—and pathological conditions like endometriosis causing extrinsic compression of pelvic structures. Venous hypertension in pelvic plexuses is transmitted through the uterine venous plexus and round ligament veins to multiple escape points including the clitoral, labial, and gluteal regions, leading to characteristic varicosities at the mons pubis, labia, perineum, and anterior thighs. In women, pelvic venous hypertension manifests clinically as chronic pelvic pain, menstrual disorders, dyspareunia, and vulvodynia, whereas in men the internal pudendal vein pathway can produce scrotal varicosities distinct from testicular vein-related varicoceles. These findings establish extra-pelvic varicose veins as a clinically significant consequence of pelvic venous dysfunction with multiple anatomical and pathophysiological underpinnings.

title: Complete bicorporeal uterus, double cervix, longitudinal obstructing vaginal septum: an integrated approach for one-stop diagnosis and ultrasound-guided endoscopic hymen-sparing treatment
summary: OHVIRA syndrome (obstructed hemivagina with ipsilateral renal anomaly), a Müllerian anomaly characterized by complete bicorporeal uterus with double cervix and longitudinal obstructing vaginal septum, typically presents with dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain caused by haematocolpos accumulation following menarche and is frequently associated with ipsilateral renal agenesis. The embryologic basis involves abnormal development of the mesonephric (Wolff's) duct, which causes lateral displacement of the Müllerian duct and prevents proper fusion with the urogenital sinus, resulting in the obstructive vaginal septum. This case demonstrates a novel integrated one-stop diagnostic and minimally invasive treatment approach using ultrasound-guided vaginoscopic resection with a 15Fr bipolar mini-resectoscope combined with hysteroscopy to resect the obstructing septum while preserving hymenal integrity. In a 17-year-old patient presenting with dysmenorrhea and abnormal uterine bleeding, this technique successfully drained the haematocolpos, relieved symptoms completely, and confirmed the dual cervix anatomy without requiring more extensive surgical intervention. Early and accurate diagnosis of congenital Müllerian anomalies is crucial for timely surgical intervention to prevent serious complications including endometriosis, extensive pelvic adhesions, and infertility. The hymen-sparing minimally invasive approach represents a significant advancement for managing complex Müllerian malformations, particularly in sexually inactive patients, while maintaining reproductive potential and avoiding the morbidity associated with hemihysterectomy.

title: Lemierre Syndrome: A Rare Complication of Infectious Mononucleosis
summary: Lemierre syndrome is a rare, life-threatening complication of oropharyngeal infections characterized by septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein. The condition is primarily caused by anaerobic bacteria, particularly Fusobacterium species and Streptococcus intermedius, which damage the pharyngeal mucosa and trigger thrombus formation through septic emboli and inflammatory-induced platelet aggregation. Without treatment, Lemierre syndrome carries a 90% mortality rate, but prompt antibiotic therapy reduces mortality to 5-18%, with some cases requiring extended regimens of culture-sensitive antibiotics for six weeks to ensure adequate penetration. The disease can be preceded by infectious mononucleosis or oropharyngeal trauma, and untreated septic thrombi may metastasize to multiple organs including the lungs, brain, liver, and kidneys, causing complications such as pleural effusions, empyemas, and pneumothoraces. Anticoagulation with agents like heparin or rivaroxaban may be indicated if clinical improvement does not occur within 48-72 hours of antibiotic initiation, though this approach carries potential risks including hematoma formation. Early recognition and aggressive antibiotic treatment remain critical for preventing the devastating consequences of this rare but highly fatal syndrome.

title: Treatment rationale in nutcracker syndrome with concurrent pelvic congestion syndrome
summary:
# Summary

Nutcracker syndrome (NCS) is caused by compression of the left renal vein between the aorta and superior mesenteric artery, resulting in left flank pain, hematuria, and proteinuria. This venous compression can lead to secondary gonadal vein insufficiency and subsequent pelvic congestion syndrome, particularly in women. Diagnostic imaging features include a markedly narrowed renal vein ("beak sign"), reduced aortomesenteric angle (<35–41°), and elevated hilar-to-aortomesenteric diameter ratios, though renocaval pressure gradient measurements may be inconclusive or normal in some patients with extensive collateral compensation. Treatment depends on symptom severity and anatomy: left renal vein stenting directly relieves renal venous hypertension and restores normal blood flow, while left ovarian vein embolization specifically addresses concurrent pelvic congestion and associated pain. The authors present four clinical cases demonstrating that both interventions can be effective and that treatment decisions should be individualized based on angiographic findings and clinical presentation.

title: Clinical Spectrum and Treatment Outcomes of Rare Bleeding Disorders in Female Patients: A Two-Center Experience in North Pakistan
summary:
# Summary

This two-center study examined rare bleeding disorders in female patients from North Pakistan, identifying factor V deficiency as the most prevalent (28%), followed by factor X deficiency (26%). The predominant clinical manifestations included menorrhagia (74%), epistaxis (58%), and skin bruising (52%), with 52% of patients experiencing major bleeding episodes. Factor V and VII deficiencies demonstrated moderate correlation with bleeding severity scores (ISTH BAT), while factor X and XIII showed only weak correlations, suggesting that bleeding severity involves multiple factors beyond simple factor level reduction. Treatment strategies were individualized but primarily involved antifibrinolytics (98%), fresh frozen plasma transfusions (96%), and recombinant factor VII for specific deficiencies, with hormonal management (oral contraceptives, levonorgestrel-releasing IUDs, GnRH agonists) providing additional benefit in 40% of women. Beyond classic bleeding symptoms, rare bleeding disorders were significantly associated with gynecological complications including menorrhagia, hemorrhagic ovarian cysts, endometriosis, endometrial hyperplasia, and increased pregnancy-related risks such as miscarriage and postpartum hemorrhage, highlighting the need for comprehensive reproductive management in affected women.

title: Determination of intraoperative complication rate and risk factors in patients undergoing surgery for tubo-ovarian abscess: A retrospective cohort study
summary: Tubo-ovarian abscess, a severe inflammatory condition arising from untreated pelvic inflammatory disease that involves the fallopian tubes, ovaries, and adjacent pelvic organs, carries significant intraoperative risks including bowel and bladder injuries during surgical treatment. Intraoperative complications occurred in 6% of surgical patients, with elevated serum C-reactive protein (CRP) levels ≥186.5 mg/L and extensive surgical procedures identified as independent predictors of complications, each substantially increasing complication risk. Multiple factors compromise pelvic immunity and predispose to abscess formation, including endometriosis, previous pelvic surgeries, prolonged IUD use, and systemic conditions such as obesity, smoking, diabetes mellitus, and hypertension. Conservative medical management with antibiotics and image-guided drainage is preferred when possible, though surgery remains necessary in emergency situations including abscess rupture or sepsis, with organ-preserving approaches recommended to minimize long-term sequelae such as infertility and hormone deficiencies. Elevated inflammatory markers including leukocytosis and high CRP levels also predict failure of antibiotic therapy, indicating need for surgical intervention and suggesting that severity of inflammation directly correlates with both treatment difficulty and perioperative complications.

title: The clinical efficacy and influencing factors of uterine artery embolization in the treatment of different types of uterine adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

Uterine adenomyosis is a hormone-dependent gynecological disease characterized by proliferation of endometrial glands and interstitium into the myometrium, stimulated by estrogen, resulting in chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and menstrual disorders. The condition exists in two forms—focal adenomyosis with isolated nodules and diffuse adenomyosis spread throughout the myometrial layer—which can be distinguished by MRI imaging and elevated CA125 levels, particularly in focal cases. Uterine artery embolization (UAE) effectively treats adenomyosis by blocking blood supply to lesions, which have poor tolerance to ischemia and hypoxia compared to normal myometrium, causing ischemic necrosis while sparing the basal endometrial layer for vascular revascularization. This minimally invasive technique significantly reduces chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, menstrual volume, and uterine volume within three months, with strong repeatability advantage over conventional hysterectomy or conservative surgical approaches. Alternative hormonal treatments including combined oral contraceptives, progestins, levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, and danazol provide palliative options for patients seeking fertility preservation.

title: Spontaneous coronary and vertebral artery dissection in early pregnancy
summary: This case report describes a rare presentation of spontaneous coronary artery dissection (SCAD) with concurrent vertebral artery dissection occurring in early pregnancy, a notably uncommon event typically observed in the third trimester. Hormonal changes and haemodynamic alterations in pregnancy weaken coronary vessel walls and increase vascular stress, predisposing affected individuals to arterial dissection and myocardial infarction. The patient, with a history of endometriosis and anxiety, presented with right coronary artery dissection requiring coronary artery bypass grafting and management with aspirin, beta-blockers, and angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitors for resulting left ventricular dysfunction. Additionally, the fetus developed jejunal atresias likely secondary to a vascular event in utero, an unusual complication requiring surgical intervention after birth. The case underscores that pregnancy-associated SCAD, though most common in young women during the third trimester and postpartum period, can occur unexpectedly in early pregnancy and carries significant maternal and fetal morbidity despite conservative management being appropriate in most cases. Comprehensive cardiac evaluation and awareness of SCAD risk factors in pregnant women presenting with acute coronary symptoms are essential for timely diagnosis and intervention.

title: Oral traditional Chinese medication for adhesive small bowel obstruction
journal: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
summary: Intra-abdominal adhesions, commonly induced by prior abdominal surgery, represent the leading cause of adhesive small bowel obstruction (SBO) and are associated with high rates of rehospitalization and substantial healthcare costs. Traditional Chinese herbal medicine has been used as a non-operative therapeutic approach to treat adhesive SBO, offering an alternative to surgery that avoids inducing additional adhesions. Evidence from multiple trials indicates that combining traditional Chinese medicine with conventional therapy improves key clinical symptoms, including abdominal pain, abdominal distension, and constipation in patients with adhesive SBO. These findings suggest that integrated treatment approaches incorporating Chinese herbal medicine may provide symptomatic relief and reduce the need for surgical intervention in select adhesive SBO cases. However, the moderate strength of evidence for symptom improvement and the specific mechanisms underlying TCM efficacy warrant further rigorous investigation to establish optimal treatment protocols and patient selection criteria.

title: Hematometra within a cesarean scar defect in a perimenopausal woman: A case report
journal: The Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Research
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents hematometra (blood accumulation in the uterus) developing within a cesarean scar defect in a perimenopausal woman, highlighting a complex interaction between structural and endocrine factors. Cesarean section creates cesarean scar defects that can manifest as postmenstrual spotting, dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and secondary infertility, while age-related cervicovaginal atrophy in perimenopausal women contributes to cervical stenosis that impairs menstrual blood drainage. The accumulated blood within the scar defect was found to contain ectopic endometrial glands and hemorrhagic tissue, suggesting coexisting endometriosis-like pathology, and caused acute abdominal pain requiring clinical intervention. Treatment options ranged from cervical dilation to preserve the uterus to hysterectomy, with progestin-based therapies considered for managing the ectopic endometrium. This case underscores that perimenopausal women with cesarean scar defects face increased risk of hematometra development due to the combined effects of structural defects and age-related anatomical changes, representing a potentially serious complication requiring careful clinical monitoring.

title: An Isolated Hydatid Cyst of the Spleen with High Serum Levels of CA 19-9—A Meaningful Association or Just a Challenge for Diagnosis? A Case Report
summary: This case report describes an unusual diagnostic challenge: a 25-year-old female presented with an isolated splenic hydatid cyst accompanied by markedly elevated serum CA 19-9 levels, a tumor marker typically associated with pancreatic and biliary malignancies. The hydatid cyst caused left upper abdominal pain and was successfully treated with splenectomy followed by postoperative albendazole therapy. Notably, CA 19-9 levels normalized within two months after surgical resection, suggesting that the parasitic cyst itself, rather than an underlying malignancy, was responsible for the elevated marker. The mechanism behind elevated CA 19-9 in hydatid disease remains incompletely understood but may involve Lewis-a antigen-bearing substances produced by the parasite or host epithelial cells within the cyst. This case highlights the diagnostic complexity of elevated CA 19-9 in benign splenic pathologies, including not only hydatid cysts but also epidermoid cysts, ovarian abscesses, endometriosis, and various hepatic diseases, underscoring the importance of comprehensive imaging and clinical correlation to avoid misdiagnosis of malignancy.

title: Early‐Life Exposures and Odds of Adenomyosis: A Population‐Based Case–Control Study
summary: This population-based case-control study identifies critical early-life exposures during the intrauterine period as risk factors for adenomyosis development in adulthood. The research demonstrates that younger maternal age at birth, birth order of fourth or later, and intrauterine cigarette smoke exposure each independently increase adenomyosis odds by approximately 50-80%. These findings suggest that the intrauterine environment represents a developmentally susceptible window wherein adverse exposures may alter uterine tissue remodeling and predispose to adenomyosis pathogenesis later in life. The clinical significance of these associations is underscored by adenomyosis's known capacity to cause debilitating pelvic pain and other life-altering symptoms in affected women. The identification of modifiable and non-modifiable early-life risk factors provides important epidemiological evidence that adenomyosis etiology has origins extending back to fetal development, warranting investigation into the mechanistic pathways linking prenatal exposures to adenomyosis pathophysiology.

title: The present adenomyosis treatment status in Luzhou, China: a small scope observational cross-sectional survey
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional survey from Luzhou, China examined adenomyosis treatment approaches and outcomes, documenting that adenomyosis—characterized by aberrant endometrial glandular tissue within the uterine myometrium—presents with high prevalence of dysmenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility, affecting women of reproductive age. As an estrogen-responsive condition, adenomyosis is managed through multiple treatment modalities including hormone therapies (combined oral contraceptive pills, gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues, levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems, and dienogest) and uterus-sparing surgical options (adenomyectomy and high-intensity focused ultrasound), with hysterectomy reserved as definitive therapy when conservative approaches fail. Both hormone treatment and uterus-sparing surgery achieved comparable symptom relief rates (approximately 75%), though surgery proved significantly more costly and was preferentially used for focal adenomyosis, which showed superior symptom relief with surgery compared to hormonal management. Notably, 87% of patients experienced treatment delays exceeding diagnosis, and delayed treatment intervals greater than 24 months were associated with increased hysterectomy rates and reduced symptom relief, underscoring the clinical importance of early intervention in this chronic condition that substantially impacts quality of life and healthcare costs.

title: Living donor uterus transplant research project in Singapore: Progress of the first case
summary:
# Summary

This report documents Singapore's first living donor uterus transplantation case in a 30-year-old woman with Mayer–Rokitansky–Küster–Hauser syndrome, a condition causing absolute uterine factor infertility through congenital absence of the uterus. Uterus transplantation represents the only medical intervention capable of restoring reproductive anatomy and function in such patients, with over 90 procedures performed globally yielding 49 live births to date. The recipient underwent comprehensive immunosuppressive management using basiliximab, tacrolimus, methylprednisolone, prednisolone, and azathioprine, along with antimicrobial and antiviral prophylaxis tailored to her CY3PA5 polymorphism status and CMV seronegative status. The transplanted uterus achieved menstruation within 38 days postoperatively and demonstrated no rejection on serial cervical biopsies, though the patient experienced significant CMV reactivation requiring treatment with ganciclovir and subsequently valganciclovir. At last report, the recipient maintained stable graft function with normal renal and hepatic indices, four established menstrual cycles, and readiness to proceed with in vitro fertilization using her cryopreserved embryos, representing a major achievement in restoring reproductive potential for patients with absolute uterine factor infertility.

title: Uterus transplantation: A bibliometric review of six‐decade study from 1960 to 2024
summary:
# Summary

This bibliometric review of uterus transplantation literature spanning 1960 to 2024 demonstrates that this procedure has emerged as a viable treatment for absolute uterine factor infertility, enabling pregnancy achievement in previously infertile women—most notably exemplified by the landmark 2015 study by Brännström and colleagues documenting the first livebirth following transplantation. Animal models, particularly rodents and non-human primates, were essential preclinical tools that enabled the translation of uterus transplantation to clinical practice. Research in this field shows marked geographic concentration, with Sweden, the USA, the United Kingdom, and Australia leading investigations, while Africa and low-income countries contribute minimal publications, reflecting a significant global knowledge disparity. Publication activity increased exponentially after 2010, with contemporary research emphasizing follow-up care, complication management, and patient quality of life outcomes, predominantly appearing in high-impact journals such as *Fertility and Sterility* and *The Lancet*. Key contributing institutions including the University of Gothenburg, University of Valencia, and Stockholm IVF have driven collaborative international efforts, particularly between Sweden and Spain, establishing the scientific foundation for this transformative reproductive technology.

title: Scientific landscape and visualization analysis of the link between adenomyosis and infertility from 2000 to 2024
summary: Adenomyosis is a chronic uterine disorder characterized by infiltration of endometrial glands and stroma into the myometrium, significantly impairing reproductive outcomes and quality of life in women of reproductive age. The condition causes infertility through multiple mechanisms, including reduced endometrial receptivity, myometrial fibrosis, junctional zone damage, and altered uterine volume, with adenomyosis patients experiencing 41% reduced live birth rates and substantially decreased clinical pregnancy and ongoing pregnancy rates compared to unaffected women. Beyond reproductive dysfunction, adenomyosis presents with prominent clinical symptoms including abnormal uterine bleeding, dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and psychological comorbidities such as anxiety and depression, while pregnancy-related complications include placenta previa, preterm birth, and preeclampsia. The pathophysiology involves epithelial-mesenchymal transition, chronic inflammation, altered eutopic endometrium function, and gene-associated mechanisms that remain active areas of investigation. Current treatment approaches encompass surgical intervention, hormone therapy (including dienogest and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems), uterine artery embolization, and high-intensity focused ultrasound, with diagnosis primarily relying on ultrasound and MRI imaging. The rising incidence of adenomyosis, particularly in younger women and those with delayed childbearing, combined with over 80% of infertile patients attributed to the disease and >30% experiencing failed assisted reproductive technology, underscores the substantial reproductive and economic burden this condition imposes.

title: Could Transcervical Radiofrequency Ablation Become a Therapy Option for Focal Adenomyosis?
summary: Adenomyosis is a common gynecological disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium, classified into diffuse and focal types, and associated with significant symptoms including heavy bleeding, severe dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and impaired fertility in approximately 70% of patients. Transcervical radiofrequency ablation, particularly using the FDA-approved Sonata System, has emerged as a promising minimally invasive alternative to hysterectomy for treating focal adenomyosis while preserving the uterus. In a series of 30 patients with focal adenomyosis treated with transcervical radiofrequency ablation, 89% reported symptom improvement, with the procedure reducing average focal lesion size from 4.4 cm to 2.8 cm and significantly decreasing both uterine and adenomyosis volumes. The technique offers advantages over traditional excision methods by allowing real-time ultrasound-guided visualization and eliminating the need for serosa puncture through transcervical access. While medical therapies such as levonorgestrel intrauterine devices, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists/antagonists, and combined oral contraceptives remain first-line treatments, radiofrequency ablation represents an effective uterus-sparing surgical option comparable to other hyperthermic approaches like high-intensity focused ultrasound and microwave ablation. This minimally invasive approach provides a patient-centered treatment alternative that addresses adenomyosis symptoms and preserves reproductive potential, potentially improving outcomes in women with focal adenomyotic lesions.

title: Interventional radiology for prevention and management of postpartum haemorrhage: a single centre retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH), the leading direct cause of maternal death, results from multiple etiologies including uterine atony, placental retention, and coagulopathy. While first-line medical management with oxytocin, ergot alkaloids, and prostaglandins is effective, approximately 2% of births require additional interventions. Interventional radiology (IR), including uterine artery embolization and balloon occlusion techniques, demonstrates a 79-95% success rate for refractory PPH and successfully prevents hysterectomy in the majority of cases. In this cohort, both prophylactic IR (primarily for placenta accreta spectrum disorders) and therapeutic IR (for refractory hemorrhage) were effective, with prophylactic approaches particularly beneficial in high-risk pregnancies, though associated with higher neonatal morbidity and NICU admissions. IR carries inherent procedural risks including puncture site hematoma (7.4%), post-embolization fever, and rare severe complications such as uterine necrosis (3.9%) and secondary sepsis, though these remained uncommon compared to the mortality and morbidity associated with PPH itself. These findings support IR as a safe and effective intervention for preventing maternal mortality and morbidity from PPH, particularly in women at highest risk for hemorrhagic complications.

title: Comparison of diagnostic tests for chronic endometritis and endometrial dysbiosis in recurrent implantation failure: Impact on pregnancy outcomes
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis and endometrial dysbiosis are major uterine pathologies associated with recurrent implantation failure, occurring in 30–57% and a substantial proportion of RIF patients, respectively. Endometrial dysbiosis is characterized by reduced Lactobacillus species and directly impairs pregnancy rates, whereas chronic endometritis is typically diagnosed via hysteroscopy or CD138 testing and treated with antibiotics alone—a approach that paradoxically does not improve pregnancy outcomes. Importantly, the three diagnostic modalities (hysteroscopy, CD138 testing, and endometrial microbiome testing) do not correlate with one another, and endometrial microbiome testing proved superior for predicting clinical success. Combined treatment with doxycycline and vaginal Lactobacillus probiotics significantly improved pregnancy outcomes in endometrial dysbiosis patients, with 88.9% achieving clinical pregnancy post-treatment. Multivariate analysis confirmed that endometrial dysbiosis diagnosis and treatment was the only factor independently associated with improved clinical pregnancy, suggesting that targeted microbiome-based assessment and restoration offers superior reproductive outcomes compared to traditional inflammatory markers alone.

title: The application of tissue engineering strategies for uterine regeneration
summary: This article reviews tissue engineering strategies for restoring uterine function and fertility in patients with uterine pathologies. Uterine injuries and endometrial damage cause a cascade of reproductive complications including abnormal menstruation, recurrent miscarriage, and infertility, while conditions such as intrauterine adhesions, leiomyomas, and absolute uterine factor infertility further compromise reproductive capacity. Tissue engineering approaches employing stem cells, biomolecules (growth factors, hormones, cytokines, and chemokines), and porous scaffolds represent emerging therapies that can be used individually or in combination to promote uterine tissue regeneration and restore fertility. Current clinical interventions such as hysteroscopic adhesiolysis significantly improve pregnancy rates and live birth outcomes, with adjunctive therapies like intrauterine devices and hyaluronic acid gels preventing adhesion recurrence and modulating inflammation. The integration of these tissue engineering components facilitates the restoration of endometrial and myometrial architecture, offering promising potential for treating diverse causes of absolute uterine factor infertility alongside conventional surgical and transplantation approaches.

title: LUSSY score predictive of failure of surgical closure of obstetric rectovaginal fistula in the Democratic Republic of the Congo
summary:
# Summary

Rectovaginal fistula, a debilitating condition arising from obstetric trauma, prolonged obstructed labor, inflammatory bowel disease, and other pelvic pathologies, results in uncontrolled passage of feces and gas through the vagina along with dyschezia, dyspareunia, and localized pain that significantly impairs quality of life. This study developed the LUSSY score to predict surgical closure failure by identifying five independent risk factors: moderate to severe fibrosis, fistula size greater than 3 centimeters, combined fistulas, perioperative hemorrhage, and postoperative infection, with postoperative infection conferring the highest risk. Fibrosis—resulting from inflammation and abnormal tissue healing after necrosis—reduces tissue flexibility and vascularization while promoting contracted scar formation, all of which impede proper fistula closure and increase recurrence risk. Successful surgical outcomes require well-vascularized tissue, tension-free closures using techniques such as Martius flap or gracilis muscle interposition, along with skilled surgical expertise, appropriate perioperative care, and long-term follow-up. The study emphasizes that use of intravaginal indigenous products and multiple fistulas further compromise surgical success, highlighting the need for early intervention and expert surgical management to achieve closure success rates above 85% in adequately equipped settings.

title: An evaluation of the relationship between striae gravidarum and intra-abdominal adhesions in caesarean section
journal: Journal of the Turkish German Gynecological Association
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated the relationship between striae gravidarum (pregnancy stretch marks) and intra-abdominal adhesions following cesarean section, identifying skin manifestations as potential predictors of peritoneal complications. The severity of striae gravidarum correlated strongly with the degree of intra-abdominal adhesions, with darker skin types (Fitzpatrick types 5-6) showing significantly higher adhesion grades compared to lighter skin types. Striae gravidarum development is influenced by maternal obesity, gestational weight gain, genetic factors, family history, and vitamin D deficiency, all of which relate to alterations in skin structural proteins including collagen, elastin, and fibrillin. The underlying mechanism appears to involve dysregulation of peritoneal wound healing, where imbalances in fibrin deposition, migration, and collagen degradation lead to excessive adhesion formation after cesarean incision. The study identified abdominal scar characteristics—including scar width, collapsed appearance, and striae color grading—as significant predictors of adhesion severity and subsequent maternal complications including pain, reduced quality of life, and increased morbidity risk. These findings suggest that genetic and phenotypic markers visible on the skin surface may serve as accessible clinical indicators for assessing adhesion risk in women undergoing cesarean delivery.

title: Infertility – a Great Challenge of the Past, Present, and Future
summary:
# Summary

Infertility represents a multifactorial disease involving both female and male reproductive pathways with distinct etiologies and treatment approaches. In females, polycystic ovary syndrome causes ovulation disorders and is associated with obesity and smoking, while obesity independently drives infertility through insulin resistance, oxidative stress, and altered adipokine levels (reduced adiponectin and elevated leptin). Endometriosis, a common finding in infertile women, promotes inflammation via macrophage infiltration and causes both ovulation and implantation disorders through elevated cytokines and prostaglandins, with genetic factors including estrogen and progesterone receptor polymorphisms contributing to disease susceptibility. Uterine pathologies including myomas, adenomyosis, endometrial polyps, and Asherman syndrome significantly impact fertility through impaired implantation and increased miscarriage risk. Male infertility involves testicular disorders (cryptorchism, varicocele), inflammatory conditions affecting semen quality, genetic mutations (Y chromosome microdeletions, CFTR mutations, Klinefelter syndrome), and infectious agents such as human papillomavirus that compromise sperm function. Treatment options span pharmacological interventions (letrozole, clomiphene citrate, gonadotropins), surgical approaches (hysteroscopy, laparoscopy), and assisted reproductive technologies (intrauterine insemination, in vitro fertilization, intracytoplasmic sperm injection), with lifestyle modifications such as obesity treatment serving as initial management strategies.

title: Laparoscopic Excision of Douglas Pouch Fibroma in Contact With the Sigmoid Colon
summary:
# Laparoscopic Excision of Douglas Pouch Fibroid

This case report describes the successful laparoscopic treatment of a retained fibroid in the rectouterine (Douglas) pouch that developed after a previous myomectomy, demonstrating a rare but clinically significant complication of laparoscopic fibroid surgery. The residual fibroid, measuring 4.5-6 cm with a multinodular appearance, was located in close contact with the sigmoid colon, presenting particular surgical challenges due to its deep pelvic location and proximity to bowel tissue. While myomectomy remains the definitive treatment for uterine fibroids and laparoscopic morcellation is widely used, the procedure carries a documented risk of generating parasitic myomas when tissue fragments implant in the peritoneal cavity, with an incidence of 0.12-0.95%. The authors highlight that retained myomas pose unique management challenges, particularly during pregnancy when the mass requires careful monitoring and cesarean delivery may be elected to avoid intraoperative complications. Gynecological laparoscopy proved effective in this complex case, with the fibroid successfully excised using bipolar cautery and laparoscopic scissors while avoiding injury to adjacent sigmoid tissue. This case emphasizes the importance of considering retained fibroid complications when counseling patients undergoing laparoscopic myomectomy and demonstrates that minimally invasive techniques remain viable for addressing difficult pelvic masses even when located near vital structures.

title: Radiological Insights Into Uterine Arteriovenous Malformation Causing Profuse Vaginal Bleeding: A Rare Case
summary: Uterine arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) are rare vascular anomalies characterized by abnormal shunting between arteries and veins that primarily affect premenopausal women and present with profuse vaginal bleeding and lower abdominal pain. These malformations can be congenital, resulting from embryological differentiation failure, or more commonly acquired following uterine trauma, curettage, miscarriage treatment, medical termination of pregnancy, or cesarean section, with additional risk factors including infection, inflammation, gestational trophoblastic disease, and endometrial malignancies. Diagnosis relies on multimodal imaging including ultrasound, which reveals characteristic hypoechoic serpiginous spaces within the myometrium, MRI for determining size and extent, and digital subtraction pelvic angiography as the gold standard that enables simultaneous therapeutic intervention. Management approaches range from conservative strategies using uterotonic agents (methylergonovine maleate, tranexamic acid), oral contraceptives, and GnRH agonists for hemodynamically stable patients, to interventional procedures including uterine artery embolization with agents such as polyvinyl alcohol, and hysterectomy reserved for recurrent profuse bleeding refractory to other treatments. A case of a 26-year-old woman with four months of profuse vaginal bleeding following prior cesarean sections demonstrated successful conservative management with symptom resolution on follow-up, illustrating the importance of radiological recognition and appropriate management strategy selection based on clinical severity and fertility preservation needs.

title: Pelvic Congestion Syndrome – Findings on Multi‐Detector Row Computerized Tomography: A Case Report
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: Pelvic congestion syndrome represents a vascular disorder that causes chronic pelvic pain, though it is frequently overlooked in clinical practice. Multi-detector row computerized tomography has emerged as an essential diagnostic tool for identifying this condition, offering imaging capabilities that surpass traditional clinical assessment methods. This case report demonstrates the importance of advanced imaging techniques in recognizing pelvic congestion syndrome, which would otherwise remain undiagnosed despite patients presenting with persistent pelvic pain symptoms. The findings underscore how radiological imaging can bridge diagnostic gaps in cases where clinical evaluation alone proves insufficient for identifying underlying vascular pathology. Recognition of pelvic congestion syndrome through appropriate imaging modalities is critical for directing patients toward effective treatment and symptom management.

title: Acute urinary retention in an adolescent female with Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome
summary: Herlyn–Werner–Wunderlich syndrome is a rare congenital Müllerian anomaly resulting from abnormal mesonephric and paramesonephric development, characterized by the diagnostic triad of uterus didelphys, unilateral obstructed hemivagina, and ipsilateral renal agenesis. The obstructed hemivagina leads to hematocolpos accumulation, which typically manifests after menarche with dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and vaginal bleeding, though uncommon presentations such as acute urinary retention can occur due to mass effect on surrounding structures. Delayed diagnosis carries significant morbidity risk, with untreated cases developing endometriosis in approximately 14% of patients and facing infertility complications. Prompt imaging diagnosis via ultrasound and MRI is essential for identifying the characteristic anatomical abnormalities and guiding surgical management. Surgical intervention involving vaginal septectomy and hematocolpos drainage effectively relieves obstruction and prevents long-term complications including endometriosis, adhesions, and infertility. Long-term follow-up monitoring is critical to assess for endometriosis development and renal-related complications in these patients.

title: Ultrasound and single‐port laparoscopic‐guided microwave ablation of abdominal wall endometriosis lesions: A single‐center observational study
summary: Abdominal wall endometriosis (AWE), a rare condition with an incidence of 0.03%–5%, typically develops following surgical procedures such as cesarean section, hysterectomy, salpingostomy, or amniocentesis, and manifests as cyclic abdominal pain and palpable masses due to estrogen and progesterone dependence. This study demonstrates that ultrasound and single-port laparoscopy-guided microwave ablation effectively treats AWE by inducing tissue coagulation and necrosis, resulting in significant reductions in lesion volume and pain scores at one year postoperatively. While medical therapies including oral contraceptives, progestogens, danazol, and aromatase inhibitors provide only temporary symptom alleviation, surgical approaches including microwave ablation, excision, and high-intensity focused ultrasound offer more durable treatment. The combination of ultrasound guidance with single-port laparoscopy provides enhanced protection of surrounding tissues, with superficial lesions treatable via percutaneous ultrasound-guided ablation and deeper lesions requiring laparoscopic visualization. Notably, the estrogen-dependent nature of AWE carries a risk of malignant transformation in approximately 1% of endometriosis cases, emphasizing the clinical importance of definitive treatment approaches.

title: Great Mimics in Oncology: A Retrospective Study from a Tertiary Care Centre of Eastern India
summary: This retrospective study examines diagnostic mimicry in oncology, where benign diseases present clinical, radiologic, and cytological features indistinguishable from malignancy, and conversely, benign-appearing tumors prove malignant upon histopathological examination. The study documents multiple cases of misdiagnosis, including benign conditions such as tuberculosis, Castleman disease, and IgG4-related disease initially suspected as malignancy, as well as cases of ovarian carcinoma, primary peritoneal carcinoma, and gallbladder carcinoma initially thought to be benign. These diagnostic errors resulted in significant clinical consequences, including unnecessary perioperative morbidity from inappropriate surgical intervention and inadvertent administration of intraoperative brachytherapy. The research emphasizes that inconclusive cytology reports and reliance solely on clinical and radiologic findings without histopathological confirmation contributed to misdiagnosis in several cases. These findings underscore the critical importance of obtaining definitive histopathological diagnosis before committing patients to cancer-directed therapy, as clinical, radiologic, and cytological findings alone can be misleading in distinguishing benign from malignant pathology.

title: Visualized hysteroscopic artificial intelligence fertility assessment system for endometrial injury: an image-deep-learning study
summary:
# Summary

Asherman's syndrome, characterized by intrauterine adhesions resulting from procedures such as dilation and curettage, uterine fibroid removal, or endometrial damage, represents a major cause of subfertility and infertility by preventing blastocyst implantation through endometrial injury. This study developed a proportional hazard convolutional neural network that integrates the DeepSurv algorithm to predict fertility outcomes and natural conception within one year following hysteroscopic adhesiolysis treatment, demonstrating superior performance (AUC 0.982-0.992) compared to traditional machine learning approaches like InceptionV3. The deep learning system identifies key morphological and vascular features—including uterine cavity morphology, Fallopian tube ostia patency, endometrial thickness, and blood supply—that associate with subfertility or successful conception, with performance comparable to senior hysteroscopists (kappa 0.84-0.89). Grad-CAM visualization provides transparent, quantifiable assessment of intrauterine pathologies, assisting clinicians in optimizing treatment strategies and counseling patients on fertility prognosis. This artificial intelligence-enhanced hysteroscopic assessment system advances beyond single-timepoint diagnosis to enable dynamic prediction of reproductive outcomes over time, improving the standard-of-care evaluation of endometrial receptivity and postoperative fertility potential in patients with Asherman's syndrome.

title: Analysis of risk factors for recurrence after laparoscopic myomectomy: A retrospective study
summary: This retrospective study identifies multiple independent risk factors for fibroid recurrence following laparoscopic myomectomy. Multivariate analysis revealed that elevated body mass index (>24), increased parity (>2 pregnancies), multiple fibroids, intermyowall fibroid location, and endometrial hyperplasia were significantly associated with postoperative recurrence. The elevated recurrence risk in these patient populations appears mechanistically linked to obesity-induced leptin signaling, prolonged inflammatory states from multiple pregnancies, and elevated estrogen levels that promote smooth muscle cell proliferation in the myometrium. Notably, factors such as fibroid diameter, postoperative pregnancy, diabetes, and endometriosis were not significantly associated with recurrence, suggesting that patient metabolic status and uterine endometrial condition are more important prognostic indicators than surgical or clinical factors alone. These findings suggest that preoperative identification of obese patients with high parity and endometrial pathology may help stratify recurrence risk and guide postoperative management decisions.

title: Teen Pregnancy
summary: I cannot provide a summary of this article's findings because no relationship triples were extracted from the text. Without structured data on the biological mechanisms, clinical outcomes, entities involved, or causal relationships discussed in the article, I am unable to synthesize the key findings into a coherent summary. To proceed, please provide the extracted relationship triples from the article, or verify that the article contains relevant scientific relationships suitable for analysis.

title: Selective Embolization in the Treatment of Pelvic Congestion Syndrome: A Comprehensive Case Report and Review of the Literature
summary: Pelvic congestion syndrome is a vascular disorder affecting women of childbearing age, characterized by chronic pelvic pain lasting over six months and caused by dilatation and dysfunction of pelvic veins secondary to venous valve insufficiency or anatomical compression syndromes such as left renal vein compression or left iliac vein compression. The condition presents with multiple symptoms including dyspareunia, dysmenorrhea, vulvar varicose veins, and bladder irritability, with higher prevalence in multiparous women and those with obesity or polycystic ovary syndrome. Phlebography with three-dimensional reconstruction can definitively confirm ovarian vein reflux, the hallmark pathophysiologic finding. Transcatheter embolization of the ovarian veins has emerged as the first-line minimally invasive treatment, achieving over 85% symptom relief and resulting in marked improvement or resolution of pelvic pain, dyspareunia, vulvar varicosities, and lower limb heaviness. This case report demonstrates the effectiveness of selective embolization in providing both immediate symptom resolution and sustained improvement in quality of life for pelvic congestion syndrome patients.

title: Comparing the accuracy of Pipelle versus hysteroscopy and curettage in the diagnosis of chronic endometritis in women with recurrent implantation failure: A prospective cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis (CE), characterized by endometrial inflammation with elevated proinflammatory cytokines including IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α, is significantly associated with recurrent implantation failure, recurrent pregnancy loss, and chronic pelvic pain in women undergoing fertility treatment. CE impairs endometrial receptivity by narrowing the implantation window, and antibiotic treatment combined with anti-inflammatory drugs substantially improves implantation rates, live birth rates, and clinically confirmed pregnancy rates. This study compared diagnostic approaches, finding that Pipelle biopsy achieved 100% diagnostic accuracy for CE detection by identifying characteristic plasma cell infiltrates, while hysteroscopy with curettage demonstrated 92.3% sensitivity but carries higher risks of uterine perforation, bleeding, and infection.
