The research identified multiple pathogenic organisms in RIF patients' uterine samples, including E. coli, Streptococci, Staphylococci, and E. faecalis, supporting the role of uterine microbiome disturbances in implantation failure. Additional therapeutic interventions such as granulocyte colony-stimulating factor and platelet-rich plasma infusions enhance implantation and pregnancy outcomes by improving endometrial thickness and receptivity in RIF patients.

title: An unsuspected case of uterine arteriovenous malformation with nidus aneurysm following vaginal delivery: Diagnostic challenges and management
summary:
# Summary

Uterine arteriovenous malformations (AVMs) are rare but potentially life-threatening vascular anomalies characterized by abnormal direct communication between arteries and veins that can cause severe postpartum hemorrhage. These lesions arise either congenitally through embryonic vascular maldevelopment or acquire secondarily following uterine tissue trauma from spontaneous miscarriage, dilation and curettage, Cesarean section, or endometriosis, with congenital lesions potentially enlarging during pregnancy. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound with color Doppler showing characteristic findings such as hypoechoic tubular structures, color mosaics with thickened vessels, low-resistance patterns, and elevated peak systolic velocities (PSV >60 cm/s indicating severity), alongside computed tomography angiography demonstrating early venous contrast filling. Critical diagnostic distinction from other postpartum hemorrhage causes like retained products of conception or gestational trophoblastic disease can be achieved through negative beta hCG levels in AVM cases. Transcatheter arterial embolization using polyvinyl alcohol particles and gelatin sponge represents the preferred treatment, effectively controlling bleeding while preserving menstrual function and future pregnancy potential, in stark contrast to curettage which can catastrophically worsen the condition.

title: Expert Consensus on the Management of Adenomyosis: A Modified Delphi Method Approach by the Taiwan Endometriosis Society
summary:
# Summary

This expert consensus document from the Taiwan Endometriosis Society establishes clinical management guidelines for adenomyosis, a condition strongly associated with obstetric complications including miscarriage and reduced reproductive success. Adenomyosis causes dysmenorrhea and is related to endometriosis, with transvaginal ultrasound serving as the primary diagnostic imaging modality. Multiple hormonal therapies—including combined oral contraceptives, oral progestins, and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists—effectively reduce adenomyosis symptoms, while both laparoscopic and laparotomic surgical approaches demonstrate equivalent pain relief outcomes. For patients pursuing assisted reproduction, surgical removal of adenomyosis lesions and preemptive GnRH agonist use before embryo transfer improve in vitro fertilization success rates, though uterine-sparing surgery carries a notable risk of uterine rupture that requires careful consideration during treatment planning.

title: Vesicouterine Cutaneous Fistula Post-cesarean Section — A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

Vesicouterine cutaneous fistula is a rare but serious postoperative complication that can develop after cesarean section, particularly when severe adhesions necessitate difficult surgical dissection or when occult bladder injuries occur due to poor surgical technique. This case report describes a 41-year-old woman who presented with pus discharge, cyclical hematuria, and fluid leakage from her cesarean wound, manifestations consistent with Youssef syndrome—a condition characterized by vesicouterine fistula without urinary incontinence but with cyclic voiding of blood-stained urine. Diagnostic imaging including computed tomography with contrast, cystoscopy, and the methylene blue test confirmed the fistula with bladder involvement. Conservative management with gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists and catheterization proved inadequate and complicated by thromboembolism, necessitating surgical intervention consisting of fistula tract resection, two-layer bladder repair, and hysterectomy, which successfully resolved symptoms. The case underscores the importance of meticulous surgical technique during cesarean delivery and demonstrates that minimally invasive laparoscopic approaches to complex obstetric fistulas offer superior outcomes with reduced recovery times compared to traditional open surgery.

title: Nongravid incarcerated uterus with leiomyomas treated with uterine artery embolization and hysterectomy: a case report
summary:
# Summary

Nongravid uterine incarceration is a rare condition most commonly caused by large uterine leiomyomas, often in combination with uterine retroversion, endometriosis, or pelvic adhesions. This case presents a 50-year-old woman with incarcerated leiomyomas who developed severe abdominal pain, urinary retention, and bladder outlet obstruction from urethral stretching and bladder neck compression caused by the retroflexed uterus. The patient was successfully managed with uterine artery embolization using polyvinyl alcohol or tris-acyl gelatin particles, which reduced leiomyoma size by 50-60%, relieved pain and urinary symptoms, and facilitated subsequent hysterectomy. Uterine artery embolization offers a minimally invasive alternative to immediate surgical intervention in nongravid uterine incarceration, effectively reducing symptom burden and uterine volume before definitive treatment. The case demonstrates that preoperative embolization may serve as a useful adjunct to hysterectomy by decreasing operative morbidity and improving patient outcomes in this uncommon but clinically significant condition.

title: Secondary Hemorrhage After Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: An Observational Study
summary: This observational study identified risk factors for secondary hemorrhage following total laparoscopic hysterectomy, defined as vaginal bleeding occurring more than 24 hours postoperatively. Elevated body mass index and cervical pathology, particularly cervicitis, emerged as significant independent predictors of secondary hemorrhage, while adenomyotic changes and uterine fibroids were also more prevalent in hemorrhagic patients. Increased endometrial thickness and anteroposterior diameter of the uterus were noted in bleeding patients, though these differences did not reach statistical significance. Conservative management with tranexamic acid and prophylactic oral cephalosporins successfully treated most cases, with one patient requiring additional silver nitrate cauterization for unhealthy granulation tissue. The findings suggest that preoperative assessment of BMI and careful attention to cervical inflammation may help identify high-risk patients, enabling appropriate preventive strategies and closer postoperative monitoring to minimize hemorrhagic complications.

title: Urologic Fistulas in Czech Women With Gynaecologic Malignancies
summary:
# Summary

This study examines urologic fistulas in Czech women treated for gynecologic malignancies, identifying both surgical and radiation-related mechanisms of fistula formation. Cervical cancer was the most common underlying malignancy (76.8% of cases), with over 70% of fistulas developing following pelvic radiotherapy rather than surgery. Radiation induces fistula formation through endarteritis, which triggers a cascade of vascular occlusion, ischemia, necrosis, and tissue fibrosis that progressively damages bladder and ureteric compliance. Surgical injury mechanisms include unrecognized cystotomy, poor repair, devascularization, and thermal injury during hysterectomy and radical gynecologic procedures. Radiation-induced fistulas present as complex, large, and multiple lesions with poor spontaneous healing and low surgical success rates (14.3%), whereas simple surgical fistulas respond well to fistuloraphy (83.3% success). The delayed presentation of post-radiation fistulas (months to years) contrasts sharply with early post-operative fistulas, reflecting the underlying pathophysiology. These complications significantly impair quality of life, delay adjuvant cancer treatment, and increase secondary surgery and hospital readmission rates, particularly when complex or radiation-induced.

title: Improving Precision and Safety in Uterine Isthmocele Repair by Utilizing the KOH Cup and Firefly Guidance
summary:
# Summary

Isthmocele, a uterine defect occurring in 20-70% of cesarean deliveries due to defective wound closure and inadequate healing, significantly impairs reproductive function through multiple mechanisms including reduced endometrial receptivity, compromised blood supply, retained blood accumulation, and disruption of the uterine microbiota. Both patient factors (genetic predisposition, endometriosis, hypertension, gestational diabetes, obesity, smoking) and delivery-related factors (incision type, suturing technique, pelvic adhesions) contribute to isthmocele formation, which reduces live birth rates and IVF success while increasing risk of secondary infertility and ectopic pregnancy. Diagnosis relies on transvaginal ultrasound and MRI for assessing residual myometrial thickness and lesion mapping, while treatment options range from medical management with oral contraceptives and progesterone-releasing IUDs to surgical repair via hysteroscopic, laparoscopic, vaginal, or robotic-assisted approaches. The combination of KOH Cup technology and Firefly near-infrared fluorescence guidance significantly enhances surgical precision during robotic-assisted repair by improving real-time tissue visualization, preventing inadvertent bladder injury, and enabling accurate defect localization and demarcation of anatomical boundaries. This advanced imaging-guided approach improves residual myometrial thickness and scar integrity while reducing complication rates, thereby improving reproductive outcomes and enabling spontaneous conception following isthmocele correction.

title: Pseudo-Meigs syndrome: a rare cause of abdominal ascites in a patient with an ovarian mass
summary: Meigs syndrome is a rare clinical phenomenon occurring in approximately 1% of patients with ovarian tumors, characterized by a triad of benign ovarian tumors, pleural effusion, and ascites that completely resolve following surgical intervention. This case report describes a young woman who presented with new-onset ascites initially suspected to result from cirrhotic portal hypertension but was ultimately found to have a benign ovarian teratoma as the underlying cause. The association between benign ovarian neoplasms, particularly teratomas, and ascites formation suggests a paraneoplastic mechanism distinct from other causes of abdominal fluid accumulation. Meigs syndrome represents an important differential diagnosis in patients presenting with concurrent ascites and ovarian masses, as recognition of this condition can prevent unnecessary investigations and direct treatment toward the underlying tumor. Surgical removal of the benign ovarian tumor results in complete resolution of both ascites and pleural effusion, confirming the pathophysiological link between the neoplasm and fluid accumulation. This case highlights the clinical significance of considering Meigs syndrome in the differential diagnosis of patients with ovarian masses and unexplained ascites or pleural effusions.

title: Analyzing 10-year time trends for Hysterectomy and Oophorectomy: Focus on Endometrial sampling and risk factors for Endometrial Cancer
summary:
# Summary

This 10-year analysis of hysterectomy and oophorectomy trends examines the surgical management of benign gynecological conditions and associated endometrial cancer risks. Hysterectomy remains the primary definitive treatment for uterine leiomyomas, adenomyosis, chronic pelvic pain, and dysfunctional uterine bleeding, though conservative alternatives including uterine artery embolization, myomectomy, intrauterine devices, and endometrial ablation have become increasingly available. Multiple significant risk factors for endometrial cancer were identified, including obesity, polycystic ovary syndrome, family history, and estrogen therapy, with atypical endometrial hyperplasia conferring particularly high malignancy risk, with up to 30% of cases progressing to cancer within 12 months. Pipelle endometrial sampling and dilation and curettage were the primary diagnostic methods employed to identify premalignant lesions and exclude concurrent endometrial carcinoma. The analysis also highlights long-term adverse outcomes associated with hysterectomy, including increased risks of cardiovascular disease, fractures, and pelvic floor dysfunction, while oophorectomy has been associated with elevated dementia risk. Laparoscopic hysterectomy approaches offer advantages over traditional abdominal techniques, including faster recovery and reduced complication rates, suggesting a shift toward minimally invasive surgical management in contemporary practice.

title: Overcoming challenges in ovarian visualization: a case report of a magnetic resonance imaging–guided oocyte retrieval resulting in live birth
summary: A 33-year-old woman with ulcerative colitis, prior colectomy, and extensive abdominal surgeries developed severe pelvic adhesive disease with nonvisualized ovaries and diminished ovarian reserve (AMH 0.77 ng/mL), making conventional oocyte retrieval infeasible. MRI provided superior soft tissue visualization of the complex pelvic anatomy, revealing extensive adhesions and a peritoneal inclusion cyst that obscured ovarian access. The team employed MRI-guided transperineal oocyte retrieval—a novel technique avoiding invasive laparoscopy—combined with controlled ovarian stimulation using gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonist protocol and high-dose recombinant follicle-stimulating hormone to retrieve a mature oocyte. The retrieved oocyte was successfully fertilized via intracytoplasmic sperm injection, developed into a blastocyst, and was transferred to a gestational carrier with endometrial preparation using estradiol and progesterone supplementation, resulting in live birth. This case represents the first documented successful MRI-guided oocyte retrieval leading to live birth, establishing a potentially transformative less-invasive alternative for infertile patients with challenging pelvic anatomy due to adhesive disease or inflammatory bowel disease.

title: Enterocervical Fistula Six Weeks After Laparoscopic Mesh Sacral Colposuspension: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare but serious complication of laparoscopic mesh sacral colposuspension, a procedure commonly used to treat apical pelvic organ prolapse and stress urinary incontinence. Six weeks after the procedure, the patient developed an enterocervical fistula resulting from small bowel adhesion to the polypropylene mesh, presenting with vaginal discharge and necrotic cervical tissue. The underlying mechanism involved lack of peritoneal re-coverage over the mesh, which allowed bowel loops to adhere directly to the synthetic material and erode through to the cervix. Treatment required multi-disciplinary intervention including cervical debridement, partial mesh excision, lysis of adhesions, and small bowel resection with primary anastomosis, followed by broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy. While mesh colposuspension has long-term success rates of 68.9-88% with generally favorable outcomes, this case highlights that bowel fistula formation—though exceedingly rare following pelvic surgery—remains a potentially devastating complication that may be prevented through adequate peritoneal re-peritonealization at the time of mesh placement.

title: Comparison of tracer application methods for sentinel lymph node detection in open surgery patients with endometrial cancer: a retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study evaluates sentinel lymph node (SLN) detection methods in endometrial cancer patients undergoing open surgery, identifying critical factors affecting detection success. Combined intracervical and intrafundal tracer application using radiolabeled technetium-99m and methylene blue dye achieved the highest bilateral SLN detection rates (42.6%), with intrafundal injection proving particularly advantageous. Advanced age exceeding 66.3 years emerged as the strongest predictor of bilateral SLN detection failure, alongside additional risk factors including elevated BMI over 30.7, prior pelvic surgery, deep myometrial invasion exceeding 19%, and large tumor volume over 153,822 mm³. SLNs were predominantly located at the external iliac vein and supraobturator regions, with successful detection more frequent on the right side (58.9%). The findings support SLN biopsy as an effective staging modality for endometrial cancer, guiding treatment decisions through lymph node status assessment in conjunction with total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy.

title: Risk factors for uterine adenomyosis diagnosed by MRI in women of reproductive age
summary:
# Summary

This MRI-based study identifies significant risk factors for uterine adenomyosis in women of reproductive age. The presence of endometriosis and a history of uterine surgery emerged as the strongest independent predictors of adenomyosis (P < 0.05), while increased parity also showed significant association. Pathophysiologically, adenomyosis arises from aberrant invasion of basal endometrial layer into the myometrium, whereby disruption from uterine procedures allows ectopic endometrial glands and stroma to invade and proliferate under estrogen stimulation, causing cyclic bleeding and myometrial hypertrophy. Adenomyosis causes substantial morbidity including dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, infertility, and miscarriage, and complicates pregnancy with increased risks of preeclampsia, placental abnormalities, and postpartum hemorrhage. The study demonstrates that hysterectomy remains the definitive treatment, while minimizing unnecessary uterine procedures and timely management of comorbid endometriosis may reduce adenomyosis development and progression.

title: Acute Tubular Injury and Fanconi Syndrome Associated With Red Yeast Rice Supplement
summary:
# Summary

Red yeast rice supplements contaminated with puberulic acid caused acute kidney injury and Fanconi syndrome in affected patients by triggering mitochondrial dysfunction in renal tubular epithelial cells. The contaminated supplements induced a pathological cascade wherein mitochondrial impairment prompted cell death through multiple mechanisms (necrosis, apoptosis, ferroptosis), leading to acute tubular injury and widespread glycogen accumulation in tubular epithelium—a pattern mimicking the rare genetic disorder Fanconi-Bickel syndrome. The glycogen accumulation resulted from reduced expression of glucose transporter 2 in proximal tubular epithelium, disrupting glucose homeostasis. If damaged tubules were not adequately repaired, fibrosis developed, progressing to chronic kidney disease. One affected patient had a concurrent medical history of endometriosis, though the primary renal pathology was driven by supplement-induced mitochondrial toxicity rather than the underlying gynecological condition.

title: Rhabdomyolysis and Acute Kidney Injury: Exploring the Potential Causes in a Hospitalized Patient
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 30-year-old female with endometriosis and adenomyosis who presented with rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury (AKI) triggered by agitation, increased motor activity, and venlafaxine use. Rhabdomyolysis—the breakdown of skeletal muscle—releases intracellular contents including creatine kinase and myoglobin into the bloodstream, causing cellular damage through increased intracellular calcium, protease activation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and reactive oxygen species production. Myoglobin deposited in the kidneys induces AKI through renal vasoconstriction, tubular obstruction, and lipid peroxidation, with hypovolemia, dehydration, and acidic urine exacerbating renal failure; the patient developed acute tubular necrosis and became anuric despite diuretic therapy. Treatment centered on early aggressive intravenous crystalloid hydration, urine alkalinization with bicarbonate infusion, and eventual hemodialysis once acute renal failure developed. This case illustrates how psychiatric medications, behavioral agitation, and underlying gynecologic conditions can converge to cause severe rhabdomyolysis with life-threatening renal complications requiring intensive intervention.

title: Ultrasound characteristics and risk factors of female patients with pelvic varicose veins and concomitant chronic pelvic pain
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery: Venous and Lymphatic Disorders
summary: This study identifies pelvic vascular insufficiency as a major mechanism underlying chronic pelvic pain in women, with left ovarian vein reflux and incompetence emerging as the strongest independent risk factors (odds ratio 9.1). Ultrasound imaging reveals that patients with chronic pelvic pain display significantly enlarged left ovarian veins, markedly higher rates of left ovarian vein incompetence (94.4% versus 23.1%), and increased prevalence of left internal iliac vein incompetence compared to controls. Beyond vascular pathology, elevated menstrual bleeding and lower body mass index are associated with increased pain risk, while the anatomic compression phenomena previously implicated in pelvic pain do not independently contribute to disease. Treatment approaches focus on correcting venous insufficiency through transcatheter embolization of incompetent ovarian veins, which effectively eliminates venous stasis and alleviates symptoms, complemented by medical options including vasoactive drugs and hormonal therapy. The findings suggest that approximately one-third of women with chronic pelvic pain have a vascular mechanism as the underlying etiology, positioning ovarian vein reflux correction as a pivotal therapeutic target.

title: Fertility-Sparing Surgery for Non-Epithelial Ovarian Malignancies: Ten-Year Retrospective Study of Oncological and Reproductive Outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This ten-year retrospective study evaluates fertility-sparing surgery combined with platinum-based and BEP chemotherapy for treating non-epithelial ovarian cancers (NEOCs), which encompass rare malignancies including malignant germ cell tumors, sex cord-stromal tumors, and ovarian sarcomas. Granulosa cell tumors were the most common subtype (53.8%), followed by dysgerminomas (20.5%) and immature teratomas (12.8%), with several serum biomarkers—including CA 125, beta-HCG, alpha-fetoprotein, inhibin B, and anti-Mullerian hormone—associated with disease detection. Advanced tumor stage emerged as the most significant predictor of disease recurrence, which occurred in 25.6% of patients and substantially reduced overall survival; however, chemotherapy treatment of recurrence improved survival outcomes. The study demonstrated successful reproductive outcomes following fertility-sparing approaches, with 70% of patients attempting conception achieving pregnancy, and term delivery correlated with second-step surgery and absence of positive lymph nodes or peritoneal involvement. Overall survival reached 87.2% with recurrence-free survival at 76.92%, confirming that early-stage diagnosis and avoidance of disease recurrence are critical prognostic factors for both oncological control and fertility preservation in NEOC patients.

title: Intrauterine Adhesions and Asherman Syndrome: A Retrospective Dive into Predictive Risk Factors, Diagnosis, and Surgical Perspectives
summary:
# Intrauterine Adhesions and Asherman Syndrome: Key Findings Summary

Intrauterine adhesions (IUAs) are scar tissue formations within the uterus that significantly impair reproductive function, causing infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, dysmenorrhea, and amenorrhea through mechanisms of implantation failure and endometrial damage. Asherman syndrome, the severe form of IUAs, is most commonly caused by termination of pregnancy and is more strongly associated with amenorrhea and second-trimester miscarriages than primary IUAs. Multiple iatrogenic procedures create substantial adhesion risk, including dilation and curettage (22% prevalence following postpartum curettage), hysteroscopic surgeries, caesarean section, and uterine artery embolization, with negative pressure during uterine aspiration identified as a critical risk factor. Pelvic inflammatory disease, chronic endometritis, septate uterus, and myomectomy represent additional significant risk factors for adhesion formation. Diagnosis relies on hysteroscopy as the gold standard, supplemented by transvaginal ultrasound, hysterosalpingography, and magnetic resonance imaging, with severity stratified by American Fertility Society scoring that correlates with infertility type and prognosis. The underlying pathophysiology involves endometrial injury, infection, and disrupted healing processes that ultimately lead to fibrosis and loss of normal endometrial function.

title: Management of Extra-Pelvic Varicose Veins of Pelvic Origin in Female Patients
summary:
# Summary

Pelvic venous insufficiency (PVI), caused by ovarian vein incompetence, internal iliac vein dysfunction, or secondary lesions such as May-Thurner syndrome and endometriotic compression, leads to pelvic venous hypertension and the development of extra-pelvic varicose veins through incompetent "pelvic escape points," most commonly the pudendal points found in 60-70% of affected females. PVI manifests clinically with chronic pelvic pain and menstrual disorders including menorrhagia and menometrorrhagia, often accompanied by vulvar varicosities associated with dyspareunia and vulvodynia. Hormonal factors during pregnancy, particularly elevated estrogen, progesterone, and relaxin, weaken venous walls and contribute to disease development, while pelvic venous reflux can secondarily cause saphenous vein incompetence and atypical lower extremity varicosities. Diagnosis relies on transvaginal ultrasonography to exclude endometriosis and adenomyosis, combined with Doppler ultrasound assessment of venous reflux patterns and escape point identification. Management employs a "bottom-up" approach using minimally invasive procedures such as ultrasound-guided foam sclerotherapy, surgical ligation, and miniphlebectomy for extra-pelvic varicosities, with endovascular techniques including ovarian vein embolization and iliac vein stenting reserved for cases of treatment failure or persistent pelvic symptoms.

title: A Qualitative Study of Fertility Preservation Experience in Women with Breast Cancer
summary: Breast cancer and its treatments, including chemotherapy and radiotherapy, significantly impair fertility through gonadal dysfunction, creating a critical clinical challenge for women of reproductive age. Fertility preservation techniques such as oocyte and embryo cryopreservation effectively treat fertility impairment, enabling women to maintain reproductive options despite cancer diagnosis. However, the fertility preservation process itself imposes substantial physical and emotional burdens, including ovulation induction-related symptoms (abdominal bloating, nausea, stomach cramps, and breast swelling), transvaginal ultrasound discomfort, and endocrine hormone-induced emotional distress. A critical tension exists between fertility preservation and cancer control: delays in fertility preservation treatments risk cancer progression, while hormone elevation during fertility preservation raises concerns about breast cancer recurrence, particularly in hormone-sensitive cases. Despite these challenges, fertility preservation treatment offers significant psychological benefits, providing women with hope for future motherhood and a sense of life continuity, with therapy improving overall psychological adjustment even amid the substantial physical and emotional demands of concurrent cancer and fertility treatment.

title: High live birth rates after laparoscopic isthmocele repair in infertility: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Cesarean section commonly causes isthmocele, a scar defect in the lower uterus that significantly reduces pregnancy and live birth rates to as low as 20-30% and creates abnormal bleeding, pelvic pain, and ectopic pregnancy risk. Isthmocele impairs fertility through multiple mechanisms, including intrauterine fluid accumulation and residual bleeding that inhibit embryo implantation and create an unreceptive endometrial environment. Laparoscopic isthmocele repair substantially improves reproductive outcomes, achieving clinical pregnancy rates of 62% and live birth rates of 72% in infertile women while reducing miscarriage rates to approximately 10%. The procedure also reduces postmenstrual bleeding and chronic endometritis through complete niche resection and offers additional benefits of diagnosing and treating coexisting conditions such as endometriosis, which was present in 29% of patients and itself impairs post-cesarean wound healing through chronic inflammation and fibrosis. These findings demonstrate that laparoscopic repair restores anatomical and functional uterine integrity, improving passage for embryo transfer and reproductive potential in women with isthmocele-related infertility.

title: Suture Fixation of Levonorgestrel-Releasing Device Using the Hysteroscopic Surgery System
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary:
# Summary

The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) is an effective therapeutic option for managing adenomyosis while simultaneously reducing associated symptoms including vaginal bleeding and dysmenorrhea. However, device expulsion represents a significant clinical challenge in certain patient populations. This study evaluated hysteroscopic cold-knife-assisted suture fixation as a novel technique to secure the LNG-IUS in place, thereby preventing device expulsion in patients with a history of IUS displacement. The suture fixation approach offers a mechanically superior solution by anchoring the device to the uterine wall during hysteroscopic placement, enhancing retention and treatment efficacy. This combined strategy—securing the LNG-IUS with suture fixation—provides patients with improved adenomyosis management while substantially reducing the risk of device-related complications.

title: Diagnostic challenges and surgical options for cysts of the canal of Nuck in adults: Two cases report
summary:
# Summary

Cysts of the canal of Nuck, arising from incomplete closure of the embryologic processus vaginalis, present as palpable groin masses that may be associated with menstrual cycle-related pain and tenderness due to involvement of nearby nerves. These cysts are classified into three types based on their anatomy: Type I (noncommunicating hydrocele, most common), Type II (communicating with the peritoneal cavity), and Type III (rare hourglass-shaped bilocular cysts), with formation potentially influenced by hormonal changes, trauma, or infection affecting lymph drainage. The primary clinical concern is that long-standing cysts can weaken the tissue structures around the inner inguinal ring, predisposing patients to indirect inguinal hernias that may contain intestines or ovaries, creating risk for serious complications including intestinal strangulation, obstruction, or ovarian torsion and necrosis. Imaging diagnosis relies on high-resolution ultrasound, MRI, or CT, though these modalities must differentiate Nuck cysts from morphologically similar conditions such as lymphangioma and endometriosis. Surgical intervention is the definitive treatment, with tension-free hernioplasty combined with cyst resection being preferred over simple cyst aspiration, which carries high recurrence rates. The surgical approach must carefully preserve the round ligament of the uterus to avoid postoperative complications such as labial edema, numbness, and sexual dysfunction.

title: MRI assessment of sacral injury location and analysis of influencing factors after high-intensity focused ultrasound ablation for patients with uterine fibroids
summary:
# Summary

Ultrasound-guided high-intensity focused ultrasound (USgHIFU) is an effective treatment for uterine fibroids but carries significant risks of sacral and adjacent tissue injury, with 42.3% of patients experiencing vertebral injuries on postoperative MRI. The location of sacral injuries is predictable based on multiple fibroid characteristics including the distance from the fibroid's ventral surface to the abdominal wall skin, uterine position, and T2-weighted signal intensity, with retroverted uteri and increased fibroid distance correlating with lower sacral involvement. Lower sacral injuries are associated with substantially higher rates of complications, including piriformis muscle damage (74.8% versus 31.5% in upper sacral injury groups) and sacrococcygeal pain. Beyond direct sacral trauma, USgHIFU can cause diffuse postoperative pain affecting the sacrococcygeal region, legs, and lower abdomen, with recovery requiring extended periods. These findings underscore the importance of preoperative MRI assessment to identify patients at highest risk for lower sacral injury and its associated musculoskeletal complications, enabling better patient counseling and potentially refined treatment protocols.

title: Infantile uterus and uterine hypoplasia: a comprehensive overview to explore possible managements amidst limited scientific certainties
summary: Infantile uterus and uterine hypoplasia are developmental disorders arising from abnormalities in Müllerian duct differentiation, which normally generate the uterus, fallopian tubes, cervix, and upper vagina. These conditions are distinguished by their body-to-cervix ratios: infantile uterus presents with a 1:1 or 1:2 ratio and total length less than 6 cm, while uterine hypoplasia maintains a normal 2:1 ratio but remains abnormally small. Multiple etiologies underlie these disorders, including endocrine failures (pituitary insufficiency, thyroid dysfunction), specific genetic syndromes (MRKH, Swyer, Turner), in-utero exposures (diethylstilbestrol), and hormonal deficiencies affecting pubertal growth. Both conditions cause significant reproductive morbidity through infertility, menstrual irregularities, dyspareunia, and pregnancy loss, mechanisms involving reduced endometrial surface area, nonfunctional endometrium, and abnormal uterine contractions due to decreased muscle fiber content. Treatment approaches include hormonal therapy with estrogen administration for hormone-deficient cases and surgical interventions including hysteroscopy, both showing variable effectiveness in improving fertility outcomes. Diagnosis relies on imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound, hysterosalpingography, and hysteroscopy, with the latter providing both diagnostic and therapeutic capabilities for managing intrauterine pathology.

title: Diagnosis, management and outcomes of incarceration or intussusception of Fallopian tubes following uterine perforation after vacuum aspiration or dilatation and curettage of the uterine cavity: a systematic review of the literature
summary:
# Summary

Vacuum aspiration and dilation and curettage procedures carry a well-established risk of uterine perforation, with surgeon inexperience accounting for approximately 95.6% of cases, particularly during postpartum hysteroscopic procedures. Uterine perforation can lead to serious complications including fallopian tube incarceration, occurring in 42.3% of cases after vacuum aspiration and 27% after dilation and curettage, as well as injury to abdominal organs, sepsis, and hemorrhage. Patients with fallopian tube incarceration present with non-specific symptoms including abdominopelvic pain (54%), abnormal vaginal bleeding, vaginal discharge, secondary amenorrhea, and secondary infertility, making diagnosis challenging since the trapped tube itself acts as a hemostatic agent delaying recognition. Diagnosis is typically established through sequential imaging with transvaginal ultrasound as first-line investigation, followed by hysteroscopy and diagnostic laparoscopy, with 3D ultrasound and color Doppler improving differential diagnosis. Treatment primarily involves laparoscopic management, with salpingectomy necessary in 46.1% of cases, though tubal preservation with extraction and myometrial suturing is advocated when feasible to maintain reproductive potential. Prevention strategies emphasize adequate surgeon training, appropriate instrumentation, careful preoperative evaluation, ultrasound guidance, and controlled negative pressure during procedures to minimize perforation risk and associated morbidity.

title: Spontaneous rupture of uterine artery in a non-pregnant woman with adenomyosis: a case report and review of current literature
summary: This case report documents a rare presentation of spontaneous uterine artery rupture in a non-pregnant woman with adenomyosis, manifesting as severe vaginal bleeding and haemoperitoneum. Adenomyosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium, appears to promote vascular pathology through mechanisms similar to endometriosis, including local erosion of uterine vessels by diseased tissue and inflammation-induced vascular defects exacerbated by oestrogen. While spontaneous uterine artery rupture typically occurs during pregnancy, labour, or the postpartum period, this case highlights adenomyosis as an underrecognized etiology in non-pregnant women, alongside other acquired causes such as caesarean section, pelvic infection, gestational trophoblastic disease, and fibroid degeneration. The pathophysiology involves pressure dynamics, anatomic disruption of vessel integrity, and hormonal changes that weaken arterial walls. Treatment options include conservative management with tranexamic acid and progestins for initial stabilization, with trans-arterial embolisation or hysterectomy reserved for definitive control of haemorrhage. This case expands the clinical recognition of adenomyosis-related vascular complications and emphasizes the importance of considering adenomyosis in the differential diagnosis of unexplained severe bleeding in non-pregnant women.

title: A comparative analysis of the impact of three distinct laparoscopic myomectomy techniques on ovarian reserve: a randomized clinical trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized clinical trial compared three laparoscopic myomectomy techniques—temporary uterine artery occlusion (TUAO), permanent uterine artery occlusion (PUAO), and vasopressin injection (VPI)—for treating symptomatic uterine fibroids while evaluating their effects on ovarian reserve. Uterine fibroids cause multiple clinical complications including heavy menstrual bleeding, painful periods, pelvic pressure, frequent urination, infertility, and miscarriage, making surgical intervention necessary for symptomatic cases. The three hemostatic techniques demonstrated comparable impacts on ovarian reserve markers, with anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) levels and hemoglobin concentrations showing no significant differences before and after surgery across all three groups. While PUAO showed the greatest decline in AMH levels post-operatively and VPI produced the largest hemoglobin reduction, these differences were not statistically significant, suggesting all three laparoscopic approaches preserve ovarian function similarly. These findings support that the choice among these minimally invasive techniques can be guided by surgeon preference and patient-specific factors without compromising reproductive outcomes.

title: T-shaped dysmorphic uterus: discrepancies between current 3D-ultrasound diagnostic criteria
summary: T-shaped uterus is a rare Müllerian anomaly strongly associated with reproductive dysfunction, affecting 46% of patients with infertility and recurrent pregnancy loss, along with 30% experiencing pelvic pain and endometriosis. Three-dimensional transvaginal ultrasound (3D-TVS) combined with hysteroscopic confirmation provides reliable diagnostic criteria, with lateral bulging ≥5 mm, R0/Wi ratio ≥5, and R10 measurement ≤10 mm identified as the most accurate independent predictors, with any two criteria identifying 96% of cases. The study reveals significant discrepancies in diagnostic thresholds across existing criteria sets, with the Exacoustos 2015 parameters (lateral bulging ≥5 mm, lateral indentation angle ≤140°, and R0/Wi ≥5) demonstrating superior diagnostic accuracy compared to newer CUME criteria. Notably, 62% of patients were infertile (21 primary and 10 secondary cases), and 90% were nulliparous, highlighting the clinical significance of accurate diagnosis. Hysteroscopic metroplasty shows promise for restoring normal uterine cavity anatomy and improving reproductive outcomes. The standardization of diagnostic criteria is essential to reduce ambiguity in T-shaped uterus detection and enable timely intervention for affected patients.

title: Dydrogesterone for preventing endometrial polyp recurrence after transcervical resection of polyps: protocol for a multicentre randomised controlled trial in China
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial polyps cause abnormal uterine bleeding, infertility, and recurrent reproductive failure, with etiology potentially involving local estrogen stimulation, progesterone receptor dysfunction, and chronic endometritis. While transcervical resection of polyps (TCRP) is the first-line surgical treatment, polyp recurrence remains a significant clinical problem that perpetuates these symptoms. Progesterone inhibits endometrial polyp development by inducing epithelial atrophy and enhancing immune function, making progestin therapy a rational preventive strategy. This multicentre randomised controlled trial investigates whether oral dydrogesterone administration for three menstrual cycles following TCRP effectively reduces endometrial polyp recurrence and associated abnormal uterine bleeding, building on evidence that both dydrogesterone and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems reduce recurrence rates. The study addresses an important clinical need by testing a convenient oral progestin regimen as an alternative to intrauterine delivery systems for polyp recurrence prevention.

title: Hematological Evaluation and Management of Menorrhagia in Adolescents: Lessons from Adolescent Heavy Menstrual Bleeding Clinics
summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding in adolescents arises from multiple etiologies, with anovulation due to hypothalamic-pituitary immaturity being the most common cause, alongside significant contributions from bleeding disorders such as von Willebrand disease, platelet function defects, and immune thrombocytopenia, as well as endocrine conditions including polycystic ovary syndrome and thyroid disorders. Bleeding disorders are identified in 10-62% of adolescents with heavy menstrual bleeding, and the bleeding assessment tool score correlates strongly with disease severity, hospitalization rates, and lower hemoglobin levels, with nearly half of evaluated patients requiring hospitalization due to anemia. Management is multifaceted, with tranexamic acid effectively reducing blood loss and serving as first-line therapy for most patients, while hormonal therapies including combined estrogen-progestin and progestin-only formulations are initiated in approximately 62% of cases. Iron supplementation, both oral and intravenous formulations, addresses iron deficiency in a substantial proportion of patients, and specific bleeding disorders receive targeted treatment including factor replacement for von Willebrand disease and platelet transfusion for Glanzmann thrombasthenia. Intrauterine devices demonstrate superior efficacy in reducing blood loss compared to tranexamic acid, though hormonal therapy and antifibrinolytics remain the cornerstones of guideline-recommended management for adolescent heavy menstrual bleeding.

title: Complete Cystic Degeneration of a Uterine Myoma Posing a Diagnostic Dilemma
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids are benign smooth muscle tumors commonly associated with heavy menstrual bleeding, iron deficiency anemia, dyspareunia, abdominal pain, and infertility, with increased incidence linked to race, age, lifestyle factors, and vitamin D deficiency. Rapidly enlarging fibroids can undergo cystic degeneration when tumor growth outpaces blood supply, resulting in ischemic and inflammatory changes that create diagnostic imaging challenges and elevated CA-125 levels. This case presents a large, completely cystic fibroid arising from the anterior myometrium that caused peritoneal distention, peritoneal irritation, and hemoglobin reduction. Degenerated fibroids display atypical imaging features including heterogeneous echogenicity, calcification, and cystic appearance on ultrasound, while MRI offers superior soft tissue contrast resolution and can concurrently evaluate for adenomyosis and endometriosis. The distinctive imaging patterns of fibroid degeneration—such as T1 hyperintense rims in red degeneration and high T2 signal intensity in myxoid degeneration—reflect underlying pathologic changes including methemoglobin accumulation and edema. Total abdominal hysterectomy provided definitive treatment for this symptomatic, degenerating fibroid.

title: Platelet Count and Platelet Hematocrit Correlate to the Occurrence and Postoperative Recurrence in Intrauterine Adhesion Patients
summary:
# Platelet Count and Hematocrit as Biomarkers for Intrauterine Adhesion Occurrence and Recurrence

Intrauterine adhesions (IUA) develop from endometrial damage caused by uterine procedures, infections, and other factors, resulting in clinical complications including abnormal menstrual flow, amenorrhea, abdominal pain, and infertility. Platelet count (PLT) and platelet hematocrit (PCT) were significantly elevated in both initial IUA patients and those experiencing postoperative recurrence compared to healthy controls, with their combination showing superior diagnostic capacity for detecting IUA. Platelets contribute to IUA formation through their involvement in wound healing, inflammatory responses, coagulation, and scar formation, working alongside monocytes, macrophages, fibroblasts, and growth factors including vascular endothelial growth factor and basic fibroblast growth factor. PLT and PCT demonstrated positive associations with IUA recurrence following hysteroscopic adhesion lysis, with recurrent IUA patients characterized by longer disease duration, greater severity, higher abortion history, and elevated platelet markers at the time of recurrence. In contrast, mean platelet volume (MPV) and platelet distribution width (PDW) showed no significant correlation with either IUA occurrence or recurrence. These findings establish elevated platelet parameters as potential biomarkers for predicting both initial IUA development and postoperative recurrence risk.

title: Diagnosing adenomyosis using transvaginal ultrasound in current practice: A scoping review and service evaluation
journal: Ultrasound: Journal of the British Medical Ultrasound Society
summary: This scoping review evaluates the effectiveness of transvaginal ultrasound in diagnosing adenomyosis in current clinical practice. Sonographers play a critical role in identifying and reporting adenomyosis during transvaginal ultrasound examinations, yet the review reveals significant diagnostic challenges. A key finding is that adenomyosis is frequently associated with heterogeneous myometrial appearance, though many cases presenting with this characteristic finding go unidentified and are instead reported simply as showing "heterogeneous myometrium" without adenomyosis diagnosis. The study highlights a substantial gap between the imaging findings present and their accurate clinical interpretation, suggesting that improved sonographer training and standardized reporting protocols may be necessary to enhance adenomyosis detection rates on transvaginal ultrasound. These findings underscore the importance of developing more consistent diagnostic criteria and education to optimize the identification of adenomyosis in routine ultrasound practice.

title: Patterns and Outcomes in the Management of Uterine Fibroids: A Hospital-Based Retrospective Study
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids are benign tumors that cause significant morbidity through multiple mechanisms depending on their anatomical location. Submucosal fibroids, located beneath the endometrial lining, trigger the most severe symptoms including heavy menstrual bleeding, which leads to secondary complications like anemia and fatigue, as well as increased risks of miscarriage and preterm labor. Intramural fibroids within the uterine muscle cause menstrual irregularities and pelvic pressure, while larger or multiple fibroids create compressive symptoms affecting the bladder, rectum, and nerves, resulting in urinary frequency, constipation, and back pain. Treatment selection is individualized based on patient age, fibroid size, and symptom severity, ranging from medical management with hormonal therapy, GnRH agonists, and intrauterine devices for smaller fibroids to surgical interventions including myomectomy and hysterectomy for larger tumors and older patients. Both surgical and minimally invasive procedures such as uterine artery embolization demonstrated high efficacy in symptom resolution with 75-90 percent patient satisfaction rates.

title: Abdominal Sepsis With Pseudomonas aeruginosa After Oocyte Retrieval
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare but severe complication of oocyte retrieval for assisted reproductive technology: intra-abdominal sepsis caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Although infection following transvaginal oocyte retrieval occurs in approximately one in 500 cases, this represents the first documented case of Pseudomonas aeruginosa-associated sepsis following the procedure, likely introduced through needle passage into the vaginal microbiota. Multiple risk factors predispose patients to pelvic infection after oocyte retrieval, including prior pelvic inflammatory disease, adnexal adhesions, and endometriomas, while inadvertent bowel puncture may also introduce infection. Patients who develop post-retrieval infections face significantly elevated risks of implantation failure, pregnancy loss, sepsis, and preterm birth if pregnancy is achieved. The patient was successfully treated with broad-spectrum antibiotics including fluoroquinolone-sensitive therapy and underwent diagnostic laparoscopy with peritoneal washout, resulting in dramatic clinical improvement and highlighting the importance of early surgical intervention in severe post-retrieval infections.

title: Research trends and hotspots of single nucleotide polymorphisms in endometrial cancer: a bibliometric analysis
summary:
# Summary

Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) represent the most common genetic variations associated with endometrial cancer risk, with specific variants in genes like HNF1B and CYP19A1 playing particularly important roles. CYP19A1 SNPs influence estrogen biosynthesis and are linked to hormone-driven endometrial cancer in postmenopausal women, where unopposed estrogen exposure initiates endometrial hyperplasia that may progress to malignancy. Recent advances in genomic approaches, including genome-wide association studies (GWAS) and Mendelian randomization, have identified susceptibility loci and enhanced causal inference in understanding endometrial cancer pathogenesis. SNP genotyping and genetic profiling enable identification of high-risk individuals and guide personalized hormone therapies tailored to individual genetic profiles, supporting early preventive interventions. Integrating SNP data with environmental and hormonal factors is emerging as crucial for advancing prevention, diagnosis, and personalized treatment of endometrial cancer, representing a shift toward precision medicine in reproductive oncology.

title: A Dual-Sequence MRI-Based Radiomics Model for Predicting High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound Ablation Efficacy in Adenomyosis Treatment
summary: This study demonstrates that adenomyosis, characterized by endometrial invasion into the uterine muscle, causes significant clinical symptoms including dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, and uterine enlargement. High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) represents a novel non-invasive treatment that effectively reduces adenomyosis symptoms, with efficacy directly correlating to immediate postoperative ablation rates. The researchers developed a dual-sequence MRI radiomics model incorporating T2-weighted fat suppression and T1 contrast-enhanced sequences to predict HIFU treatment success, as these imaging biomarkers—along with apparent diffusion coefficient and ultrasound perfusion parameters—are strongly associated with ablation efficacy. The integrated radiomics model combining both MRI sequences achieved superior predictive performance with area under the curve values of 0.840 and 0.804 for predicting ablation rates exceeding 50% and 70%, respectively, substantially outperforming individual sequence models. Pre-treatment clinical variables including baseline pain scores (VAS), ultrasonic blood flow, uterine size, lesion size, and patient age independently predicted ablation success thresholds. This dual-sequence MRI radiomics approach emerges as a promising tool for non-invasively predicting HIFU efficacy in adenomyosis treatment, potentially enabling personalized treatment planning and patient selection.

title: Exploring uterine niche: a systemic review on secondary infertility rates, pathophysiological correlations, impact on assisted reproduction technology (ART), and the efficacy of surgical interventions
summary:
# Summary

Cesarean section creates uterine niches—reservoir-like cavities in the anterior uterine isthmus—that are strongly associated with secondary infertility through multiple pathophysiological mechanisms. The decreased residual myometrial thickness (particularly below 2.5 mm), chronic inflammatory changes, and intrauterine fluid accumulation at the niche site compromise the uterine environment for sperm penetration and embryo implantation, while elevated inflammatory cytokines including tumor necrosis factor-alpha, interleukin-1 beta, and interleukin-6 further impair reproductive outcomes. Uterine niches reduce clinical pregnancy rates, live birth rates, and implantation rates in both natural conception and assisted reproductive technology cycles, with intracavity fluid and fibrosis-induced altered contractility exacerbating these effects. Surgical interventions including hysteroscopy, laparoscopy, and combined approaches effectively treat uterine niches by increasing residual myometrial thickness and improving fertility outcomes, with post-operative myometrial thickness directly correlating with pregnancy success. Additionally, uterine niches are associated with secondary complications including post-menstrual spotting, prolonged bleeding, and cesarean scar ectopic pregnancy, making their recognition and management clinically significant for women with prior cesarean delivery seeking pregnancy.

title: Robert’s Uterus-Types, Diagnosis, and Management Options: Case Series and Review of Literature
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: Robert's uterus is a rare Müllerian anomaly characterized by a blind hemi-uterine cavity with a normal external fundus, involving a thick myometrial septum in oblique orientation. The condition presents in three distinct types with different clinical manifestations: Type 1 with a fully functional hemi-uterus causes severe progressive dysmenorrhea in adolescence, Type 2 with a nonfunctional hemi-uterus presents later with recurrent pregnancy loss and infertility, and Type 3 with residual functioning endometrial tissue causes dysmenorrhea accompanied by hematometra, hematosalpinx, and endometriosis via retrograde menstruation. Diagnosis requires imaging modalities including transabdominal ultrasound, 4D ultrasound, and MRI, with combined hysteroscopy and laparoscopy providing definitive confirmation and differentiation from similar anomalies. Hysteroscopic septal resection under ultrasound guidance is the preferred treatment, offering immediate symptomatic relief of dysmenorrhea and improved future reproductive outcomes, with patients achieving complete or significant symptom resolution. Early recognition and appropriate surgical intervention are critical, as delayed diagnosis may lead to inappropriate surgeries and fertility compromise.

title: Herlyn Werner Wunderlich Syndrome/OHVIRA: A Rare Case Report with Successful Laparoscopy Assisted Vaginoplasty
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: Herlyn Werner Wunderlich Syndrome, also known as OHVIRA (Obstructed Hemivagina and Ipsilateral Renal Agenesis), is a rare congenital disorder resulting from failure of lateral fusion of the developing Mullerian ducts, which causes a complete vertical vaginal septum and uterine didelphys. This anatomical obstruction prevents menstrual blood drainage from the affected hemivagina, leading to accumulation of blood (hematometra and hematocolpos) and causing severe dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and fever typically appearing after menarche. A critical consequence of delayed diagnosis is peritoneal spillage of menstrual blood, which promotes endometriosis development and subsequently causes infertility, fallopian tube damage, and ectopic pregnancy risk. Late presentation also allows progression to tubal destruction and pelvic adhesions, significantly compromising future fertility. Early diagnosis through imaging enables timely surgical intervention via laparoscopy-assisted vaginal septal resection to unify the hemivaginas and prevent these devastating reproductive complications. This case highlights the importance of prompt recognition and treatment of OHVIRA to preserve reproductive function and prevent endometriosis-related sequelae.

title: An Uncommon Infection due to an Unusual Pathogen. Report of a Pubic Symphisis Septic Arthritis after Caesarean Delivery due to Cutibacterium Acnes
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: This case report describes an exceptionally rare infection of the pubic symphysis caused by Cutibacterium acnes following caesarean delivery, a complication previously unreported with this particular pathogen. Pubic symphysis septic arthritis is normally associated with pregnancy and delivery or urological surgery, but is more commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus and related species; the causative role of Cutibacterium acnes is particularly unusual given this organism's typical limitation to shoulder and clavicle infections and its tendency to suppress inflammatory responses, which can delay symptom recognition. The infection resulted in severe complications including chronic lumbopelvic pain, adductor muscle pain, demineralization of the symphysis margins, and destabilization of the pelvic girdle requiring surgical intervention. Treatment involved extended antibiotic regimens combining ceftriaxone, rifampicin, levofloxacin, amoxicillin, and clindamycin over multiple phases, complemented by surgical debridement and abscess drainage, which ultimately proved essential for achieving symptom resolution and functional recovery. This case highlights the importance of considering Cutibacterium acnes as a potential pathogen in postpartum pubic symphysis infections and demonstrates that aggressive surgical management combined with prolonged antimicrobial therapy is necessary to prevent serious long-term sequelae affecting pelvic stability and patient mobility.

title: Vacuum-Assisted Closure Therapy Following Mesh Removal Due to Multidrug-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Infection in a High-Risk, Multimorbid, Hernia Repair Patient: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case report demonstrates a multidisciplinary approach to managing mesh infection caused by multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa in a high-risk surgical patient. Multiple comorbidities—including obesity, diabetes mellitus, smoking, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, chronic kidney disease, and cardiovascular disease—significantly increased infection risk by impairing tissue perfusion, weakening immune defenses, and delaying wound healing. Management involved mesh removal, extensive debridement, and targeted antibiotic therapy with amikacin and colistin, combined with vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) therapy, which reduced bacterial levels and edema while promoting tissue perfusion and granulation tissue development. VAC therapy activated multiple simultaneous healing processes through negative pressure that eliminated fluid and exudate while promoting blood vessel growth and angiogenesis. Although the infection was successfully controlled and subsequent abdominoplasty achieved wound closure, the patient developed recurrent hernia during follow-up, highlighting the challenge that while mesh removal is necessary to control infection, it significantly increases hernia recurrence risk. This case underscores the importance of comprehensive patient assessment, culture-directed antibiotic selection, and multimodal wound management strategies in complex surgical infections.

title: Does adenomyosis influence tumor characteristics and survival in endometrioid-type endometrial cancer??
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated whether adenomyosis influences tumor characteristics and survival outcomes in endometrioid-type endometrial cancer. The analysis revealed that adenomyosis is associated with a notably more favorable oncological profile, characterized by significantly reduced rates of deep myometrial invasion, lymphovascular space invasion, lymph node metastasis, adnexal metastasis, and distant metastasis compared to cases without adenomyosis. Patients with adenomyosis presented with earlier-stage tumors, lower-grade lesions, and smaller tumor sizes, along with higher parity and gravidity. The 5-year overall survival rate was 90.8% in the adenomyosis group versus 87.1% in the non-adenomyosis group. The proposed protective mechanisms include adenomyosis serving as a mechanical barrier to tumor invasion, the hypertrophied endometrial stroma impeding myometrial penetration, and a distinct inflammatory cytokine profile that restricts tumor development and invasiveness. These findings suggest adenomyosis may serve as a protective factor in endometrial cancer prognosis and should be incorporated into clinical risk assessment strategies.

title: Intravesical Ectopic Pregnancy in Vesicouterine Fistula Patient: A Very Rare Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Vesicouterine fistula (VUF), an abnormal connection between the bladder and uterus, most commonly results from iatrogenic injury during obstetric procedures including cesarean section, uterine curettage, forceps-assisted delivery, and cervical dilatation, though additional risk factors such as endometriosis, placenta percreta, and intrauterine device migration have been identified. VUF presents with characteristic clinical manifestations including urinary incontinence, cyclic hematuria, amenorrhea, recurrent urinary tract infections, and vaginal urine leakage, with fetal migration potentially causing additional complications. The pathophysiology involves direct bladder damage, abnormal sutures, inadequate tissue mobilization during surgical procedures, and subsequent devascularization, infection, or hematoma formation that perpetuates fistula development. Imaging modalities including cystography, hysterography, computed tomography, and magnetic resonance imaging are essential for diagnosis and localization. Management approaches range from conservative bladder catheterization for spontaneous resolution to definitive surgical intervention using vaginal, trans-vesical, transperitoneal, or laparoscopic approaches, with hysterectomy reserved for selected cases, while this case uniquely involved fetal evacuation combined with bladder repair and fistula repair to achieve symptom resolution.

title: Adenomyosis and fibrosis define the morphological memory of the postpartum uterus of dairy cows previously exposed to metritis
summary: Early postpartum metritis in dairy cows, triggered by pathogenic bacterial invasion into the uterus within the first week after calving, initiates persistent pathological changes in uterine morphology that impair long-term fertility. Cows experiencing metritis develop significant adenomyosis characterized by increased number, diameter, and myometrial invasion of endometrial glands, alongside pronounced endometrial fibrosis particularly concentrated at the endometrial-myometrial interface and stratum basalis. These morphological alterations—adenomyosis and fibrosis—appear correlated and may be driven by sustained endometrial inflammation following the initial infection. The pathological remodeling persists through mid-lactation and is associated with decreased fertility and delayed pregnancy establishment. This study demonstrates that metritis creates a lasting "morphological memory" in the postpartum uterus, establishing that successful uterine involution following infection is critical for optimal reproductive recovery in lactating dairy cattle.

title: High Risk Factors for Placenta Accreta Other than Pregnancy and Their Impact on Patient Prognosis
summary: Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) disorders represent a serious obstetric condition where abnormal placental trophoblastic invasion into the uterine wall leads to severe maternal and neonatal complications. Multiple non-pregnancy risk factors significantly increase PAS incidence, including prior cesarean delivery, dilation and curettage, myomectomy, uterine artery embolization, and hysteroscopy—all of which cause endometrial damage that impairs local vascularization, tissue oxygenation, and decidualization. Maternal age, placenta previa, assisted reproductive technology, and uterine anomalies including endometriosis, myomas, and adenomyosis are independently associated with increased PAS risk. Coexistent complete placenta previa strongly predicts severe PAS and greater blood loss, while endometrial damage specifically leads to lower neonatal Apgar scores and asphyxia through mechanisms of insufficient placental function. Ultrasound remains the primary diagnostic tool, with characteristic findings including placental lacunae, loss of hypoechoic space, uterus-bladder interface abnormalities, hypervascularity, and bridging vessels. PAS disorders cause significant maternal morbidity including postpartum hemorrhage, disseminated intravascular coagulation, shock, and infection, with blood transfusion requirements correlating to PAS severity.

title: Metritis and the uterine disease microbiome are associated with long-term changes in the endometrium of dairy cows†
summary: Early postpartum metritis and the associated uterine disease microbiome in dairy cows trigger long-lasting alterations in endometrial gene expression that correlate with reduced fertility. Both metritis and the pathogenic microbiome induce widespread differential gene expression in the caruncular endometrium, with oxidative phosphorylation pathways elevated while growth factor signaling pathways are suppressed. Although the disease microbiome itself may resolve over time, the sustained transcriptional imprint it leaves on the endometrium persists and is associated with significantly lower pregnancy rates following artificial insemination. This study provides evidence that early postpartum uterine disease creates a prolonged molecular dysregulation of the endometrium that compromises reproductive capacity independent of continued microbial presence. These findings suggest that metritis-induced transcriptional changes in key metabolic and growth signaling pathways underlie the long-term fertility impairment observed in affected animals.

title: Fertility Preservation in Female Children and Adolescent Cancer Patients
summary:
# Summary: Fertility Preservation in Female Children and Adolescent Cancer Patients

Cancer treatments pose severe threats to reproductive health in pediatric and adolescent females through multiple mechanisms. Chemotherapy agents, particularly alkylating agents like cyclophosphamide, cause follicular apoptosis and premature ovarian failure, while radiation therapy damages ovarian tissue through DNA double-strand breaks, reactive oxygen species generation, and vascular ischemia, with notably severe effects at doses exceeding 2 Gy affecting half of all ovarian follicles. Surgical interventions and combined modalities further compromise fertility, with whole pelvic radiation at 20-30 Gy producing ovarian failure rates as high as 97%. To mitigate these gonadotoxic effects, multiple protective strategies have emerged, including mTOR inhibitors like everolimus that preserve primordial follicles and ovarian reserve, AS101 which blocks cyclophosphamide-induced follicle loss, and physical interventions such as ovarian transposition (>1.5 cm above iliac crest) and proton beam therapy that reduce ovarian radiation exposure. Adjunctive approaches including antioxidant therapies (sodium selenite, platelet-rich plasma) and transcutaneous electrical acupoint stimulation show promise in attenuating radiation-induced ovarian damage and maintaining anti-mullerian hormone secretion, while fertility-sparing surgical techniques provide additional options for preserving reproductive potential in young cancer patients.

title: Benefits of Traditional Medicinal Plants to African Women’s Health: An Overview of the Literature
summary: This literature review examines the traditional medicinal plants used by African women to address reproductive health conditions across pregnancy, postpartum recovery, menopause, and fertility issues. The article identifies numerous plant species that have been ethnobotanically documented to treat pregnancy-related symptoms such as morning sickness (ginger, chamomile, fennel), edema (corn), and constipation (linseed, okra), as well as complications including postpartum hemorrhage (Pterocarpus, Solanum, Senna species) and pelvic pain (tulip tree). Beyond pregnancy management, traditional remedies address menopausal symptoms (ginseng, chaste tree, sage) and female infertility through multiple plant species including Tetrapleura tetraptera, Annona senegalensis, and Amorphophalus abyssinicus. The review also documents plant-based approaches to uterine pathology, particularly uterine fibroids, treated with combinations of ginger, garlic, Aloe vera, and other species across West and North African regions. Notably, while most plants are reported as therapeutic agents, Momordica charantia is identified as having abortifacient properties, highlighting the need for careful use and further scientific validation of these traditional remedies to ensure safety and efficacy for African women's health.

title: A bibliometric analysis of the frontiers and hotspots in chronic endometritis
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis (CE) is characterized by endometrial plasmacyte infiltration and elevated stromal cell density, primarily caused by microbial infection that triggers aberrant expression of chemokines and adhesion molecules, ultimately leading to B-cell migration and abnormal immune responses that compromise endometrial receptivity. CD138 immunostaining on endometrial biopsy is the diagnostic gold standard, with hysteroscopy serving as a supplementary screening tool. CE is strongly associated with adverse reproductive outcomes including recurrent miscarriage, recurrent implantation failure, infertility, preeclampsia, and preterm labor through mechanisms involving sustained maternal immune activation, abnormal placentation, and vascular dysfunction. Antibiotic treatment effectively resolves CE and significantly improves pregnancy outcomes, restoring live birth rates and ongoing pregnancy rates in affected women. The molecular cascade involves bacterial lipopolysaccharides inducing E-selectin and upregulating CXCL13/CXCL1, which activate B-cell adhesion molecules, while genetic factors such as KIR AA genotype contribute to individual susceptibility by reducing protective inflammatory cytokines. CE is frequently associated with infertility-related conditions including fallopian tubal obstruction, endometriosis, and infections such as Chlamydia trachomatis and bacterial vaginosis, establishing it as a critical frontier in understanding reproductive pathology.

title: Robotic-assisted laparoscopic niche repair (RALNR): technique development and pregnancy-associated outcomes
summary: Cesarean sections create uterine scar defects (niches) that pose serious complications including cesarean scar pregnancy, placenta spectrum disorders, uterine rupture, abnormal bleeding, pelvic pain, and secondary infertility. Residual myometrial thickness (RMT) is the key prognostic factor predicting these obstetric complications, with severe deficiencies (RMT <3 mm) conferring a 26-fold increased rupture risk. Robotic-assisted laparoscopic niche repair (RALNR) significantly improves RMT from 1.5 mm preoperatively to 8.3 mm postoperatively while reducing niche width by 74% and depth by 89%, with symptom relief achieved in 83% of abnormal bleeding cases and improved pelvic pain in half of affected patients. A 77% pregnancy rate was achieved following RALNR, with 54% resulting in live births beyond 24 weeks, though 38% experienced early pregnancy loss. The procedure benefits from robot-assisted enhancement of fine motor skills and may be combined with diaphanoscopy to improve surgical precision in reconstructing functional anterior uterine walls with robust multi-layer closure.

title: The etiology differs regards to the locations of the lesion: a clinical experience of 1350 patients with adenomyosis confirmed by postoperative pathology
summary: Adenomyosis is a common gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium, causing myometrial hypertrophy, hyperplasia, and fibrosis that results in dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, secondary anemia, infertility, and obstetric complications. In this large cohort of 1,350 pathologically confirmed adenomyosis cases, the disease manifested distinct etiological patterns based on lesion location: diffuse adenomyosis was independently associated with older age, higher gravidity, elevated BMI, longer disease duration, and more severe dysmenorrhea and anemia manifestations, while posterior adenomyosis demonstrated a 3.24-fold increased risk of concurrent endometriosis, higher CA125 levels, increased intraoperative blood loss, and greater surgical complexity. Anterior adenomyosis patients exhibited higher rates of menorrhagia and greater reproductive history (gravidity and abortion), whereas focal adenomyosis presented with lumbosacral pain as a distinguishing feature. Nearly half of adenomyosis cases (49.3%) coexisted with uterine leiomyomas, followed by endometriosis (16.6%) and endometrial polyps, indicating frequent comorbidity with other uterine pathologies. These findings underscore that adenomyosis location-specific characteristics should inform clinical management strategies and surgical planning.

title: Exploratory study on the application of laser-induced cavitation bubble in the treatment of fallopian tube obstruction
summary: Fallopian tube obstruction is a major cause of female infertility, accounting for 30-40% of infertility cases and arising from inflammation, endometriosis, and surgical trauma. This exploratory study investigated laser-induced cavitation bubble (LICB) technology using an Er:YAG pulsed laser to treat obstructed fallopian tubes in rat models, with the mechanism involving rapid expansion and collapse of plasma bubbles to generate shockwaves that disintegrate obstructive fibrous tissue. Optimization of laser energy parameters was critical: 8 mJ energy achieved only 33.3% recanalization, 12.7 mJ achieved 85.7% success with minimal tissue damage, while 15.3 mJ achieved 100% recanalization but caused hemorrhagic spots and burn marks through excessive thermal damage and the Moses effect. The 12.7 mJ treatment emerged as optimal, successfully restoring tubal patency while maintaining structural integrity and reducing post-treatment inflammation and fibrous tissue proliferation. LICB technology demonstrated advantages over conventional treatments like tubal cannulation and assisted reproductive technologies by achieving effective recanalization with minimal procedural hemorrhage and reduced damage to the fallopian tube wall. These findings suggest LICB represents a promising minimally invasive approach for treating fallopian tube obstruction-related infertility, though further optimization and clinical validation are needed.

title: Isolated unilateral absence of pulmonary artery presenting as hemoptysis: A case report
summary: Isolated unilateral absence of the pulmonary artery (UAPA) is a rare congenital vascular anomaly that precipitates pulmonary hypertension through a well-defined pathophysiological cascade: abnormal blood flow through the remaining pulmonary artery generates shear stress on the endothelium, triggering endothelin release and subsequent pulmonary vasoconstriction, which progresses to chronic remodeling and elevated vascular resistance. This structural defect manifests clinically as hemoptysis resulting from excessive collateral circulation, recurrent pulmonary infections due to impaired blood supply and compromised lung defense mechanisms, and exercise intolerance. The resulting pulmonary hypertension is managed pharmacologically with endothelin receptor antagonists such as bosentan or phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors like sildenafil, often combined with diuretics and other cardiac supportive agents including spironolactone and digoxin to optimize hemodynamic function. The case report also documents concurrent autoimmune disease markers including positive antinuclear antibody, anti-PO(RPP), and anti-Ku antibodies in the patient. Recognition of UAPA as a cause of hemoptysis and pulmonary hypertension is clinically important, as it may be asymptomatic in some adults but requires targeted therapeutic intervention when symptomatic presentation occurs.

title: Ultrasound‐guided cyst aspiration for management of acute adnexal torsion
summary:
# Summary: Ultrasound-Guided Cyst Aspiration for Acute Adnexal Torsion

Acute adnexal torsion, a gynecological emergency in which the ovary and fallopian tube twist on their suspensory ligaments, commonly occurs in association with benign ovarian cysts including functional cysts, cystadenomas, and dermoid cysts. When untreated, adnexal torsion causes hypoxia leading to tissue necrosis and permanent loss of function. Ultrasound-guided cyst aspiration represents an effective alternative to emergency surgery, relieving the torque applied to the pedicle by removing cyst fluid and facilitating detorsion. In a cohort of 46 patients, 85% experienced complete symptom resolution immediately after the procedure, with 93% success rate for transabdominal aspiration and 92% success in pregnant patients.
