The patient was successfully treated with anticoagulation therapy (low-molecular-weight heparin transitioned to apixaban) and conservative management for pneumothorax, with computed tomography pulmonary angiography confirming the bilateral emboli. The authors emphasize that meticulous surgical technique employing blunt-tipped and stiff cannulas (>3.5 mm diameter), careful hemostasis with tranexamic acid, minimized positive pressure ventilation, and extended postoperative vigilance are essential to prevent these complications. Proper patient selection, comprehensive postoperative monitoring, and heightened awareness of venous thromboembolism risk in body contouring procedures are critical for surgeons performing liposuction to optimize patient safety outcomes.

title: Management of heavy menstrual bleeding in a multidisciplinary young women’s clinic: a Dutch experience
journal: Research and Practice in Thrombosis and Haemostasis
summary:
# Heavy Menstrual Bleeding in Young Women: Management and Clinical Outcomes

Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) affects young women through multiple pathways, with 52% developing iron deficiency that causes fatigue, cognitive impairment, and reduced quality of life including decreased school, work, and social participation. The underlying causes are diverse, encompassing both structural abnormalities (polyps, adenomyosis, leiomyomas) and nonstructural factors including coagulopathies, ovulatory dysfunction, and inherited bleeding disorders such as von Willebrand disease (found in 24% of patients) and platelet aggregation defects caused by genetic variants in genes like FLNA and ACTN1. In adolescents, immaturity of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis frequently leads to anovulatory cycles, which directly drive HMB. Effective management combined tranexamic acid with combined oral contraceptive pills (38% of successful cases), with levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices as an alternative, while iron supplementation addressed deficiency-related complications by improving muscle fatigability, attention, and cognition. Given that bleeding disorders represent the first symptom of previously undiagnosed coagulopathies in 34% of newly identified cases, multidisciplinary evaluation including hemostasis screening is essential for comprehensive HMB management in young women.

title: Incarceration of the Gravid Uterus in Women with Previous Cesarean Section: Case Series and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary: Incarceration of the Gravid Uterus in Women with Previous Cesarean Section

Incarceration of the gravid uterus (IGU) is a rare but serious obstetric complication with multiple risk factors, most notably previous cesarean section, which creates pelvic adhesions that mechanically restrict uterine expansion during pregnancy. A retroverted uterus—present in 15-20% of non-pregnant women—represents another major predisposing factor, along with pelvic anomalies, masses, uterine malformations, endometriosis, leiomyomas, and pelvic inflammatory disease. Clinical presentation typically manifests as abdominal or pelvic pain (44%), urinary retention (30%), and constipation, with diagnosis facilitated by ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging. Early intervention before 20 weeks of gestation through conservative management—including bladder catheterization, positional changes (knee-chest position), and manual reduction—achieves higher success rates and prevents severe maternal complications including placenta previa, placenta accreta, uterine rupture, and massive hemorrhage. Unresolved cases carry substantial morbidity risks, with 30% experiencing severe complications such as hydronephrosis and bowel obstruction, while 83% of affected pregnancies ultimately require cesarean delivery. Active early management is therefore strongly recommended to optimize outcomes in this challenging patient population.

title: Analysis of the risk factors of chronic endometritis in infertile women
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies multiple independent risk factors for chronic endometritis (CE) in infertile women, with multiparity, prolonged infertility duration, pelvic inflammatory disease sequelae, and endometrial hyperplasia emerging as the strongest predictors. Chronic endometritis compromises reproductive outcomes by activating endometrial inflammation through pathogenic infiltration of plasma cells that produce IgG2 antibodies, disrupting the immunological microenvironment critical for embryo implantation and trophoblast invasion. The condition is mechanistically linked to aberrant expression of inflammatory factors and impaired endometrial repair, particularly promoting recurrence of intrauterine adhesions and contributing to recurrent implantation failure and spontaneous abortion. Clinical presentation includes pelvic pain, dyspareunia, abnormal bleeding, and increased discharge, while diagnosis relies on detecting plasma cell infiltration via CD138 immunohistochemistry in endometrial biopsy specimens. The association between childbirth-related uterine trauma, retrograde bacterial infection, and CE is further strengthened by the observation that increased deliveries elevate risk through placental adhesions and subsequent surgical interventions. These findings underscore chronic endometritis as a significant pathogenic factor in infertility requiring consideration in reproductive management strategies.

title: Whole-disease-cycle management in a collaborative care model based on the WeChat platform: A case report
summary: This case report demonstrates the clinical management of Type I congenital vaginal atresia, a rare condition resulting from abnormal development of the urogenital sinus and Müllerian ducts that causes cyclical abdominal pain, bowel dysfunction, and impaired fertility. Delayed treatment significantly worsens outcomes by facilitating retrograde menstruation, which triggers severe pelvic endometriosis and adenomyosis with secondary inflammation. The patient was treated through multidisciplinary surgical intervention including vaginoplasty, megacolon radical surgery, and percutaneous drainage of infected hematoma, supplemented by multimodal pain management combining pharmacological relief and mindfulness music therapy. A WeChat-based telemedicine collaborative care model coordinated care across specialties—gynecology, surgery, anesthesia, nutrition, psychology, and ostomy care—enabling remote monitoring and reducing the need for frequent hospital visits while providing psychological support based on PERMA theory. This integrated approach improved quality of life and reduced postoperative complications during stoma care and the complete disease cycle. The case highlights how digital health platforms can facilitate comprehensive, coordinated management of complex gynecological and surgical conditions in pediatric patients.

title: A Complex Case Report of OHVIRA syndrome: Uterine didelphys, obstructed hemivagina, and renal agenesis
summary: OHVIRA syndrome is a rare congenital condition resulting from abnormal development of the Müllerian and Wolffian ducts, characterized by the pathological triad of uterine didelphys, obstructed hemivagina, and ipsilateral renal agenesis. The syndrome manifests clinically with cyclic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, leukorrhea, and menstrual irregularities, with the obstructed hemivagina causing hematocolpos and creating a nidus for serious complications. Delayed diagnosis significantly increases the risk of developing secondary endometriosis, pelvic infections, and infertility, underscoring the critical importance of prompt identification. Imaging plays a pivotal diagnostic role, with ultrasound serving as the first-line tool (90.9% accuracy) and MRI as the gold standard for definitively visualizing the extent of Müllerian duct anomalies and evaluating ductal communication. Treatment approaches are tailored to severity, ranging from simple septum resection for straightforward hemivaginal obstruction to hemi-hysterectomy in more complex cases involving cervical agenesis. This case report emphasizes that early recognition and appropriate surgical intervention are essential to prevent serious reproductive and infectious complications.

title: Strategies to Improve Assisted Reproductive Technique Outcomes in Women with Adenomyosis: A Narrative Review
summary: Adenomyosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue invading the myometrium, causes significant reproductive dysfunction through multiple mechanisms including myometrial fibrosis, smooth muscle hyperplasia and hypertrophy, and inflammatory changes in the eutopic endometrium. These pathological changes substantially reduce assisted reproductive technique outcomes, decreasing implantation and live birth rates while increasing miscarriage risk, particularly when the junctional zone is involved, and are associated with serious obstetric complications including preeclampsia, preterm delivery, and placental abnormalities. Multiple therapeutic strategies have demonstrated efficacy in improving ART outcomes: ultra-long gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist protocols enhance pregnancy rates in diffuse adenomyosis, the freeze-all strategy with subsequent frozen embryo transfer significantly improves cumulative live birth rates, and GnRH agonist pre-treatment combined with hormone replacement therapy increases clinical pregnancy and implantation rates. Additional approaches including levonorgestrel intrauterine system placement, dienogest pre-treatment, low-dose letrozole, and surgical adenomyomectomy also improve reproductive outcomes. These varied interventions work through suppressing ovarian hyperstimulation effects, reducing endometrial inflammatory responses, and in surgical cases, directly removing adenomyotic tissue, collectively offering women with adenomyosis substantially enhanced prospects for successful conception and live birth.

title: Valentino's Syndrome: from History to Images. A Case-Based Literature Review
summary: Valentino syndrome is a rare condition caused by gastric or duodenal perforation, typically secondary to peptic ulcer disease, in which leaked gastric fluid irritates the peritoneum and causes right lower quadrant pain that mimics acute appendicitis. Peptic ulcer disease itself results from multiple etiological factors including Helicobacter pylori infection, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug overuse, smoking, and less commonly Zollinger-Ellison disease, Crohn's disease, or Behçet's disease, with pathophysiology involving disruption of protective factors like prostaglandins and increased hydrochloric acid exposure. The perforation typically leads to either pneumoperitoneum or pneumoretroperitoneum depending on the perforation location, and the leaking intraluminal fluid causes focal peritonitis with secondary inflammatory changes in adjacent structures such as the ileum and appendix. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography is the diagnostic modality of choice for detecting and characterizing Valentino syndrome, being particularly sensitive for identifying pneumoperitoneum and pneumoretroperitoneum. Prompt surgical intervention is critical to reduce morbidity and mortality, while delayed treatment increases the risk of infectious complications, and medical management includes H. pylori eradication therapy when indicated.

title: A systems-based approach to uterine fibroids identifies differential splicing associated with abnormal uterine bleeding
summary: This systems-based study identifies molecular mechanisms linking uterine fibroids to abnormal uterine bleeding through dysregulated RNA splicing and multiple signaling pathways. Driver mutations in MED12, HMGA2, AHR, and COL4A6 are strongly associated with fibroid development and heavy menstrual bleeding, with these genes influencing angiogenesis, extracellular matrix organization, and TGF-β signaling. A key finding is that MED12-mutated fibroids alter RNA transcript isoform expression in the endometrium, leading to abnormal uterine bleeding through aberrant TGF-β signaling and disrupted alternative splicing mechanisms. Beyond genetic drivers, fibroid pathogenesis involves dysregulation of WNT-β-catenin, prolactin, and IGF-1 signaling, along with altered apoptosis, inflammation, and cytokine regulation. The research demonstrates that genetic alterations in fibroids influence endometrial function via RNA splicing disruption, establishing a mechanistic link between fibroid mutations and clinical bleeding symptoms. Treatment options span pharmacologic approaches including GnRH agonists and ulipristal acetate, alongside surgical interventions such as myomectomy, embolization, and hysterectomy.

title: Leiomyomatosis peritonealis disseminata presenting as acute abdomen: A rare case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

Leiomyomatosis peritonealis disseminata (LPD) is a rare condition characterized by proliferation of uterine smooth muscle neoplasms onto the peritoneum and other organs, predominantly affecting premenopausal women with higher hormonal levels. The pathogenesis involves hormonal factors and uterine trauma, with evidence supporting both an iatrogenic mechanism from prior myoma surgeries and a metaplastic transformation theory where elevated estrogen and progesterone levels drive submesothelial multipotent stem cell differentiation. Clinical presentation varies from asymptomatic to acute abdomen, with patients experiencing severe lower abdominal pain, vomiting, abdominal swelling, infertility, and persistent pelvic pain alongside findings of intramural and subserosal myomas on imaging. Diagnosis requires histopathology confirmation following imaging evaluation by ultrasound, CT, or MRI, with careful differentiation from leiomyosarcoma due to malignant transformation risk. Treatment depends on symptomatology, ranging from observation and hormonal suppression via GnRH agonists or aromatase inhibitors in asymptomatic cases to surgical intervention including hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy for symptomatic disease. Routine clinical and imaging follow-up is essential to identify recurrence and malignant transformation, which occur particularly with incomplete resection or widespread disease.

title: Impact of Uterine Fibroid Symptoms on Functional Work Impairment Among Employed Women Working in Healthcare in the United States
journal: Journal of Occupational and Environmental Medicine
summary: Uterine fibroids impose significant functional impairment on employed women in healthcare, causing 33% lost workplace productivity primarily through presenteeism (31%) rather than absenteeism (6%), with an estimated weekly cost of $387 per affected worker. The condition manifests through multiple symptoms including heavy menstrual bleeding (79%), painful periods (78%), and passage of large blood clots (58%), alongside additional complaints like urinary frequency that necessitate frequent restroom access. Notably, undiagnosed women with fibroid symptoms experience substantially higher presenteeism (41%) compared to diagnosed women, suggesting that clinical recognition and treatment may partially mitigate work impairment. Beyond occupational impacts, uterine fibroids are associated with 37% activity impairment in non-work settings, and obesity (present in 40% of respondents) represents an additional risk factor. Despite a variety of treatment options available—including prescription medications, over-the-counter treatments, and surgical procedures—uterine fibroids remain significantly underdiagnosed and undertreated in this population, perpetuating substantial economic burden and functional disability.

title: Nomogram to predict live birth in infertile women with adenomyosis undergoing IVF/ICSI treatment: a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis, characterized by endometrial invasion of the myometrium, significantly impairs fertility through multiple mechanisms including disruption of the uterine junctional zone, altered myometrial contractility, and reduced embryo implantation and clinical pregnancy rates. Women with adenomyosis experience elevated risks of both fertility complications (miscarriage, reduced live birth rates) and adverse obstetric outcomes (preeclampsia, preterm delivery, postpartum hemorrhage, and low birth weight). This retrospective cohort study developed a predictive nomogram for live birth outcomes in adenomyotic women undergoing IVF/ICSI treatment, identifying several key factors: younger age, lower body mass index, smaller mean initial uterine diameter, adequate endometrial thickness (≥8 mm), and singleton rather than multiple embryo transfer significantly improved live birth success. The analysis demonstrated that uterine volume is particularly important, as larger uteri correlated with higher miscarriage rates and lower live birth rates, while interventions such as GnRH-agonist treatment to reduce uterine size and optimized ovarian stimulation protocols may enhance outcomes. This nomogram provides clinicians with a practical tool for counseling adenomyotic patients on their fertility prognosis and individualizing treatment strategies to improve IVF success rates.

title: Unraveling the causal association between genetic liability to COVID-19 and Guillain-Barre Syndrome risk: Insights from genome-wide Mendelian randomization study and mediation analysis
summary: This Mendelian randomization study investigated the causal relationship between COVID-19 genetic liability and Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS) risk. While observational studies have documented associations between COVID-19 infection and GBS, an immune-mediated disorder characterized by demyelination and inflammatory cell infiltration, the genome-wide analysis found no significant causal effects of COVID-19 susceptibility, hospitalization severity, or vaccine exposure on GBS development. The study identified potential biological mediators linking these conditions, including inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-1rα) and metabolic factors (hyperlipidemia and high cholesterol), alongside hepatitis as shared risk factors in GBS pathophysiology. Notably, COVID-19-related GBS cases demonstrated predominantly demyelinating patterns and increased severity compared to non-COVID-19 cases, and SARS-CoV-2 vaccination appeared safe in individuals with prior GBS history. These findings suggest that while COVID-19 and GBS share common immunological and metabolic pathways, no direct causal genetic relationship exists between COVID-19 susceptibility and GBS development.

title: Enormous Myoma Pedunculated Through the Cervix: A Surgical Challenge
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Uterine leiomyomas are benign tumors that commonly cause abnormal uterine bleeding and pelvic pain, with Black women affected up to nine times more frequently than white women. When submucosal leiomyomas become pedunculated and prolapse through the cervix, they present a surgical challenge requiring careful preoperative characterization by MRI and selective treatment approaches. Vaginal myomectomy is the preferred treatment for prolapsed cervical leiomyomas, offering shorter operative times and reduced postoperative pain compared to laparoscopic approaches. Medical management options including GnRH analogues can reduce fibroid size by 35–61%, while uterine artery embolization achieves heavy bleeding reduction in 86–92% of patients, though complications including pain and infection must be considered. Emerging hemostatic technologies such as chitosan-covered gauze have reduced hysterectomy rates by 75%, and careful surgical technique avoiding uncontained morcellation is critical to prevent potential malignancy dissemination when leiomyosarcoma cannot be excluded preoperatively.

title: Management of post-cesarean uterine artery pseudoaneurysm in Indonesia
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery Cases, Innovations and Techniques
summary:
# Summary

Uterine artery pseudoaneurysm (UAP) is a rare but serious complication arising from cesarean section and other gynecologic procedures, caused by mechanisms such as artery ligation, puncture wounds, inflammation, or local infection. This condition presents clinically as prolonged or secondary postpartum hemorrhage occurring days to weeks after delivery and can be life-threatening if left untreated. Diagnosis is established through imaging modalities including CT angiography, ultrasound, and Doppler studies, which reveal characteristic arterial flow patterns. Transcatheter uterine artery embolization (UAE) has emerged as the definitive treatment approach, achieving approximately 97% success rates with a Vortx coil combined with gelatin sponge slurry, and offers significant advantages over surgical alternatives including shorter hospital stays, reduced morbidity, and preservation of fertility. Medical management with tranexamic acid and methylergometrine alone is ineffective, necessitating interventional approaches such as embolization or, in persistent cases, surgical ligation or hysterectomy. This case demonstrates that early radiologic diagnosis and selective endovascular embolization are critical for managing UAP and preventing maternal morbidity and mortality from hemorrhagic complications.

title: Ultrasound-guided percutaneous cryoablation under local anesthesia: a promising treatment method for symptomatic adenomyosis
journal: Quantitative Imaging in Medicine and Surgery
summary: Adenomyosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue infiltration, causes significant clinical morbidity including dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, secondary anemia, and infertility. While hysterectomy remains the definitive treatment and pharmacological approaches like gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists provide symptomatic relief, ultrasound-guided percutaneous cryoablation under local anesthesia emerges as a promising minimally invasive alternative. Cryoablation induces targeted tissue necrosis through ultra-low temperature exposure that triggers intracellular ice crystal formation and microvascular thrombosis, while its cryogenic effect provides additional nerve blockade for pain relief. In clinical application, percutaneous cryoablation achieved complete dysmenorrhea relief in treated patients with marked reduction in pain scores and uterine volume, demonstrating safety and efficacy in short-term follow-up. This technique expands the treatment armamentarium beyond established methods such as uterine arterial embolization, high-intensity focused ultrasound, microwave ablation, and radiofrequency ablation, offering patients a tissue-preserving option that maintains uterine function while effectively managing adenomyosis-associated symptoms.

title: The Clinical Significance of Placental Site Nodule and Plaque in Women with Secondary Infertility Treated with Office Hysteroscopic Surgery: The Case Series of Retrospective Cohort Study
summary: Placental site nodule and plaque (PSNP) is a gestational trophoblastic lesion localized to the endometrium that is strongly associated with secondary infertility and chronic endometritis in women of reproductive age. PSNP appears hysteroscopically as yellow-white colored protuberant lesions, micropolyps, and stalkless polyps that can be diagnosed via mini-hysteroscopy with targeted biopsy and endometrial aspiration. The lesions trigger inflammatory reactions characterized by CD138-positive plasma cell infiltration around PSNP nodules, which may provoke secondary chronic endometritis and lead to implantation failure. Hysteroscopic resection of PSNP lesions combined with doxycycline treatment for concurrent chronic endometritis effectively restores fertility, with all seven patients in this case series achieving pregnancy following treatment. These findings suggest that PSNP detection and resection should be considered in the diagnostic and therapeutic evaluation of women with secondary infertility and recurrent implantation failure.

title: Removal of FIGO V and VI fibroids with a combined size greater than 5 cm quadruples spontaneous fecundity relative to myomectomy for those with smaller fibroids
summary: Uterine fibroids contribute to infertility through multiple mechanisms including mechanical distortion of the uterine cavity, alterations in angiogenic factors and endometrial lining, and inflammation. While submucosal fibroids (FIGO 0-III) are most strongly associated with infertility, this study demonstrates that larger subserosal fibroids (FIGO V-VI) with a combined diameter of 5 cm or greater also significantly impact fertility. Myomectomy (surgical removal) of these larger fibroids quadrupled spontaneous pregnancy rates compared to removal of smaller fibroids, with 52.3% of women with fibroids ≥5 cm achieving clinical pregnancy versus only 13.3% with smaller fibroids. Patient age and total fibroid size emerged as independent predictors of post-operative pregnancy success, while BMI, infertility type, and maximum individual fibroid size did not affect outcomes. The findings suggest that surgical intervention should be considered for women with combined fibroid diameters ≥5 cm seeking fertility restoration, as nearly 50% of infertile women aged 18-40 years achieved spontaneous conception within 24 months following myomectomy.

title: Gynecologic postoperative anti-adhesion barriers: From biomaterials to barrier development
summary:
# Summary

Postoperative adhesions (POA) affect up to 90% of women following gynecologic surgery and contribute to major reproductive complications including secondary infertility (20–40% of cases), chronic pain, bowel obstruction, and placenta accreta spectrum. These adhesions form through peritoneal trauma from surgical incisions, tissue ischemia, and desiccation, which trigger histamine release, increased vascular permeability, and fibrin deposition—a process involving fibroblast activation and myofibroblast differentiation. Current FDA-cleared barrier films such as INTERCEED, Seprafilm, and SurgiWrap provide limited protection, creating demand for advanced biomaterial solutions. Emerging anti-adhesion barriers incorporate biocompatible, degradable materials including collagen, hyaluronic acid, polylactic acid, polyglycolic acid, and polycaprolactone, fabricated using electrospinning, 3D bioprinting, and Janus microneedle patches to create drug-eluting systems. These innovative approaches employ nanoengineering to modulate myofibroblast activity, reduce reactive oxygen species, and inhibit ERK-2 signaling pathways involved in scar formation. Additionally, laparoscopic surgery with warmed, humidified CO₂ supplemented with N₂O and O₂ reduces adhesion formation by 50% compared to open surgery, representing a complementary mechanistic strategy to physical barrier development.

title: Analysis of Efficacy and Safety Profile of Vaginal Surgery and Hysteroscopic Electric Resection in the Management of Post-Cesarean Section Isthmocele: a Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Isthmoceles are cesarean scar defects that have increased in prevalence alongside rising cesarean section rates and improved diagnostic capabilities via transvaginal ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging. These defects significantly impact women's reproductive health and quality of life, necessitating effective treatment options. This systematic review compared two surgical approaches—vaginal surgery and hysteroscopic electric resection—for managing post-cesarean isthmoceles. Hysteroscopic electric resection demonstrated superior safety and efficacy compared to vaginal surgery, with a pooled risk ratio of 0.7 favoring the hysteroscopic approach and notably better diverticulum recovery outcomes. The findings support hysteroscopic electric resection as the preferred method for treating post-cesarean isthmoceles, offering patients reduced complications and improved treatment success rates.

title: Evaluating treatment options for symptomatic uterine fibroids: a systematic review and meta-analysis of effectiveness, recovery, and long-term outcomes (MARIE WP1)
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids are a significant gynecological condition that causes heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pain and pressure, infertility, and iron deficiency anemia, substantially impairing patients' quality of life. Multiple treatment modalities are available, ranging from pharmacological approaches such as GnRH agonists, oral contraceptives, and progesterone-releasing intrauterine devices to surgical and minimally invasive interventions including myomectomy, hysterectomy, uterine artery embolization (UAE), and MRI-guided focused ultrasound. Myomectomy demonstrates superior efficacy in reducing symptom severity and improving mental health compared to UAE, while both procedures comparably improve overall health-related quality of life, though myomectomy and hysterectomy show greater mental health benefits. Complication profiles differ markedly among interventions: UAE carries 84% lower hemorrhage risk than surgical alternatives but is associated with higher rates of vaginal discharge, menstrual irregularities, and menopausal symptoms due to embolization-induced tissue ischemia; conversely, myomectomy and hysterectomy carry risks of urinary retention and infection. Notably, Black and African American women experience disproportionately high fibroid prevalence and require more aggressive surgical intervention, with a 2.4-fold increased likelihood of hysterectomy or repeated myomectomies compared to other populations.

title: Clinical efficacy analysis of LNG-IUS tail anchoring to the superficial myometrium under hysteroscopic direct vision
summary: The levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) effectively treats multiple gynecological conditions including menorrhagia, dysmenorrhea, adenomyosis, and endometriosis. However, LNG-IUS expulsion rates are significantly elevated in patients with adenomyosis due to enlarged uterine cavities, abnormal myometrial contractility, and heavy menstrual bleeding. Hysteroscopic anchoring surgery, particularly using a long-tail anchoring technique to the superficial myometrium, substantially reduces LNG-IUS re-expulsion rates from 43.40% in standard placement to 18.87% in anchored cases, with long-tail anchoring achieving only 6.90% re-expulsion compared to 28.77% with short-tail anchoring. Pre-treatment with uterine volume-reducing interventions such as high-intensity focused ultrasound or gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists may improve outcomes. Critically, anchoring surgery does not increase complications such as uterine perforation or infection, making it a safe and highly efficacious approach for patients with previous LNG-IUS expulsion or challenging anatomical factors.

title: A Levonorgestrel-Releasing Intrauterine Device in the Right Upper Quadrant: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a rare but serious complication of levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device (IUD) placement: uterine perforation with subsequent migration to the omentum near the hepatic flexure. Uterine perforation following IUD insertion can result from multiple etiological factors, including poor insertion technique, difficult placement due to uterine anatomy or pathology, progressive uterine wall erosion, and timing of interval placement, with pregnancy representing a known additional risk factor. The perforation led to clinically significant sequelae including abdominal and pelvic pain that prompted surgical intervention via laparoscopy. While levonorgestrel-releasing IUDs offer effective contraception and have been associated with benefits in endometriosis management, this case highlights the importance of recognizing IUD perforation as an uncommon yet potentially serious adverse outcome that can be missed or delayed in diagnosis, particularly when devices migrate to intra-abdominal locations remote from the uterus.

title: See to Believe: The Clinical Gaps in the Utility of Ultrasound Imaging in Screening for Ovarian Torsion
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian torsion is a gynecologic emergency in which twisting of the infundibulopelvic ligament causes venous obstruction followed by arterial compromise, leading to ischemia, swelling, necrosis, infarction, hemorrhage, and peritonitis that can result in permanent loss of ovarian function. While ultrasound with Doppler is the first-line imaging modality and certain ultrasound signs such as the whirlpool sign and follicular ring sign are associated with torsion, the sensitivity of these imaging findings remains unreliable due to the dual blood supply of the ovaries, creating significant diagnostic gaps. Conditions including polycystic ovary syndrome, premenopausal status, ovarian cysts, and endometriosis-related endometriomas substantially increase torsion risk, and endometriomas in particular frequently mimic torsion on imaging. Supplementary imaging with MRI or CT scans improves diagnostic accuracy and can prevent unnecessary surgical interventions, as illustrated by clinical cases where endometriomas were initially misdiagnosed as torsion. Surgical intervention remains the definitive management, ranging from detorsion to salpingo-oophorectomy in cases of ovarian necrosis, and emerging machine learning approaches show promise for enhancing detection accuracy and guiding clinical decision-making in suspected cases.

title: Shock From Hemorrhagic Ascites in a Non-cirrhotic Patient With Isolated High-Output Right Heart Failure
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a 49-year-old patient with end-stage renal disease and multiple comorbidities who presented with hemorrhagic ascites and hemorrhagic shock caused by high-output right heart failure with precapillary pulmonary hypertension. Diagnostic workup revealed elevated cardiac output (8.3 L/minute) and cardiac index (4.2 L/minute/m²) concurrent with severe pulmonary hypertension (mean pulmonary artery pressure 54 mmHg), right atrial and ventricular dilation, moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and markedly elevated B-type natriuretic peptide levels. The hemorrhagic ascites resulted from intra-abdominal bleeding and fluid leakage from hepatic interstitial spaces secondary to the high-output cardiac state and right-sided pressure overload. Treatment involved meticulous volume management through diuretic therapy, dialysis, and therapeutic paracentesis; notably, the patient's recurrent presentations were attributed to non-compliance with heart failure medications and missed hemodialysis sessions. This case illustrates a rare but serious presentation of hemorrhagic ascites in a non-cirrhotic patient with cardiac etiology, highlighting the importance of volume management adherence in preventing recurrent complications.

title: Substitution of surgical care within benign gynaecology during COVID-19: waste of a good crisis? – a quantitative longitudinal study in the Netherlands
summary:
# Summary

The COVID-19 pandemic caused substantial disruption to benign gynaecological care in the Netherlands, with surgical procedures declining by 18.3% and non-surgical procedures declining by 6% in 2020. Infertility treatment experienced the largest surgical decline at 19.3%, followed by heavy menstrual bleeding, prolapse, endometriosis, and uterine fibroids at 16.9-17.5%. While non-surgical care recovered to predicted levels by mid-2020, surgical care remained below expected trends throughout 2020 and 2021. Multiple factors contributed to sustained reductions including staff redeployment and absences, reduced patient demand (particularly among older individuals hesitant about COVID-19 exposure), and organisational capacity constraints. Critically, the reduction in surgical procedures did not correspond with increased use of non-surgical alternatives, suggesting that many patients deferred or abandoned treatment entirely rather than substituting with less invasive options. Gynaecologist preferences for surgical procedures as the gold standard and patient perceptions of non-surgical options as temporary rather than definitive further hindered this potential substitution, representing a missed opportunity to optimize resource allocation during the crisis.

title: Thinking outside the box: Axillary to superior mesenteric artery bypass for chronic mesenteric ischemia
journal: Journal of Vascular Surgery Cases, Innovations and Techniques
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes the successful surgical management of chronic mesenteric ischemia caused by superior mesenteric artery stenosis using an axillary to superior mesenteric artery bypass. The patient presented with classic symptoms of chronic mesenteric ischemia including weight loss, food fear, persistent nausea, emesis, and postprandial abdominal pain. Following the axillary-to-SMA bypass procedure, the patient achieved complete resolution of these gastrointestinal symptoms, including elimination of abdominal pain and nausea with eating. While traditional open approaches such as antegrade aortomesenteric bypass and endovascular treatments are commonly employed to treat mesenteric ischemia, this case demonstrates an alternative bypass strategy from the axillary artery that may be valuable in select patient populations. The patient demonstrated excellent postoperative recovery with well-healing incisions and sustained symptom resolution at 90-day follow-up, suggesting the axillary-to-SMA bypass is a viable surgical option for revascularization in chronic mesenteric ischemia.

title: A case of anti-N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor encephalitis with associated ovarian teratoma in a 23-year-old female
summary: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is an immune-mediated neuropsychiatric disease characterized by the presence of autoantibodies against the GluN1 subunit of the NMDA receptor, which can be detected in cerebrospinal fluid and are strongly associated with underlying ovarian teratomas in young females. The condition manifests with a complex constellation of neurological and psychiatric symptoms including memory loss, confusion, psychosis, seizures, behavioral changes, movement disorders, and potentially life-threatening complications such as hypoventilation and coma if left untreated. Management requires a multidisciplinary approach combining immunotherapy—including intravenous immunoglobulin, glucocorticoids, and monoclonal antibodies such as rituximab and tocilizumab—with surgical removal of the associated teratoma, which significantly improves outcomes and recovery time. Early detection through comprehensive radiological imaging (CT and MRI) is critical for identifying the underlying tumor and enabling prompt treatment, as the condition is highly responsive to therapy when diagnosed early. This case demonstrates that clinicians should maintain high clinical suspicion for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis in young females presenting with new-onset neuropsychiatric symptoms and cognitive decline, particularly when high levels of anti-NMDA antibodies are identified.

title: Spatial, temporal and demographic distribution characteristics of adenomyosis symptom clusters from the perspective of traditional Chinese medicine: a multicenter cross-sectional study in China from 2020 to 2022
summary: This multicenter cross-sectional study characterizes adenomyosis symptom clusters according to traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) frameworks across spatial, temporal, and demographic dimensions in China. Adenomyosis, involving abnormal growth of endometrial glands and stroma into the myometrium, presents with dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, which TCM conceptualizes through distinct syndrome patterns including Blood Stasis Syndrome, Qi Deficiency, Cold Coagulation, and Liver-Qi Stagnation. Geographic variation emerged prominently: high-latitude regions exhibited Cold Coagulation syndrome (marked by cold intolerance and cold-type abdominal pain), while low-latitude regions showed Spleen-Qi Deficiency and Blood Stasis (characterized by fatigue and menstrual clots). Seasonally, cold seasons reinforced Cold Coagulation symptoms, whereas warm seasons featured Dampness patterns with increased sweating. Demographic differences revealed younger patients predominantly presented with Cold Coagulation and Blood Stasis with Liver-Qi Stagnation (cold intolerance and irritability), while older patients displayed Qi Deficiency and Blood Stasis with menorrhagia and fatigue, reflecting their distinct treatment goals of assisted pregnancy versus menstrual flow reduction. TCM treatment strategies were matched to syndrome patterns, employing warming Yang and cold-dispersing therapies for Cold Coagulation, spleen-invigorating approaches for Qi Deficiency, and combined modalities for complex patterns, thereby providing a systematic framework for personalizing adenomyosis management.

title: Development and validation of a nomogram-based predictive model for recurrence risk of uterine leiomyoma after myomectomy
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids frequently recur after myomectomy, with recurrence rates varying significantly by surgical approach—laparoscopic techniques show rates exceeding 76% at eight years compared to lower rates with open myomectomy and hysteroscopic resection. A predictive nomogram identified six key independent risk factors for postoperative fibroid recurrence: postoperative residual fibroids (OR = 10.746), postoperative pregnancy or childbirth (OR = 4.121), family history reflecting genetic predisposition (OR = 2.045), and fibroid characteristics including larger diameter and non-submucosal location. Notably, submucosal fibroids and smaller diameter fibroids (≤4 cm) were protective factors, with hysteroscopic resection of submucosal fibroids achieving recurrence rates as low as 0.9% at two years. Fibroid recurrence leads to significant clinical consequences including menstrual irregularities, chronic pelvic pain, bulk-related symptoms, and potential infertility in severe cases. Patient age, hormonal levels, and reproductive history also contribute to recurrence risk, suggesting that personalized risk stratification using this nomogram can guide surgical planning and postoperative management strategies to improve reproductive outcomes.

title: Laparoscopic multi-bipolar radiofrequency ablation of fibroids: impact on quality of life
summary: Uterine fibroids cause significant morbidity in approximately 30% of symptomatic women, presenting with menometrorrhagia, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility, and chronic pelvic pain that substantially reduces quality of life. Laparoscopic radiofrequency myolysis treats fibroids by inducing fibroid necrosis and demonstrates efficacy in reducing fibroid volume and alleviating associated symptoms including heavy menstrual bleeding, cycle irregularity, abdominal distension, and urinary frequency. Compared to traditional myomectomy, radiofrequency myolysis offers significant procedural advantages, including shorter operative duration (94.8 versus 194.4 minutes), reduced blood loss, and shorter hospital stays, while myomectomy carries substantially higher risks of hemorrhage, acute anemia, and postoperative adhesions. Both procedures improved quality of life and reduced fibroid-related symptoms at 3 and 6 months follow-up with comparable clinical efficacy. Radiofrequency myolysis demonstrated a superior safety profile with lower peri- and postoperative complication rates, establishing it as a potentially valuable conservative treatment option for uterine fibroids and other myometrial disorders such as adenomyosis, though rare serious complications including rectal fistula and stercoral peritonitis require careful patient selection and monitoring.

title: Improved surgical outcomes in adenomyosis: a novel insight into the superiority of secretory phase adenomyomectomy
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis, a benign gynecological disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue in the myometrium, causes dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, and subfertility. While adenomyomectomy is an effective uterus-sparing surgical treatment, postoperative recurrence remains a significant clinical challenge. This study demonstrates that performing adenomyomectomy during the secretory phase of the menstrual cycle substantially improves long-term outcomes compared to surgery during the proliferative phase. During the secretory phase, progesterone induces glandular dilation, stromal edema, and enhanced vascularization while simultaneously reducing endometrial cell proliferation and placing endometrial stem cells in relative quiescence—all factors that facilitate complete lesion excision and reduce regeneration potential. The secretory phase group exhibited a significantly lower recurrence rate (14.3% versus 38.8%) and longer recurrence-free survival, with surgical timing remaining an independent protective factor against recurrence in multivariate analysis. These findings establish surgical timing within the menstrual cycle as a previously overlooked yet critical determinant of adenomyomectomy efficacy, offering a simple clinical strategy to enhance treatment success rates.

title: Risk factors for late presentation of symptomatic uterine fibroid in urban Nigeria: A mixed method study
summary:
# Summary

This mixed-methods study in urban Nigeria identified multiple risk factors for late presentation of symptomatic uterine fibroids, a condition characterized by heavy menstruation, severe anemia, pelvic pain, and reproductive complications including subfertility, miscarriage, and increased cesarean delivery risk. Key demographic and biological predictors of late presentation included age ≥40 years, nulliparity, and genetic factors such as MED12 mutations, with women who were nulliparous and older being significantly more likely to delay seeking treatment. Qualitatively, three major barriers emerged: inadequate knowledge of the condition, aversion to surgery rooted in fear of visible scarring and cultural beliefs, and financial constraints preventing access to surgical interventions including myomectomy, hysterectomy, and uterine artery embolization. Notably, women experiencing subfertility or infertility were 14 times less likely to present late, suggesting reproductive concerns motivate earlier care-seeking. Broader systemic barriers in sub-Saharan Africa, including lack of access to adequate healthcare facilities, insufficient trained providers, and poor service quality, further contributed to delayed diagnosis and treatment. The study underscores how cultural silence surrounding fibroids, religious beliefs, and reliance on home remedies compound the challenge of timely intervention in this population.

title: Abstracts for NAPCON 2025
journal: Lung India : Official Organ of Indian Chest Society
summary:
# Summary

This collection of abstracts from NAPCON 2025 covers multiple respiratory and pulmonary pathologies with distinct clinical and environmental associations. Anti-inflammatory relievers combining inhaled corticosteroids with fast-acting bronchodilators, particularly budesonide-formoterol combinations, effectively reduce asthma exacerbations. Environmental and household factors significantly influence respiratory health, with indoor air pollutants including dhoop, agarbatti, loban, and mosquito coils, along with biomass fuel use and passive smoking, all strongly associated with respiratory symptoms, while protective factors such as separate kitchens and windows reduce symptom burden. Comorbidities including anemia, hypertension, diabetes, and obesity are significantly linked to respiratory symptoms. The abstracts further detail vehicular pollution-induced lung function impairment in traffic police personnel, presenting with early spirometric changes (PRISM pattern), and discuss rare genetic conditions like Kartagener's syndrome featuring defective ciliary movement leading to situs inversus, chronic sinusitis, and bronchiectasis. Additionally, small airways disease occurs in up to 50% of asthmatics and requires detection through spirometry and lung oscillometry, while lung cancer mutation analysis reveals EGFR mutations as the most prevalent (29.58%), followed by TP53 and KRAS, predominantly in adenocarcinoma subtypes. Glycopyrronium nebulization offers improved bronchodilation for COPD and asthma exacerbations, with FEF25-75 showing superior sensitivity for early small-airway dysfunction detection.

title: Intravenous Ferric Carboxymaltose for the Treatment of Iron Deficiency Anemia During Pregnancy: Effects on Maternal and Fetal Wellbeing—A Multicenter Retrospective Observational Study
summary:
# Summary

Iron deficiency anemia affects approximately 19% of pregnant women and is associated with severe maternal and fetal complications including preeclampsia, preterm birth, fetal growth restriction, and low birth weight if left untreated. This multicenter study evaluated ferric carboxymaltose (FCM), an intravenous iron formulation, as treatment for moderate to severe iron deficiency anemia in 487 pregnancies. Intravenous FCM proved more effective than oral iron formulations, increasing hemoglobin concentrations by a median of 1.4 g/dL within five weeks while demonstrating a favorable safety profile with minimal adverse effects (primarily nausea, vomiting, and hypotension in only 1.4% of cases) and low immunogenic potential. Fetal wellbeing monitoring via computerized cardiotocography revealed normal parameters in 98.4% of cases, with transient fetal heart rate variability abnormalities in six patients that resolved following intrauterine resuscitation. The findings establish FCM as the preferred intravenous iron agent during pregnancy, effectively correcting anemia and improving maternal symptoms such as fatigue while maintaining fetal safety, though emerging concerns regarding hypophosphatemia warrant monitoring and consideration of alternative formulations like ferric derisomaltose.

title: Pregnancy outcomes following fluoroscopy-guided tubal recanalization: a comparison of spontaneous conception and intrauterine insemination—a retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

Tubal factor infertility, accounting for 11–67% of infertility diagnoses, arises from fallopian tube blockages caused by infection, pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, or prior surgical interventions. Fluoroscopy-guided tubal recanalization (FGTR) is a minimally invasive procedure that effectively treats tubal occlusion and improves natural conception prospects. The study found that clinical pregnancy rates were similar between spontaneous conception (51.2%) and intrauterine insemination (57.6%) in women achieving tubal patency after FGTR. However, the location and extent of obstruction critically determined outcomes: proximal occlusions showed favorable results with high bilateral patency rates (50%) and pregnancy rates (45–47%), whereas bilateral distal occlusions resulted in no pregnancies due to irreversible structural damage, fibrosis, and ciliary loss. Unilateral tubal obstruction yielded relatively high pregnancy rates through compensatory function of the contralateral patent tube. These findings demonstrate that FGTR offers a fertility-restoring alternative to assisted reproductive technology for proximal obstruction, though its efficacy is substantially limited by distal disease pathology.

title: Uncommon Sequela of Miscarriage: A Case of Hematometra
summary: Hematometra, an abnormal accumulation of blood within the uterus, typically results from cervical stenosis that obstructs normal menstrual outflow. Cervical stenosis can arise from congenital anomalies or be acquired through uterine procedures including dilation and curettage, suction evacuation, cesarean section, and laparoscopic myomectomy, as well as from post-surgical inflammation, infection, and surgical trauma. Patients with hematometra present with characteristic symptoms including amenorrhea, pelvic pain, rectal tenesmus, and urinary retention, while laboratory findings may show elevated C-reactive protein. If untreated, hematometra can progress to serious infectious complications including pyometra, peritonitis, endometritis, pelvic inflammatory disease, and sepsis. Transvaginal ultrasonography is critical for diagnosis, enabling visualization of blood accumulation within the uterine cavity. Prompt surgical management through uterine evacuation and hysteroscopic cervical dilation effectively treats hematometra and prevents life-threatening complications.

title: Following Fibroids: An Analysis of Social Media Narratives
journal: JSLS : Journal of the Society of Laparoscopic & Robotic Surgeons
summary: Patients with uterine fibroids increasingly rely on social media platforms for health information, yet these platforms predominantly feature low-quality content that promotes misconceptions about fibroid management. Analysis of fibroid-related social media narratives reveals that alternative remedies are discussed most frequently (46% of content), while evidence-based surgical and minimally invasive procedures receive substantially less attention (29%), potentially misleading patients about treatment options. TikTok demonstrated the highest engagement metrics, with posts reaching hundreds of thousands of views, amplifying the reach of often inaccurate information. This gap between patient information-seeking behaviors on social media and the scarcity of high-quality, medically accurate fibroid content underscores the critical need for physicians to develop strategies addressing these misconceptions and providing evidence-based patient education. The findings highlight that healthcare providers must be aware of social media narratives to effectively deliver patient-centered care and counter misinformation that may delay appropriate clinical treatment.

title: A Case of Ectopic Spleen Transplanted Into the Sigmoid Colon and Pelvic Peritoneum
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes ectopic spleen (ES), a rare complication occurring decades after splenectomy, where residual splenic tissue becomes dispersed and reimplanted in the abdomen and pelvis. The formation of ectopic spleen involves three main pathophysiological mechanisms: dissemination of splenic tissue fragments, hematogenous circulation of splenic myeloid cells, and dispersion through abdominal cavity lavage, with proliferation regulated by local microenvironments and circulating growth factors. Pelvic ectopic spleen can be clinically confused with gynecologic pathologies including endometriosis and ovarian tumors, presenting with lower abdominal pain that may develop years after the initial surgical injury. Diagnostic imaging—including arterial phase CT enhancement, T2-weighted MRI signal characteristics, technetium nuclear scans, and contrast-enhanced ultrasound—can identify ectopic splenic tissue, though laparoscopic exploration remains the preferred diagnostic and therapeutic approach. Although ectopic splenic tissue retains partial immune function, it grows slowly and may eventually cause symptoms from mass effect, torsion, or bleeding, warranting surgical intervention and post-procedural vaccination against infections for long-term patient protection.

title: Chromosomal Abnormalities in Couples Experiencing Recurrent Implantation Failure in West of Iran: A Case–Control Study
summary: This case-control study from West Iran demonstrates that chromosomal abnormalities significantly contribute to recurrent implantation failure (RIF), with translocations representing the predominant structural abnormality identified in affected couples. Robertsonian and balanced translocations are particularly associated with male infertility and spermatogenic dysfunction, while pericentric inversions in both sexes can trigger abnormal meiotic events leading to infertility and miscarriage. These chromosomal aberrations interfere with genes essential for embryo development and implantation, ultimately increasing abortion rates in assisted reproductive technology cycles. The study emphasizes the critical importance of cytogenetic screening for both partners prior to fertility treatment, as early detection can guide clinical management. Preimplantation genetic diagnosis and genetic counseling are recommended interventions that can prevent transmission of chromosomal abnormalities and facilitate informed reproductive decision-making for couples experiencing RIF.

title: Endometrial Carcinoma in a 26-Year-Old Obese Woman With Polycystic Ovary Syndrome: A Case Report
summary: This case report documents endometrial carcinoma in an unusually young 26-year-old obese woman with polycystic ovary syndrome, highlighting the underrecognized risk of this malignancy in younger populations. Multiple metabolic and endocrine factors converged in this patient to promote endometrial cancer development, including obesity, PCOS, insulin resistance, smoking, and early menarche, all of which increase estrogen exposure and endometrial proliferation. The patient presented with oligomenorrhea—a symptom often misattributed to benign conditions like PCOS or hormonal imbalance—which delayed diagnosis; however, transvaginal ultrasound revealed a heterogeneous endometrium measuring 17.0 mm, prompting hysteroscopy and dilation and curettage that confirmed well-differentiated endometrioid adenocarcinoma. Immunohistochemical testing for mismatch repair proteins (MLH-1, PMS-2, MSH-2, and MSH-6) ruled out Lynch syndrome as an underlying predisposition factor. The patient underwent laparoscopic total hysterectomy, and though adjuvant chemotherapy or radiotherapy may be considered based on staging, this case underscores the critical importance of investigating abnormal uterine bleeding and menstrual irregularities in young women with metabolic syndrome and PCOS, as endometrial cancer can occur decades earlier than typically expected.

title: Nursing care in primary health care to tackle antimicrobial resistance in pregnant women with urinary tract infections
journal: Revista da Escola de Enfermagem da USP
summary: Urinary tract infections affect 5-15% of pregnant women and pose significant risks to both mother and fetus due to anatomical, hormonal, and physiological changes during pregnancy that increase susceptibility to infection. Untreated asymptomatic bacteriuria can progress to serious complications including pyelonephritis, preterm labor, intrauterine growth restriction, low birth weight, and perinatal death, as well as postpartum endometriosis, eclampsia, anemia, and hypertension. The primary causative organism is Escherichia coli, typically treated with cephalexin, nitrofurantoin, or fosfomycin, though antimicrobial resistance—driven by inappropriate antibiotic use and self-medication—threatens treatment efficacy. Early diagnosis through urine culture and antibiogram testing is critical for preventing complications, while delays in diagnosis correlate with increased maternal and neonatal morbidity. Nurses in primary health care play a vital role in combating antimicrobial resistance through antimicrobial stewardship programs, including ensuring bacteriological cultures before treatment initiation, educating patients on proper medication use, and monitoring treatment adherence to optimize pregnancy outcomes.

title: Late post-partum hemorrhage revealing a delayed uterine rupture in an unscarred uterus: a case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary: This case report documents a rare presentation of delayed uterine rupture in an unscarred uterus manifesting as late postpartum hemorrhage. The primary risk factors for rupture in intact uteri include grand multiparity, mechanical dystocia, prolonged labor, cephalopelvic disproportion, fetal malpresentation, and use of uterotonic agents such as oxytocin, misoprostol, and prostaglandins, with additional risk factors including uterine malformations, arteriovenous malformations, uterine diverticula, and endometriosis. Uterine rupture represents a critical obstetric emergency strongly associated with severe maternal morbidity and mortality, typically presenting with postpartum hemorrhage, hemodynamic shock, pelvic pain, abdominal pain, and maternal hypotension. The patient's clinical course culminated in disseminated intravascular coagulation evidenced by decreased hemoglobin, thrombocytopenia, and severe hypofibrinogenemia, ultimately requiring hemostatic hysterectomy as definitive treatment. Diagnosis typically depends on uterine examination following postpartum hemorrhage, with imaging potentially revealing peritoneal effusion; therefore, high clinical suspicion is essential in any postpartum patient presenting with hemorrhage and signs of shock to prevent delayed intervention and reduce maternal morbidity.

title: Sepsis following hysteroscopic removal of retained products of conception: A case report and literature review
summary:
# Summary

Retained products of conception (RPOC) represent a significant risk factor for serious postoperative complications following hysteroscopic removal, including sepsis, postpartum hemorrhage, and intrauterine adhesions. While hysteroscopy is superior to traditional blind curettage in reducing complications and improving subsequent pregnancy outcomes, this case report documents a rare but severe instance where hysteroscopic RPOC resection led to sepsis caused by multiple bacterial pathogens including *Escherichia coli*, *Streptococcus* species, and *Klebsiella pneumoniae*. The pathogenic mechanism involves inflammatory infiltrates with CD138-positive plasma cells within retained tissue, combined with hysteroscopic distension pressure that facilitates translocation of inflammatory cells and pathogens into the bloodstream, particularly in patients with concurrent pathologies such as endometriosis or adenomyosis. Clinicians should maintain heightened suspicion for sepsis when postoperative fever coincides with elevated procalcitonin, leukocytosis, and elevated C-reactive protein, and should employ risk stratification using qSOFA scores for early detection. Prompt empirical broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy and fluid resuscitation are essential interventions, though optimal infection prevention strategies and identification of additional risk factors warrant further investigation.

title: Gastroesophageal reflux disease and risk of laryngeal cancer: A Mendelian randomization study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study investigated the causal relationship between gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and laryngeal cancer risk. While observational epidemiological studies have consistently identified associations between GERD and laryngeal cancer, as well as other malignancies of the upper aerodigestive tract including esophageal and oropharyngeal cancers, the genetic analysis found no significant causal effect of GERD on laryngeal cancer development, nor reverse causality from laryngeal cancer to GERD. The proposed mechanistic pathway involves pepsin-induced damage to the laryngeal epithelium and subsequent chronic inflammation leading to malignant transformation, with Helicobacter pylori infection potentially contributing through systemic inflammatory responses. Established risk factors including tobacco use and alcohol consumption demonstrated strong causal associations with laryngeal cancer and likely confound the observed GERD-cancer relationship. Anti-reflux procedures have been shown to reduce laryngeal squamous cell carcinoma risk in GERD populations, suggesting symptomatic management may provide clinical benefit despite the lack of genetically-determined causality.

title: Ovarian Hemorrhage as a Complication of Venovenous Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation
summary: This case report describes a rare but serious complication of venovenous extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO): gynecologic hemorrhage from a ruptured ovarian cyst. A 23-year-old woman with cystic fibrosis developed severe pneumonia secondary to multiple bacterial pathogens, requiring ECMO support for acute respiratory failure. The high shear stress within the ECMO circuit combined with anticoagulation therapy (unfractionated heparin) impaired hemostasis through loss of von Willebrand factor multimers, thrombocytopenia, and hypofibrinogenemia, predisposing her to spontaneous bleeding. She subsequently developed catastrophic hemoperitoneum from ovarian hemorrhage, requiring right ovarian artery particle embolization to control bleeding, though she later developed multiple additional complications including disseminated intravascular coagulation, acute tubular necrosis, and abdominal compartment syndrome requiring surgical intervention. The case highlights that while ECMO-associated hemorrhage is typically managed with transfusion and anticoagulation adjustment, gynecologic bleeding specifically may warrant consideration of hormonal therapies such as depot medroxyprogesterone or leuprorelin, which reduce thrombotic risk better than oral contraceptives in this high-risk population.

title: Post-procedural infection risk following laser vaginal rejuvenation: clinical experience from a single center
summary:
# Summary

Genitourinary syndrome of menopause and vulvovaginal atrophy represent significant clinical conditions that cause vaginal dryness, irritation, dyspareunia, and urinary incontinence, substantially impacting women's quality of life. Laser vaginal rejuvenation has emerged as a minimally invasive therapeutic option that improves vaginal tissue health and effectively treats vaginal laxity, urinary incontinence, and vaginal discomfort, with vaginal laxity being the most common indication for treatment at 69.8% of cases. This single-center clinical experience demonstrates that laser vaginal rejuvenation procedures are well-tolerated, with post-procedure adverse effects limited to transient discomfort and minor, self-limiting bleeding in a small number of patients. The safety profile and favorable patient outcomes support laser vaginal rejuvenation as a viable therapeutic approach for managing genitourinary symptoms of menopause and related vaginal pathology in reproductive health.

title: Temporal Trends and Machine Learning-Based Risk Prediction of Female Infertility: A Cross-Cohort Analysis Using NHANES Data (2015–2023)
summary: This cross-cohort analysis of NHANES data (2015–2023) identified menstrual irregularity as the strongest predictor of female infertility, alongside multiple pathogenic mechanisms including ovulatory dysfunction, tubal obstruction, endometriosis, pelvic infections, and age-related ovarian decline. Prior childbirth and total number of deliveries emerged as highly protective factors, with each additional delivery substantially reducing infertility risk. Notably, infertility prevalence nearly doubled from 14.8% (2017–2018) to 27.8% (2021–2023), coinciding with the COVID-19 pandemic, likely due to disrupted healthcare access, psychosocial stress, and delayed reproductive decision-making. Six machine learning models (logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, naïve Bayes, support vector machine, and stacking classifier) all demonstrated excellent predictive performance (AUC >0.96) for infertility classification. The temporal trend shows increasing menstrual irregularity (65.9% in 2021–2023 versus 46.1% in 2015–2016) alongside declining pregnancy rates and fewer total deliveries in the most recent cohort, underscoring emerging reproductive health challenges in the post-pandemic period.

title: Thyroid-stimulating Hormone Levels in Women with Uterine Fibroids: A Healthcare Setting Perspective from Coastal Karnataka
journal: Indian Journal of Community Medicine: Official Publication of Indian Association of Preventive & Social Medicine
summary: This study from coastal Karnataka establishes a significant association between thyroid dysfunction and uterine fibroids (UF), with elevated TSH levels detected in 40% of women with fibroids compared to 31% without fibroids. The research demonstrates that thyroid abnormalities, including goiter, thyroid nodules, and overt hypothyroidism, are more prevalent in women with UF, likely mediated by estrogen and progesterone influences on thyroid function and TSH production. Women with fibroids who had elevated TSH levels exhibited higher rates of menstrual irregularities, abdominal and back pain, and more severe symptoms including heavy menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and anemia. The study found that approximately 79% of symptomatic fibroid patients presented with multiple intramural fibroids alongside various menstrual disturbances and thyroid dysfunction. These findings suggest that thyroid hormone dysregulation may contribute to symptom severity in UF patients, indicating that thyroid assessment and correction could represent an important therapeutic target for improving disease management and reducing clinical manifestations in women with uterine fibroids.

title: Management of uterine artery pseudoaneurysm: a case report of noninterventional treatment with systematic review
summary:
# Uterine Artery Pseudoaneurysm: Management and Clinical Outcomes

Uterine artery pseudoaneurysm (UAP) is a rare but potentially life-threatening vascular condition that accounts for 0.3–1% of postpartum hemorrhage cases and most commonly results from uterine trauma sustained during cesarean section, vaginal delivery, myomectomy, hysterectomy, or dilation and curettage, with additional risk factors including previous uterine surgeries, infections, connective tissue disorders, and coagulopathies. UAP manifests differently depending on pregnancy status: pregnant patients typically present with abdominal pain (85%), while nonpregnant patients more commonly present with vaginal bleeding (81.5%), both potentially causing severe hemorrhage and maternal complications. Doppler ultrasonography serves as the primary diagnostic modality, supplemented by MRI and angiography to confirm diagnosis and assess lesion perfusion status. While selective arterial embolization remains the standard treatment with high efficacy (90–99% of cases), this case report and systematic review demonstrate that noninterventional management with conservative observation and spontaneous thrombosis may be a viable alternative in hemodynamically stable, pregnant patients with imaging evidence of thrombosis and no clinical progression. The authors emphasize that UAP should be considered in the differential diagnosis of atypical abdominal pain during pregnancy and advocate for multidisciplinary management to optimize both maternal and fetal outcomes.

title: A case of HDR syndrome with recurrent matured ovarian teratomas
summary: HDR syndrome is a rare genetic disorder caused by mutations in the GATA3 gene or deletions in chromosome 10p14, which disrupt development of the parathyroid glands, inner ear, and kidneys. The condition is characterized by a core triad of hypoparathyroidism (affecting ~93% of patients), sensorineural deafness (~96% of patients), and renal disease (~72% of patients), along with numerous atypical features including eye abnormalities, skin manifestations like psoriasis, neurological complications, and gastrointestinal and urogenital involvement. This case report describes a 27-year-old woman with a heterozygous GATA3 mutation who presented with severe hypocalcemia causing muscle cramps and paraesthesia, requiring escalating therapeutic interventions from oral calcium and vitamin D supplementation to calcitriol, teriparatide, and ultimately continuous PTH infusion. The patient also developed recurrent mature ovarian cystic teratomas associated with extensive endometriosis, treated with bilateral cystectomy and subsequent left oophorectomy. The case demonstrates the multi-system complexity of HDR syndrome and highlights that management requires coordinated care addressing hypocalcemia, hearing loss via cochlear implantation, and reproductive complications. Notably, calcium normalization improved the patient's psoriasis, illustrating the mechanistic link between hypoparathyroidism-induced hypocalcemia and disrupted cell adhesion through calcium-dependent cadherins.

title: Pregnancy outcomes and recurrence following surgical treatment of cesarean scar pregnancy: a retrospective analysis
summary:
# Summary

Cesarean scar pregnancy (CSP) represents a serious obstetric complication where implantation occurs within the scar tissue of a previous cesarean delivery, with the potential to cause secondary infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and miscarriage. This retrospective analysis demonstrates that surgical treatment outcomes and recurrence rates vary significantly by CSP type and surgical method employed. Among 74 treated patients, 67.57% achieved live birth, while 16.22% developed secondary infertility, with intrauterine adhesions emerging as the primary risk factor for failure to conceive. Combined hysteroscopic-laparoscopic surgery with direct scar repair proved most effective, achieving 100% live birth rates and zero recurrence in Type III cases, whereas hysteroscopic resection alone was associated with an 8-case recurrence rate. The pathophysiology involves repeated pregnancy losses triggering localized inflammatory responses with elevated TNF-α and IL-6, which inhibit embryo implantation, alongside endometrial damage that reduces receptivity through impaired blood perfusion and fibrotic tissue formation that decreases estrogen sensitivity. The findings underscore that avoiding excessive curettage, selecting surgical approaches that include scar repair, and managing adhesions are critical to improving reproductive outcomes in CSP patients.

title: Uretero-Fallopian Fistula after excision of ovarian cyst
summary:
# Summary: Uretero-Fallopian Fistula Following Ovarian Cyst Resection

Uretero-fallopian fistula is a serious iatrogenic complication arising from gynecological and other pelvic surgical procedures, with approximately 73% of cases resulting from unintended ureteral injuries during surgery. This case report documents a patient who developed a uretero-fallopian fistula with subsequent left-sided hydronephrosis and urinary incontinence eight months following laparotomy for left ovarian cyst excision. Common causative procedures include rectal cancer surgery, endometriosis treatment (laser procedures, laparoscopic fulguration, and surgical excision), open ureterolithotomy, and hysteroscopy with fallopian tube embolization, with histopathology revealing an inflammatory mass connecting the ureter and fallopian tube. The resulting fistula leads to significant morbidity including hydronephrosis, recurrent infections, urinary incontinence, and potential progression to renal failure if untreated. Definitive surgical management through ureter re-implantation successfully resolved the complication and restored continence in this patient. Early recognition of urinary tract symptoms following pelvic surgery and prompt imaging and urological intervention are essential to prevent long-term renal sequelae.

title: Advancements in Image‐Based Analyses for Morphology and Staging of Colon Cancer: A Comprehensive Review
summary:
# Summary

Colorectal cancer develops through uncontrolled proliferation of glandular epithelial cells driven by mutations in key genes including APC, TP53, and KRAS, as well as defects in DNA mismatch repair pathways involving MLH1 and MSH2. Epigenetic modifications such as DNA methylation and histone acetylation, combined with lifestyle and environmental factors including Western-style diets, obesity, chronic inflammation, and gut microbiota imbalances (particularly Fusobacterium nucleatum and Bacteroides fragilis), significantly contribute to colorectal cancer risk, which is further elevated in patients with inflammatory bowel diseases such as ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease. Advanced imaging and diagnostic technologies form the cornerstone of detection and staging, with colonoscopy achieving approximately 95% sensitivity for advanced adenoma detection and reducing colorectal cancer mortality by up to 68% when used for regular screening, while CT colonography and AI-enhanced imaging systems further improve lesion identification and staging accuracy. Modern therapeutic approaches include endoscopic mucosal resection for early-stage disease and targeted immunotherapies such as pembrolizumab and nivolumab for microsatellite instability-high tumors, alongside monoclonal antibodies targeting VEGF (bevacizumab) and EGFR (cetuximab) for advanced disease. Deep learning algorithms have demonstrated superior capabilities in predicting five-year survival outcomes, enhancing treatment response assessment and prognostic evaluation in colorectal cancer patients.

title: Incidental Primary Ovarian Squamous Cell Carcinoma Arising in Endometriosis Presenting As Adenomyosis-Related Abnormal Uterine Bleeding
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of primary ovarian squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) arising from endometriosis, which was initially masked by adenomyosis-related abnormal uterine bleeding. While endometriosis carries a well-established 1-2% risk of malignant transformation, most cases develop into endometrioid or clear-cell carcinomas, making OSCC from endometriosis exceptionally uncommon and typically arising instead from mature cystic teratomas or metaplasia. The tumor exhibited immunohistochemical markers characteristic of OSCC, including CK5/6 and p40 positivity with elevated Ki-67 proliferation index and p16 negativity, helping exclude HPV-related secondary tumors. Management followed epithelial ovarian cancer protocols with cytoreductive surgery including bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy followed by platinum and taxane-based chemotherapy. The case underscores that OSCC is an aggressive malignancy with poor prognosis and high recurrence rates, and notably, cases arising from endometriosis demonstrate poorer survival outcomes compared to those originating from teratomas, highlighting the clinical importance of detecting malignant transformation in endometriosis patients.

title: Prevalence of cesarean scar disorder in patients 3 years after a first cesarean section
summary:
# Summary: Cesarean Scar Disorder and Uterine Niche Formation

Cesarean section causes iatrogenic cesarean scar disorder, an iatrogenic complication characterized primarily by formation of a uterine niche—a defect resulting from inadequately healed myometrium at the incision site. Uterine niches are associated with multiple gynecological symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, postmenstrual spotting, and abnormal vaginal discharge, with larger niche volumes correlating with increased spotting risk. Beyond these localized effects, cesarean sections are associated with reduced fertility, dysmenorrhea, and chronic abdominal pain, while also increasing the risk of serious complications such as placenta accreta spectrum disorder, cesarean scar pregnancies, and uterine rupture. The cumulative effect of multiple cesarean sections exacerbates niche formation and volume, further compromising fertility and substantially reducing patient quality of life. These findings highlight the substantial long-term gynecological and reproductive burden imposed by cesarean delivery three years postoperatively.

title: Histopathological Association Between Chronic Endometritis and Adenomyosis: Clinical Findings and Risk Factors
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates a significant histopathological association between chronic endometritis (CE) and adenomyosis, with chronic endometritis occurring 2.4 times more frequently in adenomyotic patients compared to controls.
