Basal endometrial loss emerges as a critical intermediate factor, showing a strong correlation with chronic endometritis and serving as an independent risk factor for CE development. The pathological mechanism underlying this association involves the tissue injury and repair (TIAR) cascade, wherein repeated inflammatory damage to the basal endometrium triggers epithelial-mesenchymal transition, neoangiogenesis, and fibrosis, ultimately promoting myometrial infiltration characteristic of adenomyosis. Intrauterine microbial colonization and tissue inflammatory reactions activate toll-like receptor signaling and nuclear transcription factor-κB-dependent sterile inflammation, perpetuating this pathological cycle. Clinically, chronic endometritis contributes to multiple reproductive complications including recurrent implantation failure, recurrent pregnancy loss, and unexplained infertility, while adenomyosis similarly associates with pelvic pain, heavy menstrual bleeding, and adverse pregnancy outcomes. These findings suggest that chronic endometritis may represent a modifiable therapeutic target through antibiotic therapy in patients with adenomyosis and reproductive failure.

title: Uterine Artery Embolization for Symptomatic Uterine Myomas: Japanese Society of Interventional Radiology Procedural Guidelines 2021: English Version
summary: Uterine artery embolization (UAE) is an established minimally invasive treatment for symptomatic uterine myomas, effectively addressing common symptoms including excessive menstrual bleeding, anemia, dysmenorrhea, and pain. The procedure works by occluding the uterine and ovarian arteries that supply blood to fibroids, with trisacryl gelatin microspheres (Embosphere) serving as the primary embolic agent; particles ≥700 micrometers are preferred to prevent unintended ovarian artery occlusion and associated ovarian dysfunction. Critical contraindications include pregnancy, active pelvic inflammation, and suspected uterine malignancy, while complication risks increase with larger myoma size and smaller embolic particle size, which can cause uterine necrosis. Notably, UAE produces no significant effects on ovarian reserve, and including ovarian artery embolization reduces symptom recurrence rates compared to uterine artery embolization alone. Technical approaches via bilateral femoral arteries reduce fluoroscopy duration, whereas transradial approaches carry rare but serious risks of cerebral infarction and hand ischemia despite potential benefits in reducing deep vein thrombosis risk.

title: Micropolyps, Plasma Cells, and Pregnancy: Reevaluating Diagnostic and Therapeutic Strategies in Chronic Endometritis
summary: Chronic endometritis, a subclinical inflammation of the endometrium, significantly impairs female fertility by affecting endometrial receptivity and is associated with recurrent implantation failure and pregnancy loss. The condition is triggered by intrauterine colonization with multiple bacterial species including Streptococcus, Staphylococcus, Escherichia coli, Enterococcus faecalis, Mycoplasma, and Ureaplasma, which elicit dysregulated cytokine signaling, plasma cell infiltration, impaired decidualization, vascular abnormalities, and disrupted uterine contractility. Micropolyps represent the most reliable hysteroscopic marker for diagnosis, showing strong correlation with chronic endometritis, while CD138 immunohistochemical staining for plasma cells provides the gold standard histopathological confirmation. Importantly, antibiotic treatment with azithromycin significantly improves conception rates (53% versus 21%, p=0.022), with notably higher reproductive success in patients with secondary versus primary infertility. The study emphasizes the clinical importance of accurate diagnostic strategies combining hysteroscopy and endometrial biopsy to identify and treat this underdiagnosed condition in infertile populations.

title: Gestational Trophoblastic Disease: Diagnostic and Therapeutic Updates in Light of Recent Evidence: A Literature Review
summary:
# Gestational Trophoblastic Disease: Diagnostic and Therapeutic Updates

Gestational trophoblastic disease (GTD) encompasses a spectrum of conditions ranging from hydatidiform moles to malignant gestational trophoblastic neoplasia (GTN), with clinical presentation characterized by abnormal vaginal bleeding, pelvic pain, hyperemesis, and elevated human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) levels disproportionate to gestational age. Early diagnosis is critical and relies on ultrasonographic examination revealing characteristic "snowstorm" appearances in complete moles, combined with serial monitoring of serum β-hCG, allowing detection of disease before malignant progression occurs. Complete hydatidiform moles—which contain exclusively paternal genetic material and lack embryonic tissue—carry higher risks of malignant transformation to GTN, particularly in women over 40 years of age or with prior GTD history, with hCG levels often exceeding 100,000 mIU/mL. Treatment of hydatidiform moles involves uterine cavity evacuation, though hysterectomy reduces postmolar malignant evolution risk to 3–5% compared to 15–20% with evacuation alone, and Rh-negative patients receive anti-D immunoglobulin prophylaxis. Low-risk postmolar GTN responds to single-agent chemotherapy with methotrexate plus folinic acid, while high-risk or metastatic disease requires multi-drug EMA-CO protocol (etoposide, methotrexate, actinomycin D, cyclophosphamide, vincristine), with surveillance for secondary complications including neuropathy and hematologic toxicity. Approximately half of gestational choriocarcinomas arise from prior molar pregnancies and

title: Microbial profile of post-breeding endometritis in Arabian mares from the Al-Hira District, Iraq
summary:
# Summary

Postbreeding endometritis in Arabian mares is primarily caused by *Escherichia coli* (76.47% of isolates) and *Streptococcus* species, which colonize the vaginal flora and ascend to the uterus following breeding or parturition. Multiple predisposing factors promote disease development, including reproductive tract anatomical abnormalities, inadequate uterine clearance after semen deposition, retained placenta, and previous dystocia, with conformational deficiencies and compromised mechanical drainage being particularly significant in susceptible animals. The condition frequently progresses to pregnancy failure through early luteolysis and embryonic mortality, and results in significant subfertility characterized by diminished pregnancy and foaling rates. Ultrasonographic examination effectively detects endometritis by revealing uterine wall thickening, intraluminal fluid accumulation, and edema, which correlate with bacterial presence. Isolated pathogens demonstrate high resistance to aminopenicillins, first-generation cephalosporins, and fluoroquinolones, but remain susceptible to carbapenems (meropenem, ertapenem, doripenem) and β-lactam combinations like piperacillin/tazobactum, which are recommended for therapeutic management.

title: Forensic considerations in cases of fatal constipation
summary: This forensic pathology article examines fatal constipation as a cause of death, detailing the cascade of serious complications that can arise from severe bowel dysfunction. Chronic constipation can progress through multiple mechanisms including fecal impaction, megacolon formation, and volvulus, each triggering distinct pathological consequences such as bowel obstruction, stercoral colitis with perforation, and ischemic necrosis. Beyond local intestinal effects, constipation causes systemic complications including abdominal compartment syndrome, compression of pelvic veins leading to venous thrombosis and pulmonary thromboembolism, as well as respiratory compromise from space-occupying effects. Multiple medications including opiates, antidepressants, antihypertensives, and anticholinergics significantly increase constipation risk, while underlying neurological, metabolic, and psychiatric conditions (Parkinson disease, diabetes mellitus, autism, Down syndrome) are strongly associated with the condition. The article highlights that even treatment interventions carry mortality risk, particularly magnesium-containing laxatives and enema administration, which can cause fatal hypermagnesemia, rectal perforation, and electrolyte disturbances in vulnerable patients.

title: Chronic endometritis diagnosis and fertility outcomes: an old unresolved question
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis (CE) is significantly associated with infertility, recurrent miscarriage, and repeated implantation failure, yet its accurate diagnosis remains clinically challenging. CD138 immunostaining, which identifies plasma cells in the endometrium, has emerged as the most reliable diagnostic marker, showing that many patients with normal hysteroscopic findings or conventional histology still harbor CE. Hysteroscopy and conventional histology demonstrate low sensitivity and accuracy respectively, while hysteroscopic findings such as hyperemia, strawberry appearance, and micropolyps, though suggestive, cannot definitively exclude or confirm CE diagnosis. Antibiotic therapy significantly improves pregnancy rates in treated patients (53%) compared to untreated patients (20%), despite plasma cell count alone not being predictive of pregnancy outcomes. Multiple bacterial taxa including Ralstonia, Gardnerella, Streptococcus, and Ureaplasma are associated with CE, while commensal protective organisms like Bifidobacterium and lactic acid are reduced, suggesting dysbiosis plays a pathogenic role. Emerging molecular mechanisms involve upregulated microRNAs (miR-27a-3p and miR-124-3p) and anti-inflammatory therapeutic approaches via stem cell-derived exosomes targeting the TLR4/NF-κB pathway.

title: When and How Is Surgery Required for Large Endometrioma prior to in vitro Fertilization: A Survey of Practices
summary: Large endometriomas present a surgical dilemma in assisted reproduction because they reduce ovarian reserve and follicle accessibility while causing pain, yet their surgical removal through laparoscopic cystectomy—the predominant approach—further compromises ovarian function by reducing anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels and causing tissue loss. Since AMH directly correlates with ovarian response and oocyte yield, cystectomy-induced declines in AMH paradoxically impair the fertility outcomes it aims to improve. Alternative minimally invasive techniques such as CO2 laser ablation and plasma energy show promise in achieving comparable recurrence rates while minimizing ovarian damage. Current clinical practice increasingly incorporates preoperative AMH measurement to guide surgical decisions, with most practitioners adjusting their approach based on these hormone levels and prescribing progestin-based hormonal therapy before and after intervention. The evidence suggests that for large endometriomas, particularly those over 40 mm, clinicians must carefully weigh the benefits of pain relief and improved follicle accessibility against the reproductive costs of tissue loss when counseling patients undergoing in vitro fertilization.

title: Determinants of uterine synechiae at hysteroscopy: a case-control study in Kinshasa, Democratic Republic of Congo
summary: This case-control study from Kinshasa identified key risk factors for uterine synechiae (intrauterine adhesions), with curettage and uterine surgery showing the strongest associations, quadrupling and tripling risk respectively. Endometrial trauma from curettage and post-abortion/post-partum complications account for approximately 95% of synechiae formation, while dysmenorrhea doubled the risk of this condition. The study demonstrated that uterine synechiae cause substantial reproductive morbidity, including infertility, recurrent miscarriage, amenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and serious pregnancy complications such as placenta previa, placenta accreta, intrauterine growth retardation, and prematurity. Additional risk factors identified included genital tuberculosis, post-partum infections, menopause, and vascular disorders, with endometritis potentially exacerbating curettage-related risk. Hysteroscopy emerged as the reference standard for both diagnosis and treatment of uterine synechiae, offering potential intervention to prevent these severe obstetric outcomes.

title: Total self-BAT scores associated with choice of heavy menstrual bleeding treatment
summary:
# Summary

Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) frequently results from underlying bleeding disorders, including von Willebrand disease type 1 and other inherited bleeding tendencies, which can lead to excessive bleeding during menstruation and childbirth and increase transfusion requirements. The self-administered bleeding assessment tool (self-BAT) questionnaire effectively identifies patients with abnormal bleeding tendencies, with higher scores (≥5-6) associated with more severe disease and increased likelihood of undergoing surgical intervention such as endometrial ablation or hysterectomy. Patients with abnormal self-BAT scores were more commonly prescribed medical therapies including hormonal agents, tranexamic acid, and iron supplementation, while those with lower scores were more likely to receive combined hormonal and tranexamic acid therapy. Implementation of structured bleeding assessment tools in gynecological practice could improve diagnostic accuracy for bleeding disorders, guide appropriate treatment selection, and potentially prevent unnecessary hysterectomies by identifying underlying coagulation defects earlier. HMB substantially impacts patient quality of life through physical symptoms and psychological burden, highlighting the clinical importance of systematic screening for bleeding disorders and appropriate therapeutic intervention.

title: Comparison of clinical hysterectomy indications with ai-based recommendations: a prospective study
summary:
# Summary

This prospective study evaluated ChatGPT-4 as a decision-support tool for hysterectomy indications by comparing its recommendations with actual clinical decisions in 87 standardized gynecological scenarios. ChatGPT-4 recommended hysterectomy in 70.1% of cases, demonstrating substantial agreement with original clinical decisions and suggesting the large language model delivers guideline-consistent surgical recommendations. The cohort presented with diverse uterine pathologies, with uterine myomas (leiomyomas) being the most common finding at 70.1%, alongside abnormal uterine bleeding symptoms (irregular menstruation 31.0%, heavy menstrual bleeding 27.6%) as primary indications for the procedure. When hysterectomy was not recommended, ChatGPT-4 suggested uterus-sparing alternatives including myomectomy, hysteroscopy, medical therapy, and LNG-IUD, recognizing that less invasive interventions can offer comparable quality-of-life benefits. Discrepancies between AI and clinical recommendations were primarily associated with fibroid characteristics, symptom burden, anemia, and patient reproductive goals, suggesting that individualized clinical judgment remains essential for optimizing treatment selection in complex cases.

title: Management of pregnancy and childbirth in Glanzmann thrombasthenia: A case series and review
summary:
# Summary

Glanzmann thrombasthenia is a rare inherited platelet disorder caused by defects in αIIbβ3 integrins, which impair platelet aggregation and lead to increased bleeding risk, including abnormal uterine bleeding in almost all affected women. Women with GT face elevated rates of both maternal and neonatal complications during pregnancy and childbirth, with particular concerns including post-partum hemorrhage (treated with platelet transfusions, recombinant activated factor VII, and antifibrinolytics), platelet alloimmunization with development of αIIbβ3 and HLA antibodies, and associated fetal and neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia with risk of intracranial hemorrhage. Affected women also experience increased infertility due to ovarian hemorrhage and endometriosis, with approximately 50% requiring assisted reproductive therapy; in vitro fertilization and intracytoplasmic sperm injection have been performed safely in GT populations. Management recommendations include optimizing hemoglobin and iron levels, using cesarean section to minimize instrumentation-related fetal bleeding, avoiding regional anesthesia, monitoring for maternal platelet antibodies, considering intravenous immunoglobulin for suspected fetal alloimmune thrombocytopenia, and checking neonatal platelet counts.

title: Piriformis syndrome: a systematic review of case reports
summary:
# Summary

Piriformis syndrome (PS) is a neuromuscular disorder causing sciatica through sciatic nerve entrapment or compression by the piriformis muscle. Multiple etiologies contribute to PS development, including anatomical variants of the piriformis muscle or sciatic nerve, muscle hypertrophy, trauma-induced inflammation and edema, vascular anomalies (venous plexuses, arterial loops, aneurysms), and rare conditions such as pyomyositis and endometriosis. This systematic review of 212 case reports demonstrates that instrumental diagnosis via intrapelvic MRI significantly improves surgical treatment success rates (54.3% healing in diagnosed patients versus 15.5% failure in clinically diagnosed cases), while conservative treatments including injections, physiotherapy, and exercise programs are effective in 41% of patients. Surgical interventions, employed in 59% of cases, primarily involve piriformis tendon release with or without sciatic nerve neurolysis, abscess drainage, or mass resection depending on the underlying pathology. The high association between pelvic trauma and PS, combined with the diagnostic superiority of instrumental imaging, underscores the importance of comprehensive radiological evaluation for optimizing clinical outcomes in this challenging condition.

title: Traumatic transection of the uterus: missed, diagnosed, and repaired—a case report and review of literature
summary:
# Summary

Uterine transection, a rare injury resulting from high-intensity pelvic trauma such as motor vehicle accidents or seatbelt injuries, causes severe reproductive morbidity including infertility, amenorrhea, dysmenorrhea, hematometra, and hemoperitoneum when left undiagnosed. The condition is frequently associated with endometriosis through retrograde menstruation mechanisms, and can also result iatrogenically from gynecological procedures including myomectomy, adenomyomectomy, and ovarian surgery. Surgical repair involving careful alignment of the cervical and uterine canals with layered suturing and catheter placement effectively restores menstrual function, eliminates dysmenorrhea, and improves fertility prospects in affected patients. This case report emphasizes the clinical importance of recognizing uterine transection in patients presenting with primary amenorrhea, primary infertility, and severe pelvic pain, as timely diagnosis and repair can dramatically improve reproductive outcomes.

title: Understanding Preferences, Needs, and Expectations for Pharmaceutical Treatment of Symptomatic Uterine Fibroids Based on Surveys of Healthcare Providers and Patients
summary: Uterine fibroids are common benign tumors affecting up to 70% of women before menopause and cause multiple debilitating symptoms including heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pain, pelvic pressure, and urinary dysfunction in 20–50% of affected women. These symptoms significantly impair quality of life, leading to work absenteeism and decreased productivity. Multiple pharmacological treatments are available according to ACOG recommendations, including progestins, oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists and antagonists, tranexamic acid, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, alongside surgical options such as hysterectomy, myomectomy, and minimally invasive procedures that may preserve fertility. Survey data reveals distinct treatment preference segments among healthcare providers: efficacy-focused providers prioritize rapid improvements in bleeding and menstrual blood loss at six months, safety-focused providers emphasize adverse effects like hair loss and bone mineral density changes, convenience-focused providers value simplified dosing and integrated contraceptive effects, and economics-focused providers prioritize insurance coverage and out-of-pocket costs. Patients demonstrate similar segmentation, with symptom-relief-driven patients prioritizing bleeding reduction and symptom severity improvement that directly impact daily functioning, suggesting that aligning pharmaceutical treatment selection with both provider expertise and patient values optimizes therapeutic outcomes.

title: Adverse Pregnancy Outcomes in an IVF Patient with Anti-Synthetase Syndrome: Insights From a Rare Case
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of anti-synthetase syndrome (ASS), an autoimmune disorder characterized by anti-aminoacyl tRNA synthetase antibodies (particularly anti-PL-12 and anti-Jo-1), manifesting with severe reproductive complications in an infertile IVF patient. The patient exhibited diminished ovarian reserve, abnormal oocyte morphology with thickened zona pellucida, and recurrent fertilization failure across multiple assisted reproductive cycles—findings attributed to chronic immune dysregulation and autoimmune-mediated gonadal injury. Beyond oocyte dysfunction, autoimmune mechanisms including anti-zona pellucida antibodies, elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-17), impaired regulatory T cells, and antinuclear antibodies disrupted implantation and early embryonic development, culminating in adverse pregnancy outcomes and intrauterine growth restriction. The case illustrates how pregnancy-triggered immune dysregulation in ASS patients may exacerbate both systemic manifestations (myositis, interstitial lung disease) and reproductive dysfunction, underscoring the critical interplay between autoimmunity and fertility. This rare syndrome should be considered in IVF patients with unexplained poor oocyte quality and recurrent implantation failure, particularly when accompanied by systemic autoimmune features.

title: Misdiagnosis of occult heterotopic intramural ectopic pregnancy by two-dimensional ultrasound: Two case reports
summary:
# Summary

Intramural pregnancy (IMP), a rare form of ectopic pregnancy implanting directly within the myometrium, poses significant clinical risks including uterine rupture and life-threatening hemorrhage. Multiple risk factors contribute to its development, with curettage, uterine trauma, pelvic surgery, and assisted reproductive technologies being the most commonly identified culprits, particularly when they cause endometrial damage and compromise the uterine integrity. The expanding gestational sac progressively erodes myometrial tissue, creating diagnostic challenges that are frequently missed by standard two-dimensional transvaginal ultrasound, as demonstrated in these two cases where initial imaging failed to detect the abnormal pregnancy location. Three-dimensional transvaginal sonography, magnetic resonance imaging, and color Doppler flow imaging reveal distinctive features including abnormal vascularization and precise gestational sac location within the myometrial layer, substantially improving diagnostic accuracy. Serum β-hCG levels guide treatment decisions, with higher levels (>10,000 mIU/mL) typically necessitating surgical intervention via hysterectomy or laparoscopic-assisted hysteroscopic resection, while lower levels may permit conservative management or medical therapy. Early detection through multimodal imaging combined with clinical vigilance in at-risk populations and systematic postoperative β-hCG monitoring is essential to prevent catastrophic complications and preserve reproductive outcomes where feasible.

title: Impact of uterine adenomyosis on survival outcome of patients with non-endometrioid endometrial cancer
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates how uterine adenomyosis affects survival outcomes in patients with non-endometrioid endometrial cancer, a rare but aggressive malignancy characterized by deep myometrial invasion, lymphovascular space invasion, and extrauterine dissemination. Surprisingly, patients with adenomyosis demonstrated significantly improved overall survival compared to those without adenomyosis, despite adenomyosis commonly presenting as earlier-stage disease with lower tumor grades. The protective effect of adenomyosis appears mechanistically linked to an enhanced antitumoral immune response, with adenomyotic tissue showing elevated levels of interferon-γ, tumor necrosis factor-α, and interleukin-10, while displaying reduced oncogenic cytokines including interleukin-8 and transforming growth factor. Multiple factors including cancer stage, lymphovascular space invasion, lymphatic metastasis, and cervical stromal invasion independently predicted both disease recurrence and overall survival, with patients experiencing recurrence facing an 8.36-fold increased mortality risk. These findings suggest that adenomyosis may provide a protective immunological microenvironment that constrains aggressive non-endometrioid endometrial cancer progression, though the precise mechanisms warrant further investigation.

title: Parathyromatosis: The Pathogenic Background (Post-Parathyroidectomy Seeding or Exceptional Embryologic Remnant) and the Importance of a Fine Clinical Index for Recurrent Primary Hyperparathyroidism (a Narrative Review)
summary: Parathyromatosis is a rare clinical condition arising from either iatrogenic dissemination of parathyroid cells during surgical manipulation or from embryological parathyroid remnants, both leading to multiple ectopic parathyroid tissue deposits that autonomously secrete excessive parathyroid hormone (PTH). The pathogenic mechanisms differ between post-parathyroidectomy cases, where intraoperative cell seeding occurs, and surgically naive patients, where scattered embryologic tissue develops hyperplastic function. Secondary hyperparathyroidism from chronic kidney disease represents a major risk factor, wherein persistent hypocalcemia and hyperphosphatemia trigger sustained PTH stimulation that causes parathyroid gland autonomization and reduced calcium-sensing receptor expression. Patients present with diverse symptoms including bone pain, kidney stones, muscle weakness, fatigue, skin itching, and gastrointestinal disturbances related to severe hypercalcemia. Diagnostic imaging using ultrasound, 99m-Technetium sestamibi scanning, and 18F-Fluorocholine PET-CT can identify parathyromatosis, which characteristically presents as multiple micro-nodules without capsules, distinguishing it from single ectopic adenomas. While parathyroidectomy successfully treats primary hyperparathyroidism with normalization of PTH and calcium levels, subtotal parathyroidectomy carries higher recurrence risk; medical management with bisphosphonates and calcimimetics provides alternative control when surgery is not feasible.

title: Conservative Surgical Management of Adenomyosis: Implications for Infertility and Pregnancy Outcomes—A Perspective Review
summary: Adenomyosis is a significant cause of infertility and adverse pregnancy outcomes in women of reproductive age, operating through multiple mechanisms including chronic inflammation, oxidative stress, impaired uterine contractility, and compromised endometrial receptivity mediated by elevated macrophages, pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), and reactive oxygen species that damage oocytes and embryos. Conservative surgical approaches, particularly adenomyomectomy, substantially improve fertility outcomes with pregnancy rates of 40–52.7% and live birth rates up to 70%, though outcomes are superior in focal versus diffuse adenomyosis due to increased uterine rupture risk (6.8%) in the latter. Non-excisional interventions including high-intensity focused ultrasound, radiofrequency ablation, and uterine artery embolization achieve comparable pregnancy rates (~51%) with potentially reduced morbidity, while adjunctive hormonal therapies with GnRH agonists further enhance reproductive outcomes. Junctional zone thickness, lesion type, and lesion location are strong predictive factors for fertility success following surgery, with postoperative junctional zone measurements serving as the most reliable predictor of long-term cumulative pregnancy rates. These findings underscore the importance of personalized surgical strategies based on adenomyosis phenotype in restoring fertility and improving pregnancy viability in affected women.

title: Novel application of transcervical radiofrequency ablation for symptomatic cystic adenomyosis. Case report
summary: Cystic adenomyosis is a rare variant of adenomyosis characterized by myometrial cysts that causes severe dysmenorrhea and chronic pelvic pain in women of reproductive age. Traditional surgical approaches such as laparoscopic cystectomy and hysteroscopic resection carry risks of myometrial weakening, scarring, thinning, and adhesion formation that can compromise future pregnancies. This case report describes the first successful application of transcervical radiofrequency ablation (RFA) using the Sonata system to treat symptomatic cystic adenomyosis, a minimally invasive technique that uses intrauterine ultrasound guidance to precisely target and ablate deep intramural lesions while preserving myometrial integrity. The patient, who had failed hormonal therapy with dienogest and had concurrent peritoneal endometriosis, achieved complete resolution of the cystic lesion and significant symptom improvement at six-month follow-up. These findings suggest that transcervical RFA represents a promising alternative to conventional surgical techniques, potentially reducing complications while effectively treating focal adenomyotic disease and preventing future obstetric morbidity.

title: Is the hysteroscopic surgery of isthmocele related to the niche dimensions?
summary: Isthmoceles are anatomical defects in the uterine wall commonly resulting from cesarean section, occurring in 24-70% of cases and presenting as a wedge-shaped hypoechoic area on transvaginal ultrasonography. These niches are associated with multiple gynecological symptoms including postmenstrual spotting, chronic pelvic pain, abnormal uterine bleeding, dyspareunia, and secondary infertility. The pathophysiology involves blood collection within the isthmocele pouch triggering an inflammatory response that affects nerve fibers, with symptom severity correlating to niche depth and residual myometrial thickness rather than niche width. Hysteroscopic surgery effectively treats isthmoceles, achieving symptom resolution in approximately 76% of patients overall, with abnormal uterine bleeding resolving in 97% of cases within two months postoperatively. Preoperative measurement of residual myometrial thickness is a strong predictor of surgical success, with thicker residual myometrium associated with better outcomes; conversely, residual myometrial thickness below 2.5 mm warrants sonohysterography for accurate diagnosis. Three-dimensional transvaginal ultrasonography is increasingly used to improve niche evaluation and guide surgical planning for optimal patient selection.

title: Investigation of The Effect of Myomectomy on Anti-Mullerian
summary:
# Summary

Myomectomy significantly reduces anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels within six months of surgery in women with uterine fibroids, with laparotomy producing a greater decline in AMH than laparoscopic approaches. The magnitude of AMH reduction correlates directly with myoma size and type, with hybrid myomas and fibroids larger than 8 cm showing the most pronounced decreases, while subserosal and smaller myomas demonstrate minimal changes. Laparotomy also carries higher rates of postoperative complications including fever, pain, and surgical site infection compared to laparoscopy. The mechanism underlying AMH decline likely involves temporary ovarian ischemia and blood supply obstruction during surgery, exacerbated by excessive bleeding, prolonged operative time, and tourniquet use, particularly in intramural myoma cases. These findings suggest that while myomectomy addresses fibroid-related symptoms such as abnormal uterine bleeding and infertility, the procedure may transiently impair ovarian reserve, warranting consideration of minimally invasive surgical approaches to preserve fertility potential in women of reproductive age.

title: Acute fungal endometritis in women with abnormal uterine bleeding: Clinical and microbiological insights
summary:
# Summary

Acute fungal endometritis is a rare but severe infection of the uterine lining that presents with abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic discomfort, and vaginal discharge, alongside systemic symptoms including fever, abdominal pain, and uterine fluid accumulation. Candida albicans, Nakaseomyces glabratus, and Candida orthopsilosis are the primary fungal pathogens responsible, with prolonged antibiotic use and diabetes mellitus serving as major predisposing factors. Diagnosis requires direct endometrial tissue sampling, as vaginal discharge cultures characteristically yield negative results, and histopathology reveals extensive inflammation with neutrophil infiltration and microabscesses. The condition disrupts the endometrium's protective immune surveillance system—normally maintained by macrophages, natural killer cells, and T lymphocytes—and impairs blood coagulation, leading to abnormal bleeding patterns and potentially compromising fertility. Combined treatment with voriconazole, surgical debridement, and curettage proved highly effective across all identified fungal species, though emerging antifungal resistance among non-albicans Candida species warrants continued surveillance.

title: Cerebral infarction associated with adenomyosis: Two case reports with mechanistic insights and multidisciplinary management
summary:
# Summary: Adenomyosis and Cerebral Infarction Risk

This case report series reveals a striking and previously underrecognized association between adenomyosis and cerebral infarction through multiple interconnected pathogenic mechanisms. Adenomyosis causes dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, and infertility through abnormal endometrial infiltration, but the critical finding is that ectopic endometrium within adenomyotic lesions upregulates tissue factor, which initiates the extrinsic coagulation cascade and drives a hypercoagulable state. This hypercoagulability is further amplified by elevated mucinous tumor markers (CA125 and CA199) that peak during menstruation and can trigger nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis, cardioembolism, and microthrombi formation that enter systemic circulation through abnormally dilated adenomyotic vessels. The menorrhagia resulting from adenomyosis causes iron-deficiency anemia, which independently accelerates atherosclerosis development through endothelial dysfunction, increases blood viscosity, and affects platelet aggregation—additional mechanisms promoting cerebral thromboembolism. Management requires multidisciplinary coordination including acute thrombolytic or thrombectomy interventions, secondary prevention with antiplatelet and anticoagulant agents, and definitive adenomyosis treatment via gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, uterine artery embolization, or hysterectomy to prevent recurrent cerebral infarction.

title: Prospective, Multicenter Clinical Trial to Evaluate the Safety of the Stella® Intragastric Balloon at 7 Months and the Balloon Delivery System
summary:
# Summary

This prospective, multicenter clinical trial evaluated the safety and efficacy of the Stella® intragastric balloon (IGB), a novel double-lumen device designed for improved procedural safety through guidewire-assisted placement, in treating obesity over a 6-month period. The device achieved significant therapeutic outcomes, with patients experiencing mean total weight loss of 15.39% at 6-month follow-up alongside substantial improvements in metabolic parameters including blood pressure, cholesterol, triglycerides, and liver enzymes. Common adverse effects associated with balloon placement included abdominal pain (59.4%), nausea and vomiting (52.1%), dyspepsia (30.4%), and gastroesophageal reflux (26.1%), though abdominal pain decreased significantly over time, declining from 62.32% at 72 hours to 7.58% at 3 months. Additional complications included constipation (5.7%), diarrhea (4.3%), anxiety (2.8%), vitamin deficiency (2.4%), and dehydration (2.4%), with five patients (7.24%) requiring early removal due to clinical intolerance. The trial documented several important safety considerations, including one device rupture secondary to fungal colonization and a Mallory-Weiss lesion caused by catheter traction, with supportive pharmacotherapy (proton pump inhibitors, antiemetics, and analgesics) administered during the accommodative phase to manage early symptoms.

title: Comprehensive Analysis of Risk Factors for Recurrence in Women of Reproductive Age Undergoing Hysteroscopic Polypectomy
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies multiple risk factors for endometrial polyp recurrence in reproductive-age women undergoing hysteroscopic resection. Adenomyosis, polycystic ovarian syndrome, obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²), leiomyoma uteri, and tamoxifen use emerged as significant independent predictors of polyp recurrence, with adenomyosis conferring the highest risk (OR=10.3). The pathophysiologic mechanism underlying recurrence involves excessive estrogen stimulation without adequate progesterone counterbalance, exacerbated by obesity, anovulatory cycles, and hormone replacement therapy, while concurrent conditions like chronic endometritis and endometrial hyperplasia further facilitate recurrence. Levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) treatment substantially reduced recurrence rates compared to other hormonal interventions, inducing endometrial atrophy and achieving significantly improved recurrence-free survival. Alternative preventive strategies including combined oral contraceptives, cyclic progestins, and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists were less effective. These findings suggest that identifying high-risk patients and implementing tailored prophylactic therapy, particularly LNG-IUS placement, is essential for improving long-term reproductive outcomes in women with recurrent endometrial polyps.

title: Diagnosis and classification of uterine fibroids
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Uterine Fibroids: Clinical Manifestations and Pathophysiological Mechanisms

Uterine fibroids cause abnormal uterine bleeding through multiple interconnected mechanisms, including fibroid-related vascular abnormalities, impaired endometrial hemostasis, altered molecular angiogenic factors, and disrupted uterine contractility, with submucosal fibroids particularly associated with heavy menstrual bleeding. At the molecular level, elevated transforming growth factor-β3 (TGF-β3) produced by submucosal fibroids reduces expression of the endometrial receptivity genes HOXA10 and HOXA11, while altered myometrial contractility impairs both sperm transport and embryo implantation, contributing to the high rates of infertility and recurrent early pregnancy loss observed in affected women. Beyond reproductive complications, fibroids cause significant bulk-related symptoms including pelvic pressure, lower back pain, abdominal discomfort, and bloating, and when positioned anteriorly or posteriorly can compress the bladder or rectum, leading to urinary dysfunction and constipation. Uterine fibroids are also associated with serious adverse pregnancy outcomes including preterm birth, fetal malpresentation, placental abruption, and labor dystocia, with particularly severe cases occasionally causing urinary obstruction or hydronephrosis. Diagnostic approaches include transvaginal ultrasonography, sonohysterography for submucosal fibroids with near-perfect sensitivity, and magnetic resonance imaging as the gold standard, which uniquely enables detection of concerning features such as leiomyosarcoma and adenomyosis. Notably, uterine fibroids demonstrate significantly higher prevalence and more severe disease burden in Black, East Asian, and South and Southeast

title: Elevating and empowering reproductive futures: Pediatric and adolescent gynecology's specialized care across the globe
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

Pediatric and adolescent gynecology (PAG) specialists provide specialized, age-tailored care for a broad spectrum of reproductive health conditions in young patients worldwide. Endometriosis represents a frequently underdiagnosed condition in adolescents that PAG experts manage through laparoscopic diagnosis and treatment, with early intervention improving quality of life and preventing chronic pain syndromes. Beyond endometriosis, PAG specialists treat diverse menstrual disorders including dysmenorrhea and heavy menstrual bleeding using hormonal therapies (combined oral contraceptives, progestins, intrauterine devices) and nonhormonal approaches such as tranexamic acid. The field encompasses management of pubertal abnormalities, reproductive tract anomalies including Müllerian malformations and congenital differences in sex development, ovarian pathology, and fertility preservation for patients with premature ovarian insufficiency caused by genetic conditions, autoimmune disorders, or gonadotoxic cancer treatments. PAG specialists also address acute surgical emergencies, contraceptive counseling to prevent rapid repeat pregnancies in adolescents, and sexual health promotion including HPV vaccination and STI prevention. This comprehensive, globally distributed specialty transforms reproductive health outcomes in pediatric and adolescent populations through expertise in both medical management and surgical intervention tailored to preserve future fertility and function.

title: Spontaneous Hemoperitoneum From a Ruptured Vessel Overlying a Sub‐Serosal Myoma: A Rare and Life‐Threatening Emergency in Women
summary:
# Summary

Uterine leiomyomas (fibroids) are the most common pelvic tumors in women and typically cause abnormal uterine bleeding, anemia, pelvic pressure, and pain. However, this case report highlights a rare and life-threatening complication: spontaneous hemoperitoneum resulting from rupture of blood vessels overlying subserosal or pedunculated fibroids. Large fibroid size (≥10 cm), venous congestion from pregnancy, and elevated intra-abdominal pressure contribute to vascular rupture and subsequent massive bleeding that manifests with severe abdominal pain, syncope, hemodynamic instability, and hypovolemic shock. Point-of-care ultrasound using the RUSH protocol was critical for rapid diagnosis, revealing collapsed inferior vena cava and free intraperitoneal fluid with organized clots. Emergency surgical intervention via exploratory laparotomy with hysterectomy, combined with blood product resuscitation and fluid management, successfully treated the hemoperitoneum and prevented fatal hemorrhagic shock. This case underscores the importance of recognizing spontaneous hemoperitoneum as a potentially catastrophic complication in symptomatic fibroid patients presenting with acute abdominal symptoms and hemodynamic instability.

title: Left Ovarian Vein Transposition to Restore Renal Venous Outflow in a Patient With Severe Pelvic Congestion After Multiple Endovascular and Failed Open Procedures for Nutcracker Syndrome
summary: Nutcracker syndrome, caused by compression of the left renal vein between the superior mesenteric artery and aorta, produces diverse clinical manifestations including hematuria, abdominal and pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and dysmenorrhea. The syndrome frequently associates with pelvic congestion, which can cause varicosities and edema, and may be complicated by related conditions such as May-Thurner syndrome and endometriosis. This case describes a patient with severe pelvic congestion secondary to nutcracker syndrome who failed multiple interventions, including endovascular stenting (complicated by stent migration) and renal vein reconstruction (resulting in thrombosis and retroperitoneal hematoma). Left ovarian vein transposition to the left external iliac vein was successfully performed as a salvage procedure to restore renal venous outflow and alleviate the patient's severe pelvic congestion symptoms. The procedure demonstrated excellent postoperative outcomes with hemodynamic stability and resolution of venous symptoms, suggesting this approach may be valuable for patients with intractable pelvic congestion unresponsive to conventional endovascular and open surgical treatments.

title: Innovative Applications of Hydrogels in Contemporary Medicine
summary:
# Summary

Hydrogels represent a versatile platform technology with broad applications across reproductive health and beyond. In gynecology, hydrogels address critical unmet needs by promoting endometrial regeneration, preventing adhesions associated with Asherman's syndrome (which causes infertility and menstrual irregularities), and treating infections through specialized formulations such as chlorhexidine-based gels for bacterial vaginosis and hyaluronic acid preparations for atrophic vaginitis. A particularly innovative development involves acrylamide-based hydrogel implants crosslinked with photolabile or disulfide-containing molecules that effectively prevent endometriosis while remaining degradable via near-visible UV light or physiological reducing agents like glutathione. Beyond reproductive applications, hydrogels serve as multifunctional drug delivery systems, contraceptive implants, and wound dressings, with polymers like Carbomers and Hydroxyethylcellulose enabling prolonged therapeutic release. The breadth of hydrogel applications extends to cardiology (myocardial infarction treatment through angiogenesis promotion and inflammatory inhibition), ophthalmology, cancer therapy, and surgical repair, underscoring their importance as a transformative class of biomaterials in contemporary medicine.

title: Damage Control Surgery in Obstetrics and Gynecology: Abdomino-Pelvic Packing in Multimodal Hemorrhage Management
summary:
# Summary

Abdomino-pelvic packing (APP) is a damage control surgical technique that achieves hemostasis in 75–90% of cases of massive postpartum hemorrhage, with significantly lower mortality than trauma surgery, and is effective across multiple obstetric and gynecologic emergencies including placenta accreta spectrum, uterine rupture, and deep-infiltrating endometriosis-related retroperitoneal bleeding. Modern hemorrhage management integrates APP within a multimodal framework that includes early tranexamic acid administration, topical hemostatic agents, massive transfusion protocols with 1:1:1 blood product ratios, calcium chloride supplementation, and active rewarming to counteract the lethal triad of hypothermia, acidosis, and coagulopathy that amplifies perioperative bleeding. Key complications arising from specific gynecologic procedures—such as trocar-entry injuries during laparoscopy, presacral bleeding during sacrocolpopexy, and retroperitoneal hemorrhage in deep endometriosis surgery—are now manageable through APP when conventional hemostasis fails. The physiologic adaptations of pregnancy, including 30–50% increased blood volume and a hypercoagulable state, demand recognition that pregnant patients tolerate hemorrhage differently and require specialized protocols. Surgeon and hospital volume correlate directly with reduced mortality, emphasizing the importance of multidisciplinary coordination and centralization of care for hemorrhagic emergencies in obstetrics and gynecology.

title: Nutrition and Uterine Fibroids: Clinical Impact and Emerging Therapeutic Perspectives
summary:
# Summary: Nutrition and Uterine Fibroids: Clinical Impact and Emerging Therapeutic Perspectives

Uterine fibroids are benign smooth muscle tumors with multifactorial etiology involving hormonal, genetic, and environmental components that commonly cause pelvic pain, heavy menstrual bleeding, anemia, and infertility. Dietary factors significantly modulate fibroid risk through estrogen metabolism and systemic inflammation: saturated fats and red meat elevate circulating estrogen and promote fibroid development, while fiber-rich diets and plant-derived compounds like those in fruits, vegetables, and green tea provide protective effects. Obesity represents a major modifiable risk factor, promoting fibroid growth through increased estrogen production via aromatization, alongside non-modifiable factors including genetic predisposition, vitamin D deficiency, early menarche, and late menopause. At the molecular level, somatic mutations in MED12 (the most common alteration) and less frequent mutations in HMGA2, FH, and COL4A genes drive fibroid formation through dysregulation of critical signaling pathways including Wnt/β-catenin and AKT/mTOR signaling, coupled with impaired DNA repair capacity in fibroid tissue. Oral contraceptive use demonstrates preventive benefits by reducing fibroid risk and associated symptoms, suggesting that comprehensive management strategies integrating dietary modification, lifestyle interventions, and targeted therapeutics based on molecular drivers may optimize outcomes for patients with this prevalent reproductive condition.

title: Vernix Caseosa Peritonitis: A Scoping Review
summary: Vernix caseosa peritonitis (VCP) is a rare but clinically significant postpartum complication arising from spillage of vernix caseosa—a protective fetal substance produced by sebaceous glands—into the maternal peritoneum, where it triggers an acute inflammatory response and foreign body-type granulomatous reaction. While cesarean section is the primary risk factor (87% of cases), VCP also occurs after vaginal delivery (13% of cases), particularly when amniotic fluid containing vernix caseosa or fetal lanugo enters the peritoneal cavity through retrograde flow or membrane rupture. Patients typically present three to thirty-five days postpartum with acute abdominal pain (89.1%), fever over 38°C (71.7%), and notably, respiratory symptoms including dyspnea and tachypnea (23.9% and 15.2% respectively), which may initially suggest acute appendicitis or peritonitis. Diagnostic imaging, particularly abdominal-pelvic CT, reveals characteristic findings including peritoneal thickening, inflammatory fat stranding, and free peritoneal fluid. Definitive treatment requires surgical intervention via laparotomy or laparoscopy with peritoneal lavage and tissue sampling, while corticosteroids have shown promising benefits in improving symptom resolution, whereas antibiotics play a limited therapeutic role. Increased clinical awareness of VCP presentation and its association with both cesarean and vaginal delivery is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management.

title: Comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic approach to male factor infertility aimed at natural fertility: A multicentric retrospective cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This multicentric retrospective study demonstrates that comprehensive andrological assessment substantially improves fertility outcomes in couples with male factor infertility by identifying treatable etiologies and enabling targeted interventions. Male factor infertility was identified in 68% of infertile couples, with major contributors including genital tract inflammation (48.8%), genital tract infection (43.1%), and hypospermatogenesis (16.7%), while 87% of patients with isolated male factor infertility presented with abnormal sperm parameters. The comprehensive diagnostic approach reduced idiopathic infertility diagnosis from approximately 15% to just 8%, and targeted etiological treatments—including antibiotics and probiotics for infections, low-dose corticosteroids and antioxidants for inflammation, FSH treatment for hypospermatogenesis, and varicocele repair surgery—were associated with spontaneous pregnancy achievement in 40.9% of treated couples overall and 32.5% of those with isolated male factor infertility. Notably, even couples with previous assisted reproductive technology (ART) failure achieved a spontaneous pregnancy rate of 36.1% following comprehensive diagnosis and etiological treatment, suggesting that bypassing thorough andrological evaluation in favor of immediate ART is suboptimal. These findings underscore that semen analysis alone is insufficient for predicting fertility outcomes and that systematic identification of treatable male factor pathologies significantly improves natural pregnancy rates.

title: A Case of Aneurysmal SAH and Stanford Type A Acute Aortic Dissection: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare concurrent presentation of Stanford Type A acute aortic dissection and aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage, highlighting an unexpectedly high association between intracranial aneurysms and aortic dissection. The patient underwent successful treatment with neck clipping for the cerebral aneurysms and total aortic arch replacement for the dissection, with postoperative clazosentan administration to prevent vasospasm-related complications. The analysis reveals that patients with acute aortic dissection have significantly larger aneurysms (mean 5.79 mm versus 3.04 mm in controls) and higher rates of aneurysms exceeding 7 mm, suggesting a shared underlying vascular pathology. Genetic conditions such as Juvenile Polyposis Syndrome-Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia, caused by SMAD4 mutations that compromise vascular integrity, may predispose patients to both aortic and cerebral aneurysms alongside vasculature malformations. These findings underscore the importance of comprehensive vascular imaging in patients presenting with either condition and suggest potential genetic screening in cases of concurrent aortic and intracranial pathology.

title: Case Report of Invasive Salmonella Superinfection of an Ovarian Endometrioma in a Patient With Systemic Lupus Erythematosus
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a rare invasive Salmonella enterica superinfection of an ovarian endometrioma in a 40-year-old patient with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), a condition that significantly increased her susceptibility to severe infection. The patient presented with gastrointestinal symptoms including nausea, watery diarrhea, and fever, which progressed to bacteremia and subsequent infection of a pre-existing ovarian endometrioma, causing persistent abdominal pain and fever. The immunocompromised state induced by SLE immunosuppressive therapies (azathioprine, methotrexate, mycophenolate) combined with the presence of endometriomas—which occur at increased prevalence in SLE patients—created ideal conditions for disseminated Salmonella infection and complications including cyst rupture and liver abscess formation. Management required multimodal therapy including empiric antibiotics (cefepime, ceftriaxone, piperacillin/tazobactam, ciprofloxacin, and levaquin) followed by surgical intervention with right salpingo-oophorectomy and left adnexal cyst drainage as the source of persistent infection could not be controlled with antibiotics alone. This case highlights the need for heightened clinical awareness of atypical Salmonella presentations in immunocompromised patients with gynecologic pathology and demonstrates that invasive Salmonella can affect diverse organ systems beyond typical gastrointestinal manifestations.

title: A Comparative Pilot Study on the Treatment of Uterine Fibroids Using Traditional Medicines and Intrauterine Enema Therapy
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids are hormone-dependent tumors arising from genetic mutations in myometrial stem cells, with key genetic alterations involving MED12, HMGA2, fumarate hydratase, and COL4A5/COL4A6, and are significantly promoted by estrogen and progesterone signaling. Symptomatic fibroids cause substantial clinical morbidity including heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pain, infertility, and serious pregnancy complications such as abortion, placental previa, and preterm birth. This pilot study evaluated traditional Ayurvedic medicine combined with intrauterine enema therapy (Uttar Basti) using medicinal oils including Kapharbuda-har oil as an alternative to conventional treatments like Ullipristal, which carry side effects. After three months of combined Ayurvedic treatment and Uttar Basti, all trial patients reported significant symptomatic relief and demonstrable reduction in fibroid size, with the mechanism involving stimulation of neuro-endocrine signaling and balancing of vata-dosha. The Uttar Basti procedure also showed therapeutic potential for related reproductive conditions including endometriosis, tubal blockage, and cervical erosion, supporting Ayurvedic medicine as a potentially effective complementary approach to fibroid management.

title: Multidisciplinary meetings for lower urinary tract symptoms and benign prostate hyperplasia
summary:
# Summary

This article examines multidisciplinary approaches to managing lower urinary tract symptoms and benign prostate hyperplasia (BPH), demonstrating that coordinated specialist meetings improve clinical decision-making across complex cases. BPH is frequently associated with significant comorbidities including urethral stricture, prostate cancer, and hemostasis disorders, necessitating comprehensive clinical evaluation. Multiple treatment modalities are recommended depending on presentation severity, including holmium laser endoscopic enucleation and medical therapy for symptomatic relief, along with minimally invasive options such as the Rezum system, prostatic artery embolization, and prostatic stent placement for BPH specifically. The article emphasizes that chronic urological conditions benefit substantially from multidisciplinary team consultation, enabling personalized treatment selection that accounts for patient comorbidities and clinical complexity, ultimately optimizing outcomes in this patient population.

title: In vitro fertilization-assisted pregnancy combined with high-intensity focused ultrasound treatment in patients with endometriosis and adenomyosis with successful pregnancy: a case report
summary: Endometriosis and adenomyosis are closely associated conditions causing chronic pain and infertility, with adenomyosis present in up to 90% of endometriosis patients and contributing significantly to reduced fertility. Both conditions are driven by hormonal and immune factors that activate a local inflammatory microenvironment, and endometriosis is particularly hormone-dependent, responsive to gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists (GnRH-a). High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) has emerged as a noninvasive therapeutic option for adenomyosis, inducing coagulative necrosis and achieving substantial uterine and lesion volume reduction—with combination HIFU and GnRH-a treatment proving more effective than either approach alone in reducing both structural pathology and clinical symptoms like dysmenorrhea and menorrhagia. Treatment outcomes depend on multiple factors including lesion characteristics (focal versus diffuse), depth and vascularization, with focal adenomyosis showing superior response rates and pregnancy rates varying from 17.6% to 59.6% depending on adenomyosis type. The case report demonstrates that multimodal therapy combining HIFU ablation, GnRH-a downregulation, and in vitro fertilization successfully achieves pregnancy in patients with advanced stage III/IV endometriosis, offering fertility-preserving benefits compared to hysterectomy while improving quality of life.

title: An unusual variant of May-Thurner syndrome leading to deep venous thrombosis: a case report
summary: May-Thurner syndrome (MTS) is a rare vascular condition in which compression of the left common iliac vein by the right common iliac artery causes endothelial damage, intimal hyperplasia, and venous obstruction that predisposes to deep vein thrombosis (DVT). This case report describes an unusual variant where the left common iliac artery itself compressed the left common iliac vein, resulting in extensive ileo-femoral DVT extending to the peroneal vein. The authors note that while MTS typically presents asymptomatically in approximately 70% of patients, it predominantly affects women in their third to fifth decades of life and can be complicated by life-threatening conditions such as phlegmasia cerulea dolens. Diagnostic imaging with CT or magnetic resonance venography demonstrates high sensitivity for detecting MTS, while intravascular ultrasound serves as the gold standard for visualizing intimal vascular changes and stenosis. The patient was successfully treated with heparin anticoagulation, mechanical thrombectomy, and endoluminal stenting with a Wallstent, followed by long-term oral anticoagulation with apixaban and clopidogrel, which alleviated lower extremity pain and swelling while preventing post-phlebitic syndrome. The authors highlight that endoluminal stenting provides superior outcomes for MTS variants and emphasize the need for heightened clinical suspicion, particularly in women presenting with lower extremity venous symptoms.

title: Fertility-sparing treatment for overweight patients with early-stage endometrial cancer or atypical endometrial hyperplasia under weight loss intervention
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: This study demonstrates that obesity is a major risk factor for early-stage endometrial cancer and atypical endometrial hyperplasia, with the metabolic mechanisms involving excessive estrogen exposure. Obesity and associated metabolic conditions like diabetes mellitus and polycystic ovary syndrome significantly impair treatment response and increase disease recurrence rates, particularly when BMI remains ≥25 kg/m². Fertility-sparing treatment combining progestin therapy, gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists, and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems is enhanced when paired with structured weight loss interventions. Notably, patients achieving specific weight loss thresholds—including >8% loss of percent body fat, >15% loss of body fat mass, and >18% loss of visceral fat area—demonstrated substantially shorter times to complete response, lower recurrence rates, and improved pregnancy outcomes. These findings indicate that weight loss of sufficient magnitude is an independent predictor of treatment success in overweight and obese women seeking fertility-sparing management of early endometrial malignancies. The study underscores the critical importance of integrating aggressive weight loss strategies into the treatment paradigm for this population.

title: A case report of sinus node–sparing hybrid ablation for refractory sinus tachycardia following cardioneuroablation for sinus node dysfunction
summary:
# Summary

This case report demonstrates a significant complication of cardioneuroablation, where treatment of functional bradycardia mediated by excessive vagal tone paradoxically triggered refractory inappropriate sinus tachycardia in a 33-year-old woman. The patient developed severe symptoms including dizziness, palpitations, exercise intolerance, and orthostatic intolerance that proved resistant to pharmacological management with bisoprolol and ivabradine. Sinus node–sparing hybrid ablation successfully treated the post-procedural tachycardia and associated postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome, restoring normal sinus rhythm and eliminating symptoms within three months, with sustained benefit at 18-month follow-up. The post-cardioneuroablation tachycardia likely results from unmasking of underlying arrhythmogenic reentry loops involving the crista terminalis and superior vena cava, potentially complicated by sympathetic overdrive and inflammatory or autoimmunological mechanisms. This case illustrates that sinus node–sparing hybrid ablation offers superior outcomes compared to traditional sinus node modification or ablation, with minimal pacemaker implantation requirements and promising long-term results in managing this serious ablation complication.

title: Global Prevalence of Infertility Attributable to Sex Chromosome Abnormalities: Insights from the Global Burden of Disease Study
summary: Sex chromosome abnormalities represent significant contributors to global infertility burden, with Klinefelter syndrome (caused by X chromosome nondisjunction during meiosis) accounting for approximately 3% of male infertility cases and 14% of azoospermia cases. Klinefelter syndrome manifests through multiple reproductive and metabolic complications including azoospermia, hypogonadism, gynecomastia, and increased risks for obesity, dyslipidemia, insulin resistance, and osteoporosis, while mutations in the USP26 gene may further contribute to disease pathogenesis. Turner syndrome similarly causes severe female infertility through gonadal dysgenesis and premature ovarian failure, alongside systemic effects including short stature and elevated mortality rates. Geographic variation in disease prevalence is notable, with male infertility attributable to Klinefelter syndrome showing the fastest growth in East Asia, while Turner syndrome-associated female infertility has declined in North America and Western Europe. Current therapeutic approaches include hormone replacement therapy for hypogonadism management and intracytoplasmic sperm injection for assisted reproduction in affected males, though early detection combined with multidisciplinary care remains essential for optimizing long-term outcomes and addressing associated cancer risks.

title: Four-Dimensional Transvaginal Ultrasonography as a First-Line Non-Invasive Follow-Up After Hysteroscopic Metroplasty in Dysmorphic Uterus Cases
summary: Dysmorphic uterus, formerly known as T-shaped uterus, leads to poor reproductive and obstetric outcomes including subfertility, miscarriage, preterm birth, and ectopic pregnancy through mechanisms involving a narrow uterine cavity with thickened lateral walls that reduce cavity volume and compromise endometrial receptivity, thereby impairing uterine expansion during pregnancy. Hysteroscopic metroplasty successfully treats dysmorphic uterus, achieving normalization of the uterine cavity in 100% of cases and improving pregnancy rates, with 45.2% of treated patients achieving pregnancy postoperatively. Four-dimensional transvaginal ultrasonography (4D-TVUSG) serves as an optimal non-invasive first-line follow-up tool for postoperative evaluation, offering superior advantages over traditional methods including hysterosalpingography and diagnostic hysteroscopy, as it provides real-time volumetric assessment of cavity remodeling without requiring anesthesia or incurring procedure-related complications such as infection, perforation, or pain. Three-dimensional ultrasonography is the preferred diagnostic method for assessing uterine morphology as it uniquely evaluates both the uterine cavity shape and the serosal contour, information that neither HSG nor conventional hysteroscopy alone can provide. 4D-TVUSG eliminates the adverse effects and risks associated with invasive diagnostic procedures, including pain, cramping, infection, and vasovagal reactions, while demonstrating higher diagnostic accuracy and lower cost compared to alternative methods, making it the ideal choice for non-invasive postoperative surveillance in dysmorphic uterus cases.

title: Assessing Uterine Fibroid Symptoms and Health-related Quality of Life among women at Korle Bu Teaching Hospital, Ghana: an analytical cross-sectional study using the UFS-QoL questionnaire
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study evaluated symptom burden and health-related quality of life (HR-QoL) in women with uterine fibroids using the UFS-QoL questionnaire at a Ghanaian teaching hospital. Uterine fibroids caused multiple debilitating symptoms, with heavy menstrual bleeding (average score 3.12) being the most prominent, followed by pelvic pressure/tightness (3.09) and fatigue (2.72), the latter likely reflecting anemia from excessive menstrual loss. Beyond physical symptoms, fibroids substantially impaired psychosocial well-being, with 83.4% of participants experiencing moderate-to-severe quality-of-life impact, including diminished sexual desire (correlation 0.872), self-consciousness about abdominal appearance, work productivity loss (25% reporting career limitations), and persistent anxiety about harboring a foreign entity. A strong inverse relationship emerged between symptom severity and overall HR-QoL scores (correlation −0.612), with Activities and Sexual Function subscales showing the strongest correlations with global HR-QoL. Patient-relevant factors such as age, parity, and diagnosis duration did not significantly predict symptom severity, whereas fibroid location within the uterus appeared to contribute to varying symptom presentations. These findings underscore that fibroid management requires addressing both the physical and psychosocial dimensions of disease burden to improve patient outcomes.

title: Hyperammonemia in Inherited Metabolic Diseases: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a neonatal presentation of ornithine transcarbamylase (OTC) deficiency, a urea cycle disorder causing severe hyperammonemia. Mutations in OTC gene impair the urea cycle's ability to detoxify ammonia, leading to toxic accumulation that crosses the blood-brain barrier and causes profound central nervous system damage through multiple mechanisms, including excessive glutamate-mediated excitotoxicity at NMDA receptors. The affected neonate developed progressive neurological deterioration within 24 hours of life—manifesting as hyperexcitability, seizures, and loss of consciousness—alongside disseminated intravascular coagulation, with plasma ammonia levels reaching 1058 µmol/L by day three, a concentration associated with grave prognosis and severe neurological sequelae despite aggressive metabolic management including high-glucose infusion to suppress protein catabolism. The case underscores critical failures in preventive care: a maternal family history of metabolic disorder and a confirmed pathogenic OTC mutation in the maternal lineage were not identified prenatally, preventing coordinated delivery at a tertiary facility with immediate dialysis access. The authors emphasize that improved outcomes depend on systematic implementation of detailed genetic questionnaires and family history assessment in prenatal care, cascade carrier testing in affected families, newborn screening via tandem mass spectrometry detecting elevated glutamine and citrulline markers, and comprehensive genetic counseling enabling preimplantation genetic diagnosis or prenatal testing to prevent recurrence in at-risk families.

title: Image-Guided Thrombin Injection for Post-procedural Hematomas in Various Surgical Settings: A Case Series
summary: Image-guided thrombin injection represents a safe and minimally invasive technique for managing post-procedural bleeding complications across diverse surgical settings. Thrombin functions by converting fibrinogen to fibrin, promoting rapid clot formation and hemostasis at bleeding sites identified through imaging guidance. The case series demonstrates successful treatment of hematomas following various procedures including robotic hernia repair, ovarian cystectomy, percutaneous nephrostomy, diagnostic endoscopy, and gastrostomy tube insertion, with outcomes superior to conventional surgical re-exploration in terms of reduced morbidity. Notably, image-guided thrombin injection effectively treated both common complications such as femoral and hepatic pseudoaneurysms and rare vascular injuries including retroperitoneal hematomas with active aortic extravasation. The technique has achieved hemostasis rates exceeding 94% in multiple procedural contexts and offers a viable alternative when embolization or surgery is contraindicated. These findings support broader adoption of image-guided thrombin injection as a first-line intervention for post-procedural hemorrhage management, with potential for standardized dosing and technique protocols to optimize safety and outcomes.

title: Colorectal-vaginal fistula after rectal cancer resection: international comparative cohort study of characteristics and treatment
summary: This international cohort study examines colorectal-vaginal fistula (CRVF), a serious complication following rectal cancer resection that presents with recurrent vaginitis, fecal incontinence, and chronic infections leading to sexual dysfunction and delayed cancer therapy. CRVF occurs more frequently in patients with lower-positioned tumors who undergo multivisceral resection including vaginal wall resection and primary diverting stoma placement, with diagnosis often delayed due to contained vaginal drainage preventing abdominal contamination. The vaginal drainage route paradoxically facilitates initial fluid management but prevents anastomotic healing and promotes fistula tract epithelialization, creating a persistent defect that causes recurrent leakage after stoma reversal. Defect circumference is the primary determinant of treatment success, with defects ≤25% amenable to conservative management using antibiotics or endoscopic intervention, while defects >50% consistently require permanent stoma. Restoration of bowel continuity places pressure on the fragile healed anastomosis, necessitating surgical approaches including redo anastomosis, dismantling, or abdominoperineal resection, with significantly lower one-year stoma-free survival in CRVF patients compared to those without fistula.

title: Physiotherapeutische Differenzialdiagnose von Rückenschmerzen im Zusammenhang mit Endometriose
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes a broad spectrum of pain symptoms through multiple pathophysiological mechanisms, with low back pain and chronic pelvic pain being particularly common presentations that often present as primary complaints in physiotherapy practice. The condition arises from ectopic endometrial tissue implantation outside the uterus, where it triggers inflammation, scarring, and infiltration of pelvic structures including the bladder, bowel, nerves, and blood vessels, with retrograde menstruation and lymphatic dissemination serving as proposed mechanisms of tissue spread. Pain in endometriosis operates through three distinct mechanisms—nociceptive pain from myofascial and inflammatory triggers, neuropathic pain from nerve demyelination and pudendal neuralgia, and neuroplas­tic pain from central sensitization—ultimately resulting in dysmenorrhea affecting 77% of patients, dyspareunia, urinary and bowel dysfunction, infertility, and fatigue syndrome. Multiple factors including age, diet, body mass index, hormonal contraceptive use, and physical activity associate with endometriosis development, while immune system dysregulation appears central to pathogenesis by promoting inflammation that paradoxically inhibits apoptosis of aberrant cells. The article emphasizes physiotherapeutic differential diagnosis is essential, as endometriosis frequently overlaps with fibromyalgia, migraine, irritable bowel syndrome, and other chronic pain syndromes that show higher prevalence in women and share disturbed central pain processing as a common mechanism.

title: Umbilical Pilonidal Sinus: a Case Report and Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary: Umbilical Pilonidal Sinus

Umbilical pilonidal sinus (UPS) is a rare condition characterized by chronic inflammation and accumulation of hair and epithelial debris within a sinus tract at the umbilicus. The pathophysiology involves abnormal hair eruption and subsequent inversion, triggering an inflammatory cascade that leads to granuloma formation and sinus development; bacterial translocation can secondarily cause cellulitis and abscess formation. Multiple risk factors predispose to UPS, including male sex, hirsutism (driven by androgen-based villous hair growth after puberty), deep navels, obesity (which increases skin creases and sweating), poor personal hygiene, and tight clothing, while smoking is specifically associated with recurrence. Management follows a stepwise approach beginning with risk factor control (improved hygiene, hair removal, weight reduction) in conservative cases treated with local care, antibiotics, and dressing, with surgical options including umbilectomy or umbilicus-preserving excision reserved for recurrent or complicated cases; surgical management achieves a lower recurrence rate (1.4%) compared to conservative management (7.9%). Histopathological findings consistently demonstrate hair shafts, keratin, epithelial debris, chronic inflammatory infiltrates, granulomas, and vascular changes within the sinus tissue.

title: Developing standardized informed consent for hysterectomy and vulva cancer surgery
summary: This study presents a standardized informed consent framework for hysterectomy and vulvar cancer surgery. Laparoscopic-robotic hysterectomy is indicated for multiple benign and malignant uterine conditions including leiomyomas, adenomyosis, abnormal uterine bleeding, intractable dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, endometriosis, cervical intraepithelial neoplasia, endometrial hyperplasia, pelvic inflammatory disease, and pelvic organ prolapse. The consent documentation comprehensively addresses potential surgical complications including bleeding, pain, thromboembolism, bowel obstruction, surgical wound infection, urinary disorders, fistula formation, and neurological deficits. For vulvar cancer management, surgical options encompass wide local excision, vulvectomy, and sentinel or inguinofemoral lymph node biopsy, with significant postoperative morbidity including wound breakdown, lymphedema, infection, and quality-of-life impacts. The framework identifies key surgical risk factors (age >70 years, prolonged operations, extensive surgery, limited mobility, malnourishment, hypertension, diabetes, and immunological disorders) and intraoperative contingencies such as conversion to open surgery due to severe bleeding or adhesions. This comprehensive consent approach ensures patients understand the indications, complications, and management strategies for both hysterectomy and vulvar cancer procedures.

title: Analysis of Risk Scoring for Placenta Accreta Spectrum and Its Impact on Maternal and Fetal Outcomes
summary: Placenta accreta spectrum (PAS) is a serious obstetric condition characterized by abnormal placental invasion into the myometrium, primarily caused by defective decidualization, which can result in life-threatening complications including massive hemorrhage, hypovolemic shock, metabolic acidosis, coagulopathy, and multi-organ dysfunction. The Placenta Accreta Index (PAI), a risk-scoring system integrating clinical parameters (such as history of previous cesarean delivery and placental location) with ultrasound findings (myometrial thickness, placental lacunae, and intraplacental features), demonstrated exceptional diagnostic performance with 100% sensitivity and 97.3% specificity for detecting PAS.
