Key ultrasound markers associated with PAS include loss of the retroplacental clear zone, myometrial thinning (<1 mm), Grade 2-3 placental lacunae, and bridging vessels, with histopathological confirmation showing absence of decidua and direct villous contact with myometrium. Prenatal identification of PAS using the PAI enables appropriate delivery planning and resource allocation, with high PAI scores guiding physicians to anticipate massive postpartum hemorrhage and prepare for interventions such as uterine artery embolization or conservative surgery. The study found that 60.7% of cases had previous cesarean sections and 70% had concurrent placenta previa, both major risk factors for PAS development. Systematic prenatal screening with the PAI, combined with a multidisciplinary team approach, significantly improved maternal and neonatal outcomes, with excellent results in preventing the complications historically associated with undiagnosed PAS.

title: Obturator Neuropathy Following Minimally Invasive Pelvic Surgery: A Report of Two Cases
summary:
# Summary

Obturator nerve injury (ONI) is an uncommon but clinically significant complication arising from the lumbar plexus (L2-L4) that can occur following minimally invasive pelvic surgeries, including robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy and laparoscopic radical hysterectomy for cancer treatment. The nerve's anatomical course along the lateral pelvic wall and posterior to lymph node packets makes it vulnerable to injury through multiple mechanisms, including direct trauma, intraoperative stretch, thermal injury, and excessive medial traction during dissection. Clinical presentation typically includes medial thigh pain, adduction weakness, sensory changes, and gait abnormalities, with diagnosis confirmed through electrodiagnostic testing such as electromyography. Both cases presented demonstrated complete functional recovery within six months following intensive rehabilitation programs that incorporated neuromuscular electrical stimulation, electromyographic biofeedback, and muscle strengthening exercises. Prevention strategies emphasize proper patient positioning, bipolar rather than monopolar cautery use, and careful nerve identification and preservation during endopelvic dissection, while early intraoperative recognition and microsurgical repair offer the best prognosis for optimal recovery.

title: Gynecologic surgery for benign disease: Preserving reproductive potential
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary: Gynecologic Surgery for Benign Disease and Reproductive Preservation

This review addresses surgical management of common gynecologic conditions affecting reproductive-aged women, including uterine fibroids (affecting ~50% of this population), endometriosis with ovarian endometriomas (17-44% of endometriosis cases), polycystic ovary syndrome (~10% prevalence), and adenomyosis (20-30% prevalence). The article emphasizes that standard surgical techniques can compromise fertility through multiple mechanisms: dilation and curettage causes intrauterine adhesions (particularly with sharp curettage or repeated procedures), loop electrosurgical excision for cervical pathology increases pregnancy loss and preterm birth risk (especially with shorter conception intervals and larger excision areas), and hysteroscopic myomectomy creates adhesions proportional to the number of fibroids removed. For endometriosis, surgical excision of ovarian endometriomas reduces ovarian reserve and anti-Müllerian hormone levels, while conservative approaches like sclerotherapy preserve more ovarian function; salpingectomy for hydrosalpinx is preferred over salpingostomy to prevent reocclusion and preserve IVF outcomes. The review advocates for minimally invasive approaches and tissue-sparing techniques including barbed sutures for myomectomy, cold scissor adhesiolysis, and preventive strategies such as intrauterine balloons with estrogen therapy or adhesion-reducing products. For PCOS management, letrozole emerges as superior first-line ovulation induction therapy compared to laparoscopic ovarian drilling, achieving equivalent live birth rates with fewer adhesion complications.

title: Uterine rupture in pregnancy over 5 years: A retrospective descriptive study
summary: This retrospective study demonstrates that uterine rupture, though rare, represents a severe obstetric emergency with substantial maternal and fetal consequences. Prior cesarean section with uterine scarring emerged as the predominant risk factor, present in 84.6% of rupture cases, while labor induction and oxytocin augmentation significantly elevated rupture risk and were employed in the majority of affected pregnancies. Clinical presentation was nonspecific but consistently included abnormal cardiotocography (76.9% of cases), abdominal and shoulder pain, vaginal bleeding, and fetal heart rate abnormalities including bradycardia and prolonged decelerations. Fetal complications were severe, with fetal extrusion occurring in 69.2% of cases and one perinatal death recorded, alongside high maternal morbidity including postpartum hemorrhage affecting 61.5% of patients requiring transfusions. Additional risk factors identified included labor induction, congenital uterine anomalies, fetal macrosomia, prior myometrial surgery including myomectomy, short interpregnancy intervals, and notably, deep pelvic endometriosis. The findings underscore the critical importance of cautious management of labor induction and augmentation in high-risk pregnancies, particularly those with prior uterine surgery, and emphasize continuous fetal heart rate monitoring for early detection and intervention in this life-threatening complication.

title: Empirical Antibiotic Therapy in Chronic Endometritis With and Without Focal Lesions: A Prospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis (CE) is an immune-mediated condition associated with infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, and recurrent implantation failure, characterized by abnormal expression of adhesion molecules and chemokines that promote B cell infiltration into the endometrium. These infiltrating B cells differentiate into stromal plasma cells that produce excessive immunoglobulin G, which impedes embryo implantation. The study demonstrates that both antibiotic therapy (ofloxacin and metronidazole) and hysteroscopic polypectomy effectively reduce plasma cell density and key histopathological features of CE including stromal edema and cell compaction, with notable effectiveness in cases without focal lesions. Endometrial polyps and cesarean scar defects were significantly associated with CE development, and spindled transformation of stromal cells with edema correlated strongly with elevated plasma cell density. These findings suggest that empirical antibiotic therapy combined with surgical management of focal lesions represents an effective dual approach to treating chronic endometritis and potentially improving reproductive outcomes in affected patients.

title: Adult Small Intestinal Intussusception Due to Bleeding Lipoma: A Rare Case Report (with Video)
journal: Reports - Clinical Practice and Surgical Cases
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare instance of adult small intestinal intussusception caused by a bleeding submucosal lipoma in a 55-year-old female patient. A lipoma, a benign fatty tumor, acted as a lead point that triggered intussusception—the telescoping of bowel segments—and caused acute gastrointestinal bleeding manifesting as melena, severe anemia, abdominal pain, and gastrointestinal symptoms including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. While endometriosis and inflammatory bowel disease are recognized causes of intussusception, benign lipomas represent an uncommon etiology in adults. Untreated intussusception can progress to serious complications including intestinal ischemia, necrosis, and perforation; therefore, surgical resection was performed as the definitive treatment to relieve the obstruction, control bleeding, and enable histological examination. The patient's anticoagulation therapy for atrial fibrillation was managed with vitamin K and blood transfusions to achieve hemodynamic stability during the acute phase. This case highlights the importance of considering benign tumors as potential lead points in adult intussusception and the critical role of prompt surgical intervention in preventing life-threatening complications.

title: Case Report: Rare Müllerian malformation assisted by digital 3D CT reconstruction technology for diagnosis and treatment
summary:
# Summary

Müllerian duct anomalies (MDAs) are congenital malformations resulting from incomplete development or abnormal fusion of the Müllerian ducts during embryogenesis, affecting four to seven percent of the general population and presenting clinically with primary amenorrhea, cyclic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, infertility, and recurrent pregnancy loss. This case report describes a rare presentation of a left rudimentary horn uterus with absent right ovary in a 12-year-old girl whose obstructive anatomy led to increased intrauterine pressure, retrograde menstruation, and associated adenomyosis—complications that underscore the importance of early surgical intervention. Digital 3D CT reconstruction technology proved instrumental in precisely visualizing the complex spatial anatomy of the rudimentary horn and guiding surgical planning, enabling successful laparoscopic resection of the obstructed rudimentary horn and fallopian tube, which completely resolved the patient's cyclic pain. The pathophysiological cascade linking outflow obstruction to adenomyosis and potential endometriosis demonstrates that timely surgical correction can prevent these secondary complications while preserving functional ovarian tissue for future fertility. This case illustrates how advanced imaging modalities such as contrast-enhanced CT with 3D reconstruction, along with MRI and 3D ultrasonography, enhance diagnostic accuracy and facilitate individualized surgical management of rare Müllerian malformations.

title: Bone mineral density with elagolix plus add-back therapy in women with heavy menstrual bleeding and uterine fibroids: open-label and post-treatment results of a 60-month phase 3 trial
summary: Elagolix, a gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonist, combined with hormonal add-back therapy (estradiol and norethindrone acetate) effectively reduces menstrual blood loss and treats uterine fibroids in premenopausal women, offering a noninvasive alternative to hysterectomy. The add-back therapy is crucial because it mitigates the hypoestrogenic effects associated with GnRH antagonists, including bone mineral density loss and hot flushes. While elagolix plus add-back therapy caused decreased bone mineral density in some patients (most frequent treatment-emergent adverse event at 14.0%), bone loss was modest (<2% at most anatomical sites) and largely reversible following treatment cessation. The therapy was well tolerated over 48 months with no new safety signals, and notably, no cases of ovarian cysts or pregnancies occurred. Black women showed disproportionately higher incidence of uterine fibroids and associated heavy menstrual bleeding compared to White women, underscoring the clinical importance of this therapeutic option. These findings support elagolix plus add-back therapy as a viable long-term medical treatment for fibroid-associated heavy menstrual bleeding with manageable bone safety considerations and appropriate monitoring.

title: Clinical characteristics and social burden of menstrual migraine in Japan: an online-based cross-sectional questionnaire study
journal: The Journal of Headache and Pain
summary: Menstrual migraine is triggered by estrogen withdrawal and hormonal fluctuations during the perimenstrual period, resulting in significantly higher pain intensity and longer pain duration compared to non-menstrual migraine. Patients with menstrual migraine experience substantially greater disability, with elevated MIDAS and MIBS-4 scores, reduced quality of life on the MSQ, and increased presenteeism and work impairment assessed by WPAI-M metrics. The condition is associated with higher rates of comorbid symptoms including nausea, photophobia, phonophobia, osmophobia, sleep disorders, premenstrual syndrome, dysmenorrhea, and uterine pathologies such as fibroids, endometriosis, and adenomyosis. Common migraine triggers in menstrual migraine patients include stress, fatigue, climate change, and emotional fluctuations, with these triggers reported more frequently in the menstrual migraine group. Treatment predominantly involves NSAIDs, acetaminophen, and triptans, with menstrual migraine patients demonstrating significantly increased medication usage overall. This Japanese cross-sectional study highlights the substantial clinical burden and social impact of menstrual migraine, demonstrating its association with multiple gynecological and neurological comorbidities.

title: Reporting of management and outcomes in the hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn: a systematic literature review
summary:
# Summary

Hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN) occurs when maternal antibodies cross the placenta and destroy fetal red blood cells, with disease severity most commonly associated with anti-Rh(D), anti-K, and anti-Rh(c) antibodies. Maternal alloimmunization develops following sensitizing events such as incompatible blood transfusions or feto-maternal hemorrhage, though anti-D immunoglobulin prophylaxis has substantially reduced Rh D disease incidence in developed countries. Current management of alloimmunized pregnancies relies on antenatal monitoring via fetal ultrasound and middle cerebral artery Doppler measurements, with intrauterine transfusions serving as the primary treatment, supplemented by non-invasive strategies including therapeutic plasma exchange and intravenous immunoglobulin. Severe HDFN manifests with serious complications including fetal hydrops, hyperbilirubinemia progressing to kernicterus, anemia, jaundice, and neurological impairment, while survivors face long-term morbidities including neurodevelopmental impairment and behavioral problems. Postnatal management involves exchange transfusion and phototherapy, with total serum bilirubin serving as a key severity indicator. This systematic review highlights that while perinatal survival has improved through modern interventions, affected women experience significant physical and emotional burden, particularly those requiring recurrent procedures in subsequent pregnancies.

title: Obstetric Rectal Buttonhole Tear: Case Series, Literature Review, and Management Recommendations
summary:
# Summary

Obstetric rectal buttonhole tear (ORBT) is a rare but serious complication of vaginal delivery, characterized by isolated full-thickness lacerations of the rectal mucosa and vaginal epithelium while preserving the anal sphincter complex. The condition arises from excessive mechanical distension of the rectovaginal septum during fetal head descent and is associated with significant morbidity including rectovaginal fistula, fecal incontinence, infection, and wound dehiscence, with increased risk following forceps-assisted delivery, operative vaginal delivery, and rapid fetal descent in primiparous women. Early diagnosis through mandatory postpartum rectal examination and examination under anesthesia is critical, as primary transvaginal layered repair performed within 12 hours by experienced surgeons using polyglactin 910 sutures achieves favorable outcomes in over 80% of cases, whereas delayed diagnosis necessitates colostomy. Comprehensive postoperative management includes broad-spectrum antibiotics (predominantly cephalosporin-metronidazole combinations), stool softeners like lactulose, low-residue diets, and bowel rest to prevent wound complications and fecal impaction.

title: Spontaneous uterine rupture due to adenomyosis in a nongravid, nulliparous woman: A case report
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a rare but catastrophic complication of adenomyosis: spontaneous uterine rupture in a nulliparous, nongravid woman. Adenomyosis weakens the myometrial wall through multiple pathophysiologic mechanisms, including disruption of myometrial fiber continuity by ectopic endometrial glands and stroma, increased matrix metalloproteinase activity, aberrant angiogenesis, and imbalances between cellular proliferation and apoptosis, which together compromise the uterus's structural integrity and tensile strength. Superimposed ischemia and focal hemorrhagic necrosis created vulnerable zones leading to multiple transmural ruptures at the uterine cornua and posterior wall, resulting in massive intra-abdominal hemorrhage, hemorrhagic shock, and hematologic complications including coagulopathy and leukocytosis. The patient presented with acute severe abdominal pain and required emergency treatment with rapid resuscitation and surgical hemorrhage control, ultimately necessitating total hysterectomy due to the extent of myometrial damage. This case underscores the importance of proactive management of severe adenomyosis in young women, combining medical therapy and interventional options to prevent catastrophic outcomes.

title: Congenital uterine anomalies and congenital heart disease: An underappreciated association?
journal: International Journal of Cardiology Congenital Heart Disease
summary: This study reveals a previously underappreciated association between congenital uterine anomalies (CUAs) and congenital heart disease (CHD), particularly in patients with more complex cardiac pathology. The analysis demonstrates that patients with moderate or complex CHD have significantly higher prevalence of CUAs than the general population, with the complexity of uterine anomalies correlating with cardiac complexity—for instance, arcuate uterus appears predominantly in simple CHD while more severe anomalies associate with complex CHD. The relationship appears mediated largely by shared genetic syndromes, including VACTERL association, disorders of laterality, and multiple single-gene conditions such as Loeys-Dietz and Marfan syndromes, each of which can affect both cardiac and Müllerian duct development. Beyond this genetic connection, CUAs carry substantial gynecological and obstetrical morbidity, including primary dysmenorrhea, infertility, recurrent pregnancy loss, and increased cesarean delivery risk, with obstructive anomalies additionally predisposing to endometriosis through retrograde menstruation. The study emphasizes that MRI or ultrasound screening should be considered in female CHD patients, particularly those with genetic syndromes, to enable appropriate pre-pregnancy counseling and reduce lifelong reproductive complications including chronic pelvic pain and infertility from ascending infections and adhesion formation.

title: Acute Pulmonary Embolism Treated by Surgery and Complicated With a Pulmonary Artery Pseudoaneurysm: A Case Report
summary: This case report describes a 48-year-old woman with a submassive pulmonary embolism complicated by an intracardiac thrombus straddling a patent foramen ovale, originating from a deep vein thrombosis of the right superficial femoral vein. Given the high risk of systemic embolization with pharmacologic thrombolysis, the patient was treated with surgical pulmonary thrombectomy under cardiopulmonary bypass, which successfully removed extensive bilateral pulmonary and intracardiac emboli and rapidly improved right ventricular function. However, the procedure was complicated by a left lower pulmonary artery pseudoaneurysm, likely caused by Fogarty catheter trauma during distal embolectomy, which was successfully managed by endovascular embolization. The case illustrates that while surgical thrombectomy offers superior outcomes in high-risk pulmonary embolism by addressing acute right ventricular failure and preventing thromboembolic recurrence, it carries procedural risks that require careful technical consideration and multidisciplinary management. Long-term anticoagulation with apixaban maintained the patient's normal clinical status nine months postoperatively, demonstrating favorable outcomes when surgical intervention is appropriately selected and complications are promptly managed.

title: Hematocolpos Presenting as Acute Urinary Retention: When Gynecologic Pathology Mimics Urologic Disease
summary:
# Summary

Imperforate hymen, a rare congenital failure of the vaginal plate to canalize during embryologic development, is the most frequent cause of genital outflow obstruction in females and typically presents at puberty with hematocolpos—the accumulation of retained menstrual blood in the vagina. This retained blood creates a significant mass effect that produces the characteristic triad of primary amenorrhea, cyclic pelvic and lower abdominal pain, and abdominal distension, and can uncommonly precipitate acute urinary complications including urinary retention, urinary frequency, and hydronephrosis, affecting up to 20% of affected individuals. The diagnosis is supported by imaging modalities such as pelvic ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging, which demonstrate the characteristic midline cystic pelvic mass, and clinical examination may reveal a pathognomonic bulging bluish hymenal membrane at the vaginal introitus. Cruciate hymenectomy or hymenotomy, which surgically creates a patent outflow tract, is the definitive treatment and rapidly resolves urinary and gynecologic symptoms while preserving reproductive function, though careful surgical technique with gentle evacuation of retained blood is critical to prevent serious complications such as retrograde menstrual flow into the fallopian tubes, which can lead to endometriosis and pelvic adhesions. Postoperative management includes antibiotic prophylaxis and topical estrogen application to reduce hymenal reclosure, with cruciate incision design and tissue excision further minimizing re-adhesion risk. This case exemplifies the importance of considering gynecologic pathology in the differential diagnosis of adolescent females presenting with acute urinary retention, particularly when accompanied by primary amenorrhea and cyclic pelvic pain.

title: Pregnancy Outcomes With and Without Adenomyomectomy in Infertile Patients With Adenomyosis: A Single‐Center Retrospective Study
journal: The Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Research
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis causes infertility through multiple mechanisms, including endometrial thinning, abnormal endometrial function, and excessive uterine peristalsis that impair embryo implantation and transport. The condition is associated with dysmenorrhea, hypermenorrhea, and significantly elevated miscarriage risk, with diffuse adenomyosis conferring particularly poor pregnancy outcomes including second-trimester miscarriage and preeclampsia. Adenomyomectomy using triple- or double-flap techniques substantially improves dysmenorrhea, increases postoperative endometrial thickness, and enhances fertility outcomes compared to conservative management alone, particularly benefiting patients with severe diffuse disease. However, adenomyomectomy carries risks of uterine rupture, placenta accreta, and postpartum hemorrhage, which are influenced by factors including adenomyosis remnants, myometrial defect size, and uterine wall reconstruction methods. For patients with focal adenomyosis and manageable symptoms, non-surgical approaches combined with assisted reproductive technology achieve favorable pregnancy rates of approximately 70 percent. This study demonstrates that surgical intervention followed by ART represents an effective strategy for managing adenomyosis-related infertility, though careful postoperative monitoring is essential given increased perinatal complications.

title: A reclassification and comprehensive examination of uterine arteriovenous malformation: insights derived from a case series
summary:
# Summary

Uterine arteriovenous malformation (UAVM) is a rare vascular abnormality that presents as abnormal direct connections between uterine arteries and veins, causing severe vaginal bleeding and potentially life-threatening complications including anemia, hypovolemic shock, and congestive heart failure. Primary UAVM results from abnormal embryonic vascular development and manifests with more severe and recurrent symptoms, while secondary UAVM arises from iatrogenic trauma such as induced abortion, dilation and curettage, or cesarean section, triggering excessive activation of endothelial cells, fibroblasts, and smooth muscle cells. Non-pregnancy-related UAVM can be caused by underlying pathology including uterine fibroids, trophoblastic disease, endometrial cancer, and cervical lesions. Ultrasound serves as the primary diagnostic tool, with angiography as the gold standard, while treatment approaches vary by UAVM type and reproductive status, ranging from uterine artery embolization and balloon tamponade for acute hemorrhage control to hysterectomy with bilateral internal iliac artery ligation for patients with completed reproductive needs. The case series underscores the clinical importance of recognizing UAVM in women of childbearing age with abnormal vaginal bleeding, particularly following pregnancy-related procedures or in association with uterine pathology, to prevent serious hemorrhagic complications.

title: Approach to pelvic venous disorders
summary: Pelvic venous disorders (PeVDs) are frequently misdiagnosed yet common causes of chronic pelvic pain and varicose veins, particularly in women. These conditions arise from two primary pathophysiological mechanisms: venous reflux and venous obstruction, both of which lead to varicose veins and venous hypertension in the pelvic vasculature. The resulting venous hypertension and hemodynamic disturbances perpetuate the chronic pain symptoms that characterize these disorders. Interventional treatments, particularly embolization and stenting procedures, have demonstrated effectiveness in managing symptoms and improving patient outcomes. Recognition of PeVDs as a distinct clinical entity is important for family physicians, as the misdiagnosis of these conditions may delay appropriate treatment and perpetuate patient suffering from chronic pelvic pain.

title: Recommendations for recognizing and diagnosing Acute Hepatic Porphyria in atypical patient populations
summary:
# Summary

Acute hepatic porphyria (AHP) is a group of four metabolic disorders caused by genetic defects in the heme synthesis pathway, with Acute Intermittent Porphyria (HMBS gene mutations, 67% of cases) being most common, followed by Variegate Porphyria (PPOX gene, 22%) and Hereditary Coproporphyria (CPOX gene, 11%). Disease manifestations result from accumulation of neurotoxic heme precursors including porphobilinogen and delta-aminolaevulinic acid, presenting with abdominal pain, muscle weakness, neurological symptoms, and notably higher rates of obstetric/gynecological complications including miscarriage, low birth weight, and infertility if untreated. Diagnosis requires combination biochemical and genetic testing performed during acute attacks, with diagnosis more frequently achieved by gastroenterologists, hematologists, and neurologists than reproductive specialists. Current treatments include acute phase hemin therapy and the recently FDA-approved RNA-silencing agent givosiran for disease prevention, with patients also reporting associations between symptom exacerbation and menses or pregnancy.

title: Anal sphincter reconstruction for fecal incontinence: Techniques, outcomes, and future directions
summary:
# Summary

This review examines anal sphincter reconstruction techniques for treating fecal incontinence, a condition arising from multiple etiologies including obstetric trauma, surgical injury, aging, and pelvic infections. Fecal incontinence causes significant psychosocial distress and impaired quality of life through mechanisms involving muscle atrophy, fibrotic scarring, and functional sphincter dysfunction affecting the internal anal sphincter, external anal sphincter, and puborectalis muscle. Conventional approaches like overlapping sphincteroplasty provide immediate anatomical restoration but are limited by progressive muscle atrophy and scar tissue formation, necessitating evaluation through endoanal ultrasound, magnetic resonance imaging, and anal manometry. Emerging regenerative strategies including mesenchymal stem cell injection, nanofat enrichment, autologous myocyte transplantation, and dynamic muscle transposition (graciloplasty, gluteus maximus transposition) aim to overcome long-term limitations by promoting tissue regeneration and enhancing muscle quality. Sacral nerve stimulation represents a promising neuromodulation approach that improves sphincter contraction, pelvic floor coordination, and rectal sensation with reported efficacy rates around 67%, while artificial anal sphincter implantation offers mechanical restoration despite complications from peri-prosthetic tissue changes. These multifaceted approaches reflect the evolution toward integrating structural repair with regenerative and neuromodulatory strategies to achieve durable continence restoration.

title: Spontaneous ureteral rupture during pregnancy: a case report and literature review
summary: Spontaneous ureteral rupture during pregnancy is a rare but serious condition typically caused by increased intraluminal pressure from urinary calculi, ureteral stricture, or tumor obstruction, with pregnancy itself contributing through mechanical compression of the enlarging uterus and physiological hydronephrosis and hydroureter. Predisposing factors including endometriosis, antiphospholipid syndrome, and adhesions from prior abdominal surgery can increase rupture risk. If untreated, urine extravasation from the ruptured ureter leads to life-threatening complications including urinoma formation, sepsis, and renal failure. Clinical presentation includes acute flank pain and nausea, with diagnosis confirmed through imaging modalities such as intravenous pyelography, magnetic resonance urography, or ultrasonography. Management strategies include percutaneous nephrostomy and ureteral stent placement, both of which effectively treat the condition and preserve renal function while preventing infectious complications through prophylactic antibiotics. This case illustrates the importance of recognizing and promptly treating spontaneous ureteral rupture in pregnant patients to avoid severe morbidity.

title: Unilateral Facial Swelling as the Inaugural Sign of Anti-NPX2 Dermatomyositis: A Diagnostic Challenge in Oral Medicine
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents anti-NXP2 dermatomyositis presenting with the unusual inaugural sign of unilateral facial swelling in a 41-year-old female patient, a presentation that delayed diagnosis and posed significant diagnostic challenges in oral medicine. Anti-NXP2 autoantibodies, identified through myositis-specific antibody panel testing, enabled definitive diagnosis of this inflammatory myopathy characterized by progressive muscle weakness in the upper and lower limbs, erythematous skin plaques, and markedly elevated creatine phosphokinase levels. The patient's clinical course revealed that cutaneous manifestations can precede myositic involvement, particularly in amyopathic forms, complicating early recognition when orofacial swelling is the primary presenting feature. Treatment with corticosteroid therapy (intravenous methylprednisolone and oral prednisolone), methotrexate, and intravenous immunoglobulins successfully controlled the disease, though the patient experienced relapse requiring additional immunoglobulin administration. This case highlights the importance of considering anti-NXP2 dermatomyositis in the differential diagnosis of orofacial manifestations, particularly unilateral facial swelling, as early recognition prevents delays in immunosuppressive therapy and mitigates the risk of severe systemic complications including pulmonary and cardiac involvement.

title: Robotic-assisted surgery in benign gynecology: single-center experience with 106 patients
summary: This single-center study evaluated robotic-assisted surgery using the Da Vinci Xi system in 106 patients (mean age 50 years) with benign gynecological diseases including uterine fibroids, endometriosis, adenomyosis, and endometrial hyperplasia. Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory condition with ectopic lesions commonly found in the ovaries, pelvic peritoneum, rectovaginal septum, bowel, bladder, and pouch of Douglas, causes fibrosis and adhesions that increase surgical complexity, particularly in deep infiltrating endometriosis. Robotic-assisted hysterectomy was the most common procedure (102 of 106 cases), often combined with salpingectomy (72.5%), adnexectomy (25%), and adhesiolysis (21%), with all cases completed successfully without conversion to open surgery. The study found that greater uterine weight and higher body mass index were associated with increased surgical complexity, though robotic console time decreased by 28% in more complex cases, suggesting improved operative efficiency with experience. While no serious adverse events occurred, the cohort experienced expected complications including two infected vaginal hematomas, one pulmonary embolism, and one deep vein thrombosis, all managed with appropriate medical intervention. These findings demonstrate that robotic-assisted surgery is an effective and safe approach for treating multiple benign gynecological pathologies, with improved surgical efficiency despite increased patient and disease complexity.

title: Etiological and associated factors of thin endometrium among infertile women
summary:
# Summary

Thin endometrium is a significant reproductive pathology that impairs embryo implantation and contributes to infertility, with endometrial thickness below 8.2 mm linked to reduced live birth rates and increased miscarriage risk. Multiple etiological factors cause or associate with thin endometrium, including infectious disease (tuberculosis and chronic endometritis), iatrogenic trauma (dilation and curettage, adhesion lysis, and repeated curettage), advancing age, parity, and conditions such as uterine fibroids and endometriosis. The underlying mechanisms involve impaired endometrial receptivity, which is fundamental for successful implantation and pregnancy continuation, with vascular abnormalities such as elevated uterine artery resistance further compromising endometrial development and associated with adverse assisted reproductive technology outcomes. Current therapeutic approaches remain limited, with low-dose aspirin and estrogen therapy showing only modest efficacy in enhancing endometrial development. These findings underscore the critical role of endometrial integrity in reproductive success and highlight the need for improved interventions to restore adequate endometrial thickness in infertile women.

title: Past, Present, and Future: A Review of Uterus Transplant
summary:
# Summary: Uterus Transplantation as Treatment for Absolute Uterine Factor Infertility

Uterus transplantation represents a novel therapeutic approach for absolute uterine factor infertility (AUFI), a condition caused by congenital abnormalities such as Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser syndrome, or acquired conditions including hysterectomy, uterine fibroids, Asherman syndrome, and endometriosis-related pathology. The procedure uniquely enables pregnancy and delivery in women with AUFI, though it requires careful immunosuppressive management combining induction therapy with thymoglobulin and maintenance through calcineurin inhibitors and antimetabolites, with mycophenolate mofetil requiring discontinuation prior to embryo transfer due to teratogenic risk. Acute cellular rejection can be managed effectively with corticosteroids, though emerging biomarkers including specific genes (AGHDIB, BASP1, FCGR3A/B, KLF4, PTPN6) and cytokines (granzyme B, IL1β) are being investigated for improved graft monitoring, while clinical rejection signs include increased uterine volume and watery discharge. Procedural advances such as robotic-assisted hysterectomy in living donors reduce blood loss and enhance graft viability, though ureteric injury remains a recognized complication. Recipients face additional challenges including pre-eclampsia development in thirty percent of cases with associated kidney injury risk, and the requirement for in vitro fertilization since Fallopian tubes are excluded from the transplant, with embryo exhaustion affecting approximately twenty percent of US center patients.

title: Laparoscopic excision of the sciatic nerve endometriosis
summary: This case report describes a 40-year-old woman with adenomyosis presenting with heavy menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and atypical sciatic nerve pain caused by an endometriotic cystic nodule compressing the right sciatic nerve at its pelvic exit. While initial treatment with a levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine device (LNG-IUD) successfully resolved the menstrual symptoms, the sciatic pain persisted due to external nerve compression rather than perineural infiltration. Laparoscopic excision of the endometriotic nodule, performed using retroperitoneal pelvic surgery principles, successfully treated the sciatic nerve endometriosis and was followed by postoperative dienogest therapy for six months. Intensive physical therapy was critical for complete functional recovery, with the patient regaining muscle strength and normal nerve function over a two-year follow-up period. The case emphasizes that extrapelvic endometriosis involving the sciatic nerve can cause severe neurological complications including foot drop, and that timely surgical intervention combined with rehabilitation is essential for optimal outcomes in such uncommon presentations.

title: Ultrasound-guided ethanol sclerotherapy versus laparoscopic surgery for endometriomas: a randomized clinical trial in a real-world setting
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian endometriomas, affecting approximately 6.1% of reproductive-age women, are associated with significant morbidity including chronic pain and infertility. This randomized clinical trial compared ultrasound-guided ethanol sclerotherapy with laparoscopic cystectomy for treating these cysts and found that sclerotherapy achieved superior pain resolution in 89.1% of cases versus 65.7% with surgery, while maintaining lower complication rates with predominantly Grade I-II adverse events compared to Grade III complications in the surgical group. Recurrence rates were comparable between both approaches (approximately 26%), though larger cyst size (>6.5 cm) and shorter ethanol retention times (<10 minutes) were associated with increased recurrence risk. Notably, sclerotherapy demonstrated higher procedural success rates (72.6%), substantially lower hospital costs (€472 versus €2,128), and was performed as an outpatient procedure in nearly all cases, establishing it as a safe and cost-effective alternative to surgery for endometrioma management in real-world clinical settings.

title: Isthmocele risk in repeated cesarean: the diagnostic and clinical role of morphometric parameters
summary:
# Summary

Repeated cesarean deliveries are a major risk factor for isthmocele (cesarean scar defect) formation, with prevalence increasing dramatically from 42.9% after one cesarean to 100% after four procedures. The number of cesarean sections correlates strongly with characteristic morphometric changes in the niche, including increased depth, length, and depth-to-residual myometrial thickness (RMT) ratio, alongside reduced RMT. Three or more prior cesareans independently predict isthmocele development, and morphometric parameters—particularly niche length ≥5 mm and elevated depth-to-RMT ratios—distinguish symptomatic from asymptomatic cases. Isthmoceles are associated with serious obstetric complications including scar dehiscence, uterine rupture, and placental adhesion disorders, as well as gynecological symptoms such as postmenstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. Sonohysterography demonstrates superior diagnostic accuracy (56–84% prevalence detection) compared to transvaginal ultrasonography (24–70%), enabling reliable identification and characterization of these defects for clinical management.

title: Hysteroscopic sutureless fixation of the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system in the treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding
summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding, a condition often associated with adenomyosis, uterine fibroids, and ovulatory disorders, is effectively treated with the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system (LNG-IUS), which reduces menstrual blood loss while preserving fertility compared to surgical alternatives like hysterectomy. However, certain patient factors including adenomyosis, larger uterine cavities, severe dysmenorrhea, and previous IUS expulsion significantly increase the risk of device expulsion, with conventional LNG-IUS placement resulting in expulsion rates of 13.3%. Hysteroscopic sutureless fixation substantially reduces this risk, achieving expulsion and displacement rates of only 3.3% and 10% respectively, representing a marked improvement over conventional implantation. This minimally invasive fixation procedure produces significant reductions in menstrual flow, dysmenorrhea symptoms, and pain scores, with sustained improvements in quality of life measures including EQ-5D scores and elimination of postoperative anemia at one year follow-up. The procedure demonstrates efficacy comparable to traditional endometrial resection and ablation techniques while maintaining the reversibility and fertility-preserving benefits of the LNG-IUS. These findings establish hysteroscopic sutureless fixation as a safe and effective advancement for treating heavy menstrual bleeding, particularly in high-risk populations prone to device expulsion.

title: Peritoneal Inclusion Cyst Mimicking Peritoneal Carcinomatosis: A Benign Lesion Posing a Malignant Dilemma
summary: Peritoneal inclusion cysts (PICs) are benign, multiloculated lesions arising from reactive mesothelial proliferation that commonly develop following pelvic inflammation, adhesions, endometriosis, infection, or prior pelvic surgery, predominantly affecting premenopausal women. These lesions are characterized by thin internal septations and predominantly cystic morphology lined by flattened to cuboidal mesothelial cells without atypia, distinguishing them from peritoneal carcinomatosis, which presents with malignant features including soft-tissue implants, irregular peritoneal thickening, omental caking, and ascites. Peritoneal carcinomatosis, typically associated with advanced gastrointestinal or gynecologic malignancies, poses a significant diagnostic challenge because imaging findings of PICs can mimic malignant disease, potentially leading to unnecessary clinical concern. The critical distinction relies on imaging characteristics, as PICs show benign morphology without solid enhancing nodules, whereas carcinomatosis displays aggressive features indicative of malignant cell dissemination. Importantly, PICs demonstrate high recurrence rates of 30–50% when excision is incomplete, necessitating complete removal to prevent relapse. This case illustrates the diagnostic importance of recognizing PICs as a benign reactive process and the value of pathologic confirmation to definitively exclude malignancy.

title: The Annual Economic Burden of Uterine Fibroids in the United States (2010 Versus 2022): A Comparative Cost-Analysis
summary: Uterine fibroids represent a significant clinical and economic burden, causing a spectrum of reproductive and non-reproductive symptoms in 20-50% of affected women, including abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, urinary frequency, and anemia. Beyond symptom management, fibroids substantially impact pregnancy outcomes, increasing risks of preterm labor, placental abruption, postpartum hemorrhage, and fetal malpresentation, while also contributing to infertility in 1-3% of women and appearing in up to 27% of those seeking reproductive assistance. Multiple treatment approaches exist, ranging from medical management and minimally invasive procedures such as uterine artery embolization and magnetic resonance-guided focused ultrasound to surgical interventions including hysterectomy and myomectomy, with fertility-preserving options preferred for younger patients. While hysterectomy remains the most effective and commonly performed treatment, myomectomy and uterine-sparing minimally invasive approaches offer faster recovery and fertility preservation benefits, though some carry risks such as uterine rupture in subsequent pregnancies. The economic analysis comparing 2010 versus 2022 data reveals the substantial costs associated with obstetric complications, cesarean deliveries, and the rising expenses of assisted reproductive technologies for fibroid-related infertility, highlighting the need for widespread adoption of conservative treatment strategies to reduce both morbidity and healthcare expenditures.

title: Near-Infrared Fluorescence Imaging With Indocyanine Green for Intraoperative Nerve Root Visualization in Spinal Surgery: From Preclinical Studies to a Pilot Randomized Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

Iatrogenic nerve injury during endoscopic spinal surgery for lumbar disc herniation remains a significant clinical challenge, causing postoperative sensory disturbances, motor dysfunction, and long-term neurological deficits. This study evaluated near-infrared fluorescence imaging with indocyanine green (ICG), an FDA-approved fluorophore with peak excitation at 805 nm, as a strategy to improve intraoperative nerve root visualization and reduce nerve injury risk. Preclinical studies demonstrated that ICG accumulates in nerve root microvascular regions through the enhanced permeability and retention effect, with optimal signal-to-background ratios achieved at 2.8 and 5.5 mg/kg doses three hours post-injection. In the clinical pilot trial using unilateral biportal endoscopy, the 2 mg/kg ICG group achieved the highest fluorescence intensity while significantly reducing nerve root identification time without adverse effects. ICG fluorescence imaging proved feasible and safe for intraoperative nerve root visualization, offering potential to enhance surgical safety and reduce nerve injury complications in spinal procedures.

title: Plain Language Summary of the ACCEPT Guideline for the Management of Recurrent Pregnancy Loss
journal: The Australian & New Zealand Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology
summary:
# Summary

The ACCEPT guideline identifies multiple pathways contributing to recurrent pregnancy loss (RPL), with chromosomal abnormalities—particularly aneuploidy, which increases with maternal age—representing the most common cause, while parental balanced translocations can be managed through IVF with preimplantation genetic testing. Uterine anatomical factors including Müllerian anomalies, submucosal and intramural fibroids, and intrauterine adhesions all contribute to RPL and can be improved through surgical intervention. The guideline emphasizes screening and management of treatable medical conditions including thyroid disorders (hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, and autoimmune thyroid disease treated with levothyroxine), antiphospholipid syndrome (managed with low-dose aspirin and heparin), chronic endometritis (treated with antibiotics), and metabolic conditions such as PCOS and obesity. Lifestyle factors in both partners significantly influence RPL risk—maternal smoking and obesity impair outcomes while paternal smoking causes sperm DNA damage, which can be addressed through lifestyle modification, antioxidant supplements, or advanced reproductive techniques. Emerging evidence supports progesterone supplementation for threatened pregnancy loss and identifies endometriosis as a potential contributor to RPL through inflammatory mechanisms and compromised egg quality.

title: Evaluation of coagulation disorders and iron deficiency in women with heavy menstrual bleeding
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary:
# Summary

Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) affects a substantial proportion of women and stems from both gynecologic and hematologic etiologies. While structural causes such as adenomyosis, myomas, and polyps account for approximately one-quarter of cases, congenital bleeding disorders—particularly von Willebrand disease, which presents with HMB in 70% of affected women—represent an important but frequently undiagnosed contributor to excessive menstrual bleeding. The study identified that one-third of women with HMB presented with overt anemia and nearly three-quarters had depleted iron stores despite oral supplementation, indicating persistent iron deficiency that warrants more aggressive intervention. Coagulation testing proved valuable for identifying previously undiagnosed von Willebrand factor deficiency in 9.2% of women without known bleeding disorders. Management strategies include pharmacologic interventions such as tranexamic acid and desmopressin for bleeding disorders, alongside iron supplementation, intravenous iron infusion, or blood transfusion for anemia in severe cases. These findings underscore the importance of comprehensive hematologic evaluation in women with HMB to identify treatable coagulation defects and prevent long-term iron deficiency complications.

title: Adenomyosis-Modern Techniques for Ultrasound and Histo-Pathological Diagnosis of the Endo-Myometrial Junction Zone Changes
summary: Adenomyosis is a benign but invasive uterine condition characterized by the presence of ectopic endometrial glands and stroma invading the myometrium, causing significant clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea, heavy menstrual bleeding, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility in approximately two-thirds of affected women. The pathophysiology involves disruption of the junctional zone—the inner myometrium critical for uterine peristalsis and implantation—resulting in abnormal uterine contractility, impaired peristalsis, chronic inflammation, and oxidative stress, alongside structural changes such as smooth muscle cell hyperplasia, increased collagen deposition, and altered α-smooth muscle actin expression. Key risk factors for adenomyosis include multiparity, age over 40 years, and previous uterine surgery such as cesarean section, with the condition frequently coexisting with uterine fibroids (35–55%) and endometriosis (65–70%). Modern diagnostic approaches combine 3D transvaginal ultrasound assessment of junctional zone morphology with hysterosalpingo-contrast sonography and immunohistochemical analysis, where findings such as contrast agent reflux and elevated α-smooth muscle actin expression provide high diagnostic accuracy. Adenomyosis significantly impairs reproductive outcomes by reducing clinical pregnancy rates and increasing miscarriage rates, with severity stratification revealing that more advanced adenomyosis (A2 subgroup) associates with marked ovarian reserve reduction and more severe symptomatology including dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, and hypertension. Additionally, adenomyosis demonstrates associations with enlarged uterine dimensions, increased myometrial vascularization, and adverse perinatal outcomes, making early diagnosis and appropriate management critical for optimizing fertility and reproductive health.

title: Efficacy and Outcomes of the Hormone-Releasing Levonorgestrel-Intrauterine System for Adenomyosis Treatment
summary: Adenomyosis, characterized by the presence of endometrial tissues within the myometrium, causes multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, heavy menstrual bleeding, and chronic pelvic pain, with prevalence rates ranging from 43-89% in affected populations. The levonorgestrel-intrauterine system (LNG-IUS) effectively treats adenomyosis by inducing decidualization and stabilizing ectopic endometrial tissue through local progesterone delivery, mechanisms that reduce estrogen production in the endometrium. Clinical outcomes demonstrate substantial symptom improvement at six months post-insertion, with dysmenorrhea visual analog scale scores decreasing from 5.54 to 1.37, blood loss declining from 66.73 to 4.86 mL, and 75-81% of patients experiencing overall pain alleviation across symptom categories. The study recommends LNG-IUS as first-line medical treatment given its high efficacy and patient satisfaction rates, though it is most effective in patients with uterine volumes below 197.50 mL and shorter uterocervical lengths, as larger uteri correlate with device expulsion or displacement requiring hysteroscopic suture fixation. Adverse effects are generally mild and include amenorrhea (18-20% of users), vaginal dryness, genital discharge, and pelvic pain, but these do not substantially offset the significant symptomatic benefits achieved through LNG-IUS insertion.

title: Cesarean Scar Pregnancy Case Report in a Grade 2 Maternity and Review of the Literature
journal: Reports - Clinical Practice and Surgical Cases
summary:
# Summary

Cesarean delivery leads to cesarean scar pregnancy (CSP), a rare but serious complication in which pregnancy implants within the uterine scar, along with long-term risks including endometriosis, adhesions, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Healing defects and niche development at the cesarean scar—associated with factors such as multiple prior cesarean sections, higher body mass index, smoking, and cesarean delivery before labor onset—predispose women to CSP and subsequent complications including vaginal bleeding, abdominal pain, placenta accreta spectrum, and uterine rupture. Early diagnosis via transvaginal ultrasound is crucial, as presentations with β-hCG levels below 5,000 mIU/mL, gestational sacs smaller than 20 mm, and absence of fetal heartbeat respond favorably to medical management with methotrexate or mifepristone, while late presentation significantly increases risk of placenta accreta, emergency hysterectomy, and fetal death. Surgical and minimally invasive treatment options—including ultrasound-guided curettage, hysteroscopic resection, and laparoscopic repair—are guided by myometrial thickness and gestational sac diameter, with treatment approach selection critical to preventing severe complications such as intraoperative bleeding and morbidly adherent placenta.

title: A Unique Case Series of Endometrial Vascular Dystrophy: Rising Incidence and Association With Uterine Pathologies
summary: Endometrial vascular dystrophy (EVD) is a rare hysteroscopic finding characterized by abnormal, tortuous, and dilated vascular structures within the endometrium that is increasingly recognized as an under-diagnosed contributor to abnormal uterine bleeding. This case series demonstrates that EVD occurs in association with multiple uterine pathologies including intrauterine adhesions, endometriosis, fibroids, and isthmoceles, though notably it is absent from isthmocele defect regions themselves. Histopathologically, EVD is consistently found in secretory endometrium and involves dilated glands containing entrapped erythrocytes, with progesterone hypothesized to play a key role in its pathogenesis through effects on stromal edema and vascular remodeling. The condition appears to represent a secondary morphological response to various uterine insults, with dysregulated angiogenic factors such as VEGF and matrix metalloproteinases likely contributing to abnormal endometrial vascular architecture in associated conditions like endometriosis and adenomyosis. Diagnosis requires hysteroscopy combined with histopathology and special stains (PAS and Alcian blue) to differentiate dilated vessels from distended secretory glands. Despite its alarming appearance, EVD demonstrates benign and often self-limiting behavior, with patients showing significant clinical improvement following endometrial curettage and symptomatic resolution at six-month follow-up.

title: Office hysteroscopic treatment of vaginal bleeding and related pain after supracervical hysterectomy: A case report
summary: Supracervical hysterectomy frequently results in vaginal bleeding and pelvic pain due to the development of hyperplastic residual epithelial tissue at the cervical stump, which retains hormonal responsiveness. Pathological examination of affected tissue revealed microglandular hyperplasia without residual endometrial foci. Office hysteroscopic partial epithelial ablation using a bipolar Versapoint Twizzle Electrode provides effective treatment for both symptoms through minimal tissue dissection while preserving ovarian function. This minimally invasive approach successfully reduced vaginal bleeding and associated pelvic pain in this case, offering an alternative to more extensive surgical intervention. The technique's safety profile and efficacy suggest it represents a viable option for managing these common post-hysterectomy complications in an office setting.

title: Machine learning models for the prediction of uterine fibroids
summary:
# Summary

This study developed machine learning models to predict uterine fibroids by analyzing demographic, clinical, and laboratory variables in a large patient cohort. LASSO regression identified 13 significant predictors including age, body mass index, blood pressure, cholesterol levels, and lifestyle factors such as work intensity and fruit intake, with SHAPLEY analysis confirming age and BMI as the most critical risk factors. The CatBoost machine learning model demonstrated superior predictive performance with an area under the curve of 0.808, outperforming other tested algorithms. Uterine fibroids are associated with serious reproductive and metabolic complications, including heavy menstrual bleeding, pelvic pain, infertility, miscarriage, and increased endometrial cancer risk, making early prediction clinically valuable. Current treatment options include traditional surgical approaches such as hysterectomy, myomectomy, and endometrial ablation, though hysterectomy remains the only definitive cure. These findings underscore the importance of integrating metabolic and demographic factors into predictive models to identify women at high risk for uterine fibroids and enable earlier intervention.

title: Acute Small Bowel Evisceration Through Vaginal Cuff Following Intercourse After Total Laparoscopic Hysterectomy: A Rare Surgical Emergency—Case Report
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: This case report describes a rare but serious complication of total laparoscopic hysterectomy: vaginal cuff dehiscence leading to acute small bowel evisceration through the vagina during sexual intercourse three months post-operatively. The dehiscence occurred when mechanical stress from intercourse caused shearing forces on an incompletely healed vaginal cuff, resulting in bowel protrusion with associated risks of ischemia, infection, and peritonitis. Multiple surgical and patient factors contributed to this complication, including use of energy devices for colpotomy, inadequate cuff closure technique, and premature resumption of sexual activity, while protective factors such as barbed sutures and laparoscopic cuff closure reduce dehiscence risk. The patient's underlying endometriosis and heavy menstrual bleeding, which prompted the hysterectomy, were successfully treated by the procedure despite this subsequent emergency. Management involved urgent surgical repair of the cuff defect and assessment of bowel viability, with options including vaginal, abdominal, or laparoscopic approaches depending on clinical presentation. This case emphasizes the importance of meticulous surgical technique, appropriate patient counseling regarding sexual activity timing, and awareness of dehiscence risk factors particularly with minimally invasive hysterectomy approaches.

title: Case Report: Two clinical cases of severe deep infiltrating endometriosis with infertility—transplantation first or surgery first? Natural cycle or artificial cycle?
summary:
# Summary

Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) is a severe form of the disease characterized by lesions penetrating at least 5 mm into affected organs, causing chronic pelvic pain, functional impairment, and infertility that frequently fails to respond to conventional ovulation induction. This case report demonstrates a sequential management strategy combining fertility preservation via embryo cryopreservation, radical cytoreductive surgery (including partial rectal and vaginal resection with adhesiolysis and ureteral repair), and adjuvant medical therapy with dienogest and gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists to suppress residual microscopic disease. Natural-cycle frozen embryo transfer was then employed rather than artificial cycles to reduce the risk of activating residual endometriotic lesions with exogenous estrogen stimulation. Both patients achieved successful pregnancies following this integrated approach, with marked clinical improvement evidenced by significantly reduced CA125 levels, alleviation of pelvic pain and bowel symptoms, and restored pelvic mobility. This multidisciplinary strategy of "freeze-all embryos, perform radical surgery, natural-cycle embryo transfer" proved effective for treating infertility secondary to severe DIE, particularly in cases with organ dysfunction, intractable symptoms, or previous assisted reproductive technology failures.

title: Impact of obstetric and surgical factors on isthmocele development: insights from a prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective cohort study identifies multiple obstetric and surgical factors contributing to isthmocele (cesarean scar defect) formation and its clinical consequences. Isthmoceles cause significant morbidity including abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and secondary infertility, while also increasing risks of placenta accreta spectrum, uterine rupture, and maternal hemorrhage in subsequent pregnancies. Key risk factors identified include higher gestational age at delivery, shorter inter-delivery intervals, retroverted uterine position, locking suture repair techniques, prolonged labor, and prior endometritis, with retroverted uterus and gestational age emerging as independent predictors in multivariable analysis. Mechanistically, prolonged uterine distension near term causes myometrial thinning and tension that impair wound edge alignment, while retroverted position increases mechanical stresses and compromises blood supply to the scar. Locking suture techniques may further compromise microvascular perfusion, leading to localized ischemia and impaired healing, whereas longer inter-delivery intervals provide protective effects by allowing adequate scar remodeling. These findings underscore the importance of surgical technique selection and pregnancy spacing in reducing isthmocele development and its associated reproductive complications.

title: Vaginal Resection of a Large Nascent Uterine Pyomyoma Following Late Miscarriage
summary:
# Summary

Uterine fibroids are common benign pelvic tumors originating from myometrial smooth muscle cells, with established risk factors including early menarche, hereditary predisposition, Black ethnicity, arterial hypertension, and chronic stress. This case presents a 29-year-old woman with hypothyroidism and endometriosis who developed a rapidly expanding fibroid that underwent red degeneration during pregnancy—a hemorrhagic infarction occurring when fibroid growth exceeds vascular supply. The necrotizing fibroid became secondarily infected with *Escherichia coli*, transforming into a pyomyoma that caused severe abdominal pain, foul-smelling vaginal bleeding, and urinary complaints despite initial antibiotic therapy with piperacillin-tazobactam and doxycycline. Elevated inflammatory markers, leukocytosis, and declining hemoglobin levels indicated progressive infection requiring escalation to meropenem and ultimately surgical intervention. A fertility-preserving vaginal myomectomy successfully removed the 120 mm necrotic fibroid, with tranexamic acid and misoprostol administered to prevent excessive bleeding. This case demonstrates that cavity-distorting fibroids (FIGO types 0–3) significantly increase miscarriage risk through impaired implantation, placental complications, and infection, and highlights vaginal myomectomy as an effective approach offering reduced morbidity, faster recovery, and preservation of uterine integrity in selected patients.

title: The Role of Laparoscopic Surgery in Gynecological Congenital Anomalies
summary: Müllerian duct anomalies (MDAs) are congenital malformations of the uterus, cervix, and vagina that cause significant reproductive morbidity including menstruation impairment, sexual dysfunction, and infertility. Minimally invasive laparoscopic surgery has transformed the management of MDAs by enabling effective correction of these complex malformations while reducing postoperative adhesion formation. For specific anatomical variants, targeted laparoscopic approaches show high efficacy: laparoscopic Strassman metroplasty reduces symptoms in bicornuate uteri and decreases miscarriage risk, laparoscopic excision of rudimentary horns addresses unicornuate uteri, and combined vaginal-laparoscopic approaches treat uterus didelphys and obstructed hemivagina with ipsilateral renal anomaly (OHVIRA). For vaginal agenesis associated with Mayer-Rokitansky-Küster-Hauser syndrome, multiple laparoscopic options are available including laparoscopic Vecchietti, Davydov, and sigmoid vaginoplasty. Overall, laparoscopic techniques represent the standard of care for gynecological congenital anomalies, offering superior outcomes with minimal morbidity compared to traditional open approaches.

title: A rare case of pulmonary sequestration with elevated CA19-9 levels
summary: This case report describes an unusual presentation of pulmonary sequestration, a congenital lung malformation, complicated by elevated serum CA19-9 levels, a tumor marker typically associated with pancreatic and gastrointestinal cancers. The elevated CA19-9 arose not from malignancy but from chronic inflammation and tissue damage within the sequestered lung tissue, where abnormal respiratory columnar epithelium secreted multiple tumor-associated markers including CA19-9, CEA, CA125, CA50, and CA242. Infection of the sequestered lung tissue by pathogens such as Aspergillus species and Pseudomonas aeruginosa triggered chronic inflammation, which stimulated epithelial cell proliferation and marker secretion. The patient's clinical presentation included recurrent respiratory infections, hemoptysis, and pleural effusion, all common complications of intralobar pulmonary sequestration. Surgical lobectomy effectively resolved the condition, with CA19-9 levels normalizing within three months postoperatively. This case highlights that elevated CA19-9 in non-malignant lung disease can result from severe alveolar damage and epithelial remodeling rather than cancer, emphasizing the importance of comprehensive diagnostic evaluation in cases with unexpected tumor marker elevation.

title: The prevalence and burden of heavy menstrual bleeding, and market access barriers of medical interventions with a focus on low- and middle-income countries: a scoping review
summary: Heavy menstrual bleeding (HMB) is a significant global health burden with multiple etiologies, most commonly uterine fibroids but also including ovulatory disorders, adenomyosis, endometriosis, hematological disorders, endometrial polyps, and malignancies. HMB frequently triggers iron deficiency anemia and is associated with dysmenorrhea, fatigue, headaches, impaired quality of life, mental health issues including depression, and reduced physical and social functioning. First-line medical treatments include non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, antifibrinolytic agents, combined hormonal contraceptives, and levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine devices, with the latter showing the highest clinical uptake. Second-line surgical options such as myomectomy, endometrial resection and ablation, and hysterectomy are available when medical management fails. The review documents both conventional pharmacological approaches and complementary interventions including herbal remedies, nutritional supplements, and practices like yoga and cupping, though access to these treatments remains substantially limited in low- and middle-income countries.

title: Management of strangulated, perforated gastrothorax with resultant tension pneumothorax secondary to an iatrogenic diaphragmatic hernia: a case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare complication of diaphragmatic endometriosis treatment in a 29-year-old woman who underwent video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery for endometrial implants on the pleural surface. The surgical procedure inadvertently created a diaphragmatic defect, theorized to result from heat injury to deeper diaphragmatic layers caused by energy devices, which subsequently weakened under pleuroperitoneal pressure differentials. Several months after the initial surgery, the patient presented with acute, severe chest pain due to gastric herniation through the defect, resulting in gastric strangulation, perforation, and a life-threatening tension pneumothorax from intraperitoneal air escaping through the unseparated hernia margins. Emergency management included intercostal catheter decompression, nasogastric tube placement for gastric decompression, and definitive surgical repair comprising partial fundectomy for the gastric perforation and primary diaphragmatic closure with absorbable synthetic mesh reinforcement. The case highlights the rare but serious risk of iatrogenic diaphragmatic hernia following thoracoscopic endometriosis surgery and emphasizes that delayed presentations with nonspecific symptoms often characterize such complications, underscoring the importance of careful tissue handling during energy device application near the diaphragm.

title: Treatment of bronchobiliary fistula: a 13-year experience
summary:
# Summary

Bronchobiliary fistula (BBF) is an abnormal communication between the bile duct and bronchial tree that carries poor prognosis and can result from diverse etiologies including liver hydatid disease, malignancies (hepatocellular carcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma), bile duct stones, trauma, and complications from interventional procedures such as transcatheter arterial chemoembolization and radiofrequency ablation. The condition classically presents with a triad of refractory cough, bilioptysis, and recurrent pulmonary infections, alongside jaundice and chest/abdominal pain, with delayed diagnosis potentially progressing to life-threatening sepsis or respiratory failure. Diagnostic modalities include sputum bilirubin analysis, fiberoptic bronchoscopy revealing bilious secretions, and imaging studies (CT, MRI, magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography, cholangiography) demonstrating the fistulous tract. Treatment strategies vary by etiology: benign cases respond to surgical intervention with curative potential, while malignant cases benefit from minimally invasive interventions such as endoscopic sphincterotomy, biliary stent placement, percutaneous transhepatic cholangial drainage, and bronchial occlusion devices (glue, coils) that provide effective symptomatic palliation. In this 13-year series of 17 patients, surgical intervention achieved long-term survival in 50% of cases, while minimally invasive interventions successfully palliated symptoms in 91% of patients, with outcomes driven primarily by underlying disease etiology and early recognition.

title: Massive Hemoperitoneum Caused by Spontaneous Rupture of a Superficial Uterine Fundal Vein During Preterm Labor: A Case Report
summary: Spontaneous rupture of superficial uterine fundal veins represents a rare but potentially life-threatening cause of hemoperitoneum during pregnancy, as illustrated in this case of a preterm laboring patient. Pregnancy-induced physiologic changes—including marked dilation and increased venous capacitance of uterine and pelvic veins, progesterone-mediated smooth muscle relaxation, and elevated intra-abdominal pressure from uterine contractions—render thin-walled superficial veins particularly susceptible to rupture. The patient's underlying pelvic venous congestion, evidenced by persistent left ovarian vein and pelvic venous plexus dilation postpartum, likely contributed to venous fragility and rupture risk. Diagnosis is challenging preoperatively, with imaging typically showing only localized fundal hematoma without arterial extravasation; emergent cesarean delivery enabled hemostasis via fibrin sealant patch and prophylactic gelfoam embolization of abnormally dilated uterine vasculature. This case highlights the importance of recognizing venous-origin hemorrhage in pregnant patients presenting with unexplained abdominal pain and localized hemoperitoneum, particularly when hemodynamic instability or fetal compromise develops, warranting prompt surgical intervention.

title: Spontaneous Pneumomediastinum in COVID-19 and Myasthenic-like Symptom Complications in Two Relatives: A Coincidence or Spike Toxicity with Thymic Response in Predisposed Individuals? Two Clinical Cases with a Comprehensive Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

This case series examines spontaneous pneumomediastinum (SPM) and myasthenia gravis-like complications in two relatives following SARS-CoV-2 infection and COVID-19 vaccination. The pneumomediastinum arose through multiple mechanisms: direct viral damage causing interstitial pneumonia with inflammatory alveolar infiltration and vascular injury, which together trigger alveolar destruction and rupture; additionally, mechanical ventilation strategies (non-invasive ventilation and high-flow oxygen) with positive end-expiratory pressure contributed to alveolar overdistension, while corticosteroid therapy further elevated risk. The myasthenia gravis-like symptoms manifested as thymic hyperplasia associated with both SARS-CoV-2 infection and mRNA vaccination, leading to increased T lymphocyte production and aberrant immune responses. The authors propose that spike protein toxicity and lung dysbiosis represent potential common denominators in both the pneumomediastinal and thymic complications, particularly in genetically predisposed individuals, with the mother's history of grade IV endometriosis suggesting underlying immune dysfunction. Thymectomy in the affected son provided clinical improvement in thoracic pain and muscular weakness, while pyridostigmine offered symptomatic relief, supporting the autoimmune nature of the thymic complications.

title: Change in faecal incontinence pattern after gastric bypass surgery: related to change in anal sphincter thickness?
summary: Obesity is strongly associated with fecal incontinence, with women having a BMI over 40 showing a threefold higher prevalence of anal incontinence compared to normal-weight women. Roux-en-Y gastric bypass surgery effectively induces weight loss and improves fecal incontinence, as demonstrated by decreased Wexner incontinence scores six months post-operatively; however, the pattern of incontinence changes, with reduced fecal leakage but increased urgency and loose stools. While sphincter thickness measurements showed minimal change following surgery, resting anal pressure decreased, suggesting that improvements in continence may result from physiological changes beyond structural sphincter alterations. The study reveals a complex relationship where weight loss paradoxically shifts the type of fecal incontinence experienced rather than eliminating it entirely, with dietary modifications and altered bowel physiology post-surgery contributing to these symptom pattern changes. These findings indicate that bariatric surgery's beneficial effects on obesity-related fecal incontinence operate through mechanisms involving pressure reduction and stool consistency changes rather than significant changes in anal sphincter anatomy alone.

title: Abdominal Striae, Cesarean Scar, and Ultrasound Sliding Sign as Predictors of Uterine Adhesions in Women Undergoing Repeat Cesarean Section
summary:
# Summary

This study identified clinical and ultrasound predictors of intra-abdominal adhesions in women undergoing repeat cesarean section. Adhesion risk increased significantly with the number of previous cesarean sections, rising from approximately 27% after one prior surgery to over 50% after multiple procedures due to cumulative inflammatory and fibrotic responses triggered by successive surgical trauma. Three key predictors were evaluated: a negative ultrasound sliding sign (indicating restricted uterine mobility), severe abdominal striae graded by the Davey score, and poor cesarean scar quality assessed by the Vancouver Scar Scale greater than 4, all of which were strongly associated with adhesion presence. The investigators propose that a shared genetic predisposition toward aberrant collagen remodeling may explain why women prone to visible dermal manifestations—such as stretch marks and hypertrophic scarring—are similarly susceptible to internal fibrous adhesion formation. Intraoperative adhesions significantly increase operative morbidity by prolonging surgical time, increasing blood loss, and raising the risk of bladder and bowel injury, with severe adhesions occasionally necessitating hysterectomy. These readily accessible clinical and bedside ultrasound assessments offer a practical screening approach in resource-limited settings to identify high-risk patients and plan appropriate surgical management.

title: Profiling Sociodemographic Risk Factors and Clinical Outcomes of Women with Endometrial Cancer in Puerto Rico: The Central Role of Obesity and Obstetric Features
journal: Journal of Racial and Ethnic Health Disparities
summary: This study examined endometrial cancer risk factors and clinical outcomes in Puerto Rican women, identifying obesity, diabetes mellitus type 2, and hypertension as the major modifiable risk factors, with 72% of the cohort having a BMI ≥30. Postmenopausal bleeding was the most common presenting symptom, while more advanced disease presentations included pelvic pain and abdominal distention; notably, obesity paradoxically associated with earlier FIGO stages and younger age at diagnosis, whereas older age at diagnosis correlated with more advanced disease and higher FIGO grades. The study found that nulliparity and dysmenorrhea associated with younger age at diagnosis, while pregnancy history showed a protective effect with lower FIGO grades. Type I endometrial cancers (estrogen-dependent endometrioid adenocarcinoma) predominated in earlier-stage, lower-grade cases and carried good prognosis, whereas Type II cancers were estrogen-independent with poorer outcomes. Hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy remained the standard treatment, and Lynch Syndrome was identified as a significant non-modifiable hereditary risk factor. The findings underscore the critical interplay between metabolic factors and reproductive history in determining endometrial cancer risk and severity in this Puerto Rican population.
