Cesarean scar defect (CSD) is strongly associated with prolonged menstruation, menstrual irregularities, infertility, and chronic pelvic pain, with hysteroscopic resection serving as an effective minimally invasive treatment for symptomatic patients. Notably, patients with CSD frequently present with coexisting endometrial lesions, particularly endometrial polyps and hyperplasia, which further exacerbate abnormal uterine bleeding and complicate the clinical presentation. Patients with combined CSD and endometrial pathology display distinct clinical characteristics, including significantly elevated rates of anemia, thicker endometrium, higher prevalence of CSD-related cysts and uterine fibroids, and reduced preoperative hemoglobin levels. Independent risk factors for developing endometrial comorbidities include anemia, lack of previous medical treatment, increased endometrial thickness, and the presence of pericystic cysts. The underlying mechanisms appear to involve chronic inflammation within the scar defect and local endometrial abnormalities that create an altered uterine microenvironment with elevated pelvic inflammatory cytokines, which collectively contribute to the observed reproductive dysfunction. Endometrial thickness and CSD-related cyst formations were identified as significant predictors of surgical outcomes, suggesting that careful patient stratification based on these imaging features may optimize hysteroscopic treatment results.

title: Advanced Management of Severe Adenomyosis in IVF: A Personalized Approach With Extended GnRH Agonist and Letrozole Therapy
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis causes significant clinical burden through multiple pathways, including pelvic pain, infertility, heavy menstrual bleeding, uterine inflammation, and abnormal contractility, with particular detriment to in vitro fertilization outcomes through increased miscarriage risk and reduced clinical pregnancy rates. The underlying pathophysiology involves adenomyotic tissue autonomously producing estrogen via intrinsic aromatase activity, combined with progesterone resistance driven by increased estrogen receptor expression. A personalized treatment approach combining extended gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonist therapy with the aromatase inhibitor letrozole effectively suppresses serum estradiol levels below 5 pg/mL and reduces uterine size, demonstrated through prolonged five-month suppression protocols. This dual suppression strategy, paired with intensive luteal phase support and preparatory hysteroscopy to enhance endometrial receptivity, successfully improved frozen embryo transfer outcomes in severe cases, representing a novel management strategy for adenomyosis refractory to conventional therapies. The approach acknowledges adenomyosis's frequent coexistence with endometriosis and addresses the unique challenge that adenomyosis significantly increases miscarriage risk even when euploid embryos are transferred, independent of other factors.

title: Symptom improvement in adenomyosis patients after ultrasound guided microwave ablation or uterine artery embolization, A randomized controlled pilot study
summary: Adenomyosis is a benign uterine condition causing heavy menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, anemia, and impaired fertility through abnormal infiltration of endometrial glands and stroma into the myometrium. This randomized controlled pilot study compared two minimally invasive treatment approaches—ultrasound-guided microwave ablation (MWA) and uterine artery embolization (UAE)—demonstrating that both significantly reduced adenomyosis symptoms including menstrual bleeding (PBAC scores decreased from 563 to 122 in MWA and 877 to 197 in UAE), dysmenorrhea, uterine volume, and symptom severity scores, while substantially improving quality of life. Microwave ablation demonstrated superior postoperative outcomes compared to UAE, including no hospitalization versus three days, faster return to daily activities (3 versus 14 days), and shorter duration of pain medication use (3 versus 10 days). Both treatments effectively reduced anemia by 56-79% and decreased Ca-125 levels, a marker correlated with uterine size and adenomyosis severity, though UAE showed significantly greater Ca-125 reduction. Neither treatment negatively affected ovarian reserve as measured by anti-müllerian hormone levels, preserving fertility potential in treated patients. These findings support both MWA and UAE as effective adenomyosis treatments, with MWA offering advantages in recovery profile and patient tolerance.

title: Prominent venous intravasation in hysterosalpingo-contrast sonography (HyCoSy): A case report
summary:
# Summary

This case report presents a rare diagnostic challenge during hysterosalpingo-contrast sonography (HyCoSy), where extensive venous intravasation (VI) mimicked fallopian tube duplication malformation in a patient with bilateral tubal obstruction. Venous intravasation occurs when contrast agent unintentionally enters myometrial vessels, a complication that affects 13-26% of HyCoSy procedures and frequently leads to misdiagnosis that compromises fertility treatment planning. In this case, bilateral tubal obstruction created elevated intrauterine pressure that forced high-volume contrast through a compromised endometrial-myometrial junction, opacifying parametrial venous plexuses that appeared as pseudo-tubular structures mimicking duplicated fallopian tubes. Key diagnostic features distinguishing VI from true tubal pathology included absence of peritoneal contrast spillage, lack of a periovarian halo, and delayed ovarian stromal enhancement indicating systemic recirculation of contrast into the venous system. After hysteroscopic polypectomy and confirmation of bilateral obstruction through chromopertubation testing, the patient was referred for in vitro fertilization-embryo transfer and successfully achieved pregnancy. This report highlights the critical importance of dynamic real-time imaging, frame-by-frame cine-loop analysis, and recognition of VI mimics to prevent diagnostic errors that could otherwise misdirect fertility treatment.

title: Retrospective analysis to evaluate Allium stents as an alternative method for the treatment of ureteral strictures
summary:
# Summary

Ureteral strictures, caused by urolithiasis, tumors, external compression, pelvic surgery, infection, and malignancy treatments, lead to serious complications including hydronephrosis, renal impairment, renal failure, and symptomatic presentations such as renal colic and urinary tract infections. This retrospective study evaluated Allium metallic ureteral stents as a treatment alternative for ureteral strictures, particularly in elderly and high-risk patients who failed conservative management. The Allium stent's metal structure combined with an inner polymer coating prevents tissue ingrowth and encrustation while reducing friction and inflammation, successfully improving hydronephrosis regression and renal function in most patients. However, stent migration emerged as the most common failure mechanism (occurring in approximately 22% of cases), associated with advanced age, ureteral peristalsis, physical activity, and complete stricture dilation, potentially leading to loss of ureteral patency and recurrent infections. The findings support Allium stents as an effective treatment option for refractory ureteral strictures with improved clinical outcomes, though careful patient selection and monitoring are necessary to mitigate migration risk and other complications.

title: Giant prolapsed uterine leiomyoma causing acute urinary retention and severe anemia: a case report and review of the literature
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare presentation of giant uterine leiomyoma with concurrent acute urinary retention and severe anemia. Giant leiomyomas, whose growth is promoted by estrogen and progesterone, typically cause abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, and chronic lower urinary tract symptoms through mass effects on adjacent pelvic organs. The acute urinary retention in this case resulted from a "cork-in-bottle" mechanical obstruction caused by the prolapsed fibroid impacting the bladder. The severe anemia had an atypical etiology: as the giant fibroid outgrew its vascularity, it underwent ischemic degeneration and central necrosis, followed by pressure necrosis and surface ulceration of the devitalized tissue, leading to chronic occult hemorrhage and iron-deficiency anemia without heavy menstrual bleeding. The patient was successfully treated with a combined abdomino-vaginal approach including laparotomy and anterior colpotomy for myomectomy, demonstrating that giant leiomyomas should be considered in the differential diagnosis of female acute urinary retention, particularly when accompanied by unexplained anemia.

title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of the efficacy of addition and reduction of Shixiao powder in the treatment of coronary heart disease
summary: This meta-analysis evaluates the efficacy of Shixiao Powder, a traditional Chinese medicine formulation, as an adjunctive therapy to conventional Western medicine for treating coronary heart disease and its associated angina pectoris. When combined with standard pharmaceutical treatments (antiplatelet agents, statins, beta-blockers, and other cardioprotective drugs), Shixiao Powder significantly improved clinical outcomes including angina symptoms, ECG ST segment changes, and traditional Chinese medicine symptom scores while reducing serum C-reactive protein levels, indicating anti-inflammatory benefits. The therapeutic mechanisms of Shixiao Powder components—particularly Typhae Pollen, which promotes angiogenesis, prevents thrombosis, and reduces inflammation, and Trogopterori Faeces, which suppresses inflammatory reactions—align with the pathophysiology of coronary atherosclerosis-induced myocardial ischemia and hypoxia. The combination approach addresses limitations of Western medicine monotherapy, which frequently causes adverse effects including myopathy, hepatorenal dysfunction, and bleeding complications. Overall, the evidence supports Shixiao Powder combined with Western medicine as an effective and safer complementary treatment strategy for coronary heart disease patients experiencing angina pectoris.

title: International Practices in Managing Preconception, Pregnancy and Childbirth in Women With Glanzmann Thrombasthenia: A Survey From the European Association of Haemophilia and Allied Disorders (EAHAD)
summary: Glanzmann thrombasthenia is an inherited platelet disorder characterized by defects in platelet membrane glycoprotein αIIbβ3, resulting in severely reduced platelet aggregation and substantially elevated bleeding risk. Women with GT face significant reproductive challenges, including heavy menstrual bleeding, endometriosis, subfertility, and a postpartum hemorrhage rate of 34% compared to 6.4% in the general population, with additional risks of secondary hemorrhage and iron deficiency anemia. Pregnancy in GT patients triggers formation of anti-αIIbβ3 and anti-HLA antibodies, with the former potentially causing fetal/neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia and severe fetal bleeding including intracranial hemorrhage. Current management protocols prioritize comprehensive preconception counseling, screening for iron deficiency and pre-existing antibodies, avoidance of neuraxial anesthesia, and hemorrhage prevention using tranexamic acid, recombinant activated factor VII, and HLA-matched platelet transfusions while avoiding random-donor platelets that increase alloimmunization risk. Intravenous immunoglobulins are recommended for FNAIT prevention in women with rising αIIbβ3 antibody titers or prior intracranial hemorrhage, and assisted reproductive treatment is offered for conception difficulties. This multidisciplinary approach reflects international consensus on optimizing pregnancy outcomes in this high-risk population.

title: Spontaneous uterine rupture after myomectomy in patients during pregnancy: Clinical cases in a single university center
journal: International Journal of Gynaecology and Obstetrics
summary: This case series examines spontaneous uterine rupture during pregnancy following myomectomy, a complication occurring in approximately 0.2-0.4% of deliveries after fibroid removal. Both laparoscopic and open myomectomy procedures create uterine scars, with laparoscopic approaches showing slightly higher rupture rates (0.4% versus 0.2%), likely due to deeper myometrial penetration and intramural fibroid location. Key risk factors for rupture include entry into the uterine cavity, inadequate suturing technique (single versus double-layer closure), intramural myoma location, and underlying uterine pathology such as adenomyosis or endometriosis. Clinical presentation typically occurs in the third trimester with abdominal pain, irregular contractions, and fetal distress, progressing to life-threatening complications including massive hemoperitoneum, fetal bradycardia, and the need for emergency cesarean delivery or hysterectomy. While maternal and neonatal outcomes were generally favorable in this cohort, the devastating potential for fetal loss underscores the importance of careful surgical technique and appropriate counseling regarding vaginal birth after myomectomy, particularly in older women increasingly undergoing these procedures.

title: Risk Factors and Preoperative Prediction Model for Endometrial Lesions in Postmenopausal Patients with Benign Ovarian Tumors: A Retrospective Single-Center Study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective study identified independent risk factors for concurrent endometrial lesions in postmenopausal women with benign ovarian tumors, including earlier menarche age, increased endometrial thickness on ultrasound, solid tumor morphology, and elevated estradiol levels (≥10 pg/mL). The underlying pathophysiology involves unopposed estrogen exposure driving endometrial hyperplasia, which can progress to endometrial cancer if untreated, with irregular endometrial hyperplasia being the most common pathologic finding in patients with concurrent lesions. Additional clinical parameters such as FSH, LH, and platelet counts showed significant associations with endometrial lesions in this population. The authors developed a predictive nomogram using these independent risk factors to estimate the preoperative probability of concurrent endometrial pathology, which may help guide clinical decision-making regarding the extent of surgical intervention, including whether hysterectomy should accompany bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy in these patients.

title: Minimally Invasive Management of a Mullerian Anomaly: A Clinical Decision-Making Tree
journal: CRSLS : MIS Case Reports from SLS
summary: Müllerian duct anomalies (MDAs) are congenital structural abnormalities that frequently present with chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, and menorrhagia, and if left untreated can lead to serious complications including infection, endometriosis, pelvic abscess, adhesions, appendicitis, and infertility. This case describes a patient with a unicornuate uterus and noncommunicating rudimentary horn who developed hematometra and appendiceal endometriosis, a particularly high-risk complication occurring in up to 90% of MDA cases. Notably, renal anomalies are present in approximately 40% of patients with Müllerian agenesis and up to 95% in unilateral obstructed anomalies, necessitating thorough urological evaluation as part of the diagnostic workup. Minimally invasive laparoscopic resection of the rudimentary horn effectively treated the patient's pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and menorrhagia, with postoperative hormonal suppression addressing the concomitant endometriosis. The surgical approach demonstrated favorable obstetric outcomes, and patients with associated single kidney require cautious counseling regarding NSAID use. This case illustrates the importance of timely diagnosis through imaging modalities such as MRI and the critical role of early surgical intervention in preventing potentially severe complications in patients with MDAs.

title: Characteristics of transferrin saturation and anemia-related biomarkers in patients with uterine adenomyosis
summary: Adenomyosis is a benign gynecological disorder characterized by endometrial tissue invasion into the myometrium and is strongly associated with hypermenorrhea, dysmenorrhea, and obstetric complications including preterm labor and preeclampsia. The condition is marked by inflammatory pathology including macrophage infiltration, NF-κB activation, and elevated interleukin-6 (IL-6) expression by endometrial stromal cells; IL-6 subsequently induces hepatic hepcidin production via JAK/STAT3 signaling, which internalizes the iron exporter ferroportin and reduces systemic iron availability. Adenomyosis patients show significantly higher rates of functional iron deficiency compared to those with anemia alone, and transferrin saturation (TSAT) proves more sensitive than ferritin in detecting iron deficiency in this population, since ferritin is elevated in inflammatory conditions and may mask underlying iron depletion. Iron deficiency causes substantial clinical symptoms including fatigue, cognitive impairment, reduced exercise capacity, and headaches even in the absence of anemia, as iron is essential for oxidative metabolism, mitochondrial function, and neurotransmitter synthesis. Notably, in adenomyosis patients with hypermenorrhea, TSAT and hemoglobin levels show no correlation, indicating that iron-dependent symptoms may occur independently of anemia severity. Iron supplementation, particularly intravenous administration, effectively improves fatigue and exercise capacity in iron-deficient patients, suggesting that adenomyosis patients warrant routine assessment of iron status using TSAT to identify and treat functional iron deficiency.

title: Impact of adenomyosis on pregnancy outcomes following frozen embryo transfer: development and validation of a single-center clinical predictive model
summary: Adenomyosis is a complex, estrogen-dependent uterine disorder that significantly impairs fertility through multiple structural and molecular mechanisms. At the pathophysiological level, adenomyosis causes myometrial thickening, junctional zone disruption, and abnormal uterine peristalsis while inducing aberrant expression of implantation-related factors (HOXA10, HOXA11, LIF) and progesterone resistance alongside persistent chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation. Women with adenomyosis undergoing frozen embryo transfer exhibit substantially reduced clinical pregnancy and live birth rates with elevated miscarriage risk compared to unaffected populations. This study developed and validated a predictive model incorporating CA125 levels, uterine volume, junctional zone involvement, adenomyosis type (focal versus diffuse), dysmenorrhea severity, and pretreatment modality as independent predictors of pregnancy outcomes. The model demonstrated strong discriminatory ability (AUC 0.830) with stratified clinical pregnancy rates ranging from 11.1% in low-probability cases to 68.8% in high-probability cases, with medical therapy conferring significant protective benefit against adverse outcomes. These findings provide clinicians with a quantitative tool to risk-stratify adenomyosis patients and counsel on realistic pregnancy expectations following embryo transfer.

title: Robotic-integrated intraoperative ultrasound for excision of accessory cavitated uterine malformation (ACUM): an innovative surgical technique
summary:
# Summary

Accessory Cavitated Uterine Malformation (ACUM) is a rare congenital Müllerian anomaly predominantly affecting young nulliparous women, presenting with refractory dysmenorrhea, cyclic pelvic pain, and lateral lower abdominal pain due to functional endometrial tissue within the myometrium that undergoes cyclical hemorrhage. Medical management with NSAIDs, oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists, and progesterone provides only temporary symptom relief, necessitating definitive surgical intervention. Laparoscopic excision remains the gold standard for ACUM treatment, achieving complete symptom resolution in 90.7% of cases, though robotic-assisted approaches integrated with intraoperative ultrasound represent an innovative advancement offering real-time lesion localization, tissue plane identification, vascular mapping, and myometrial integrity assessment to enhance surgical precision. ACUM is frequently associated with concomitant endometriosis and adenomyosis—conditions characterized by immune dysregulation involving proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, IL-8, IL-17) and macrophage infiltration that impair fertility through reduced endometrial receptivity, sperm apoptosis, and compromised embryo implantation and placental development. Surgical excision improves reproductive outcomes and facilitates spontaneous conception in infertile patients, though ACUM remains unclassified in current rASRM and ESHRE/ESGE classification systems, highlighting the need for standardized diagnostic and management protocols.

title: A Bicornuate Uterus with a Unilateral Focal Adenomyosis: A Case Report
summary:
# Summary

This case report documents a 43-year-old woman with a bicornuate uterus presenting with focal adenomyosis confined to the left hemiuterus, characterized by ectopic endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium. The adenomyosis caused significant clinical manifestations including chronic pelvic pain, severe progressive dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, and secondary infertility, with imaging showing posterior wall thickening and heterogeneous myometrium. Multiple iatrogenic factors likely contributed to adenomyosis development, including two prior cesarean sections and curettage procedures, which disrupt the endometrial-myometrial interface and trigger myometrial disruption leading to basalis endometrium invagination. The pathophysiology involves a vicious cycle of myometrial contractions, estrogen release, auto-traumatization, and inflammation, potentially complicated by the patient's congenital uterine anomaly. Total abdominal hysterectomy provided complete symptom resolution with improved quality of life, confirming this as the gold standard management for adenomyosis-related pain when fertility is no longer desired.

title: Possible overestimation of treatment effects of pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy for early-stage ovarian clear cell carcinoma: a retrospective propensity-score weighted multi-center cohort study
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective propensity-score weighted study challenges the perceived benefit of pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenectomy for early-stage ovarian clear cell carcinoma (OCCC), which is frequently associated with endometriosis and distinguished by chemotherapy resistance in advanced disease. While lymphadenectomy is commonly performed as part of surgical treatment alongside hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, the analysis revealed that adhesions in the Douglas' pouch and cardiovascular disease—both more prevalent in the control group—were independently associated with recurrence, suggesting these confounding factors may overestimate the therapeutic effect of lymphadenectomy rather than reflecting its true benefit. The study identified that severe adhesions physically prevented complete lymphadenectomy and that pelvic adhesions, commonly linked to the endometriosis that underlies OCCC pathogenesis, were associated with poor prognosis. Additional recurrence risk factors included older age, larger preoperative cyst size, and thromboembolism complications, which frequently occur in OCCC and further compromise prognosis. The findings suggest that adjuvant chemotherapy, rather than lymph node dissection, may offer greater therapeutic benefit for stage I disease, and that baseline comorbidities substantially influenced treatment selection and outcomes in this population.

title: Combined pulmonary thrombectomy and kidney embolization as bailout to thrombolysis: a case report
summary: This case report describes a 47-year-old woman with short bowel syndrome resulting from endometriosis-complicated bowel perforation who presented with massive pulmonary embolism and acute right ventricular dysfunction. Although systemic thrombolysis initially improved hemodynamics, it triggered a life-threatening retroperitoneal hemorrhage, necessitating a combined bailout approach of catheter-based aspirational thrombectomy using the AlphaVac system and percutaneous kidney arterial embolization with microcoils and EmboSpheres. The combined intervention successfully resolved both the pulmonary thrombotic burden and the retroperitoneal bleeding, with rapid hemodynamic stabilization, restoration of pulmonary artery blood flow, and restoration of right ventricular function. This case demonstrates that percutaneous catheter-based thrombectomy represents an effective alternative when systemic thrombolysis fails or is contraindicated due to severe bleeding complications in high-risk pulmonary embolism patients. The successful integration of two complementary percutaneous interventions highlights the utility of multimodal catheter-based therapy in managing complex thromboembolic emergencies with concurrent hemorrhagic complications.

title: Impact of diabetes mellitus on stone access and surgical outcomes in patients undergoing ureteroscopic lithotripsy: a retrospective cohort study
summary: Diabetes mellitus impairs ureteroscopic lithotripsy outcomes through multiple interconnected pathological mechanisms affecting the ureter. The disease induces chronic microvascular damage, neuromuscular dysfunction, and connective tissue remodeling that collectively reduce smooth muscle contractility, tissue compliance, and peristaltic activity in the ureter. At the cellular level, diabetes stimulates hyperproliferation of ureteral smooth muscle cells via p-ERK/p-JNK/VEGF/PKC signaling pathways, which further compromises peristaltic function and structural adaptability of the ureteral wall. These morphologic and neuromuscular alterations significantly reduce the likelihood of successful scope passage to stones and decrease stone-free rates in diabetic patients undergoing ureteroscopic lithotripsy. Additionally, diabetes impairs immune response and increases mucosal trauma risk, leading to higher rates of infection and febrile urinary tract infection following endoscopic stone surgery. Multivariable analysis confirmed that diabetes mellitus is an independent predictor of surgical failure, independent of confounding factors such as age and body mass index.

title: Endometrial carcinoma risk prediction and gynecological comorbidity in adenomyosis: a 5648-case study
summary:
# Summary

This large-scale study of 5,648 cases establishes adenomyosis as a significant risk factor for endometrial carcinoma and documents extensive gynecological comorbidities associated with the condition. Adenomyosis frequently coexists with uterine leiomyoma (57.1% of cases), endometriosis (10.7%), and precancerous lesions including endometrial hyperplasia, polyps, and atypical polypoid adenomyoma, which may itself precede endometrial adenocarcinoma. The pathophysiology involves altered estrogen receptor and progesterone receptor expression, elevated matrix metalloproteinases, and dysregulation of signaling molecules including ERα, KRAS, and PTEN, with PTEN loss combined with KRAS and BCL2 mutations creating a molecular pathway toward endometrial carcinogenesis. Risk factors for adenomyosis include mid-40s age, multiparity, smoking, elevated follicle-stimulating hormone and prolactin, and prior cesarean delivery, while endometrial cancer risk is further elevated in older, postmenopausal women with excess body weight. The findings support adenomyosis as a precancerous condition warranting clinical surveillance for endometrial cancer development, particularly in patients with concurrent endometrial polyps or atypical hyperplasia.

title: The impact of histopathologically proven adenomyosis on surgical outcomes and total laparoscopic hysterectomy complication rates, types, and severity
summary: Adenomyosis is a pathology characterized by the ectopic presence of endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium, frequently associated with endometriosis, heavy menstrual bleeding, and endometrial hyperplasia. Risk factors for adenomyosis include estrogen-increasing conditions such as increased parity, early menarche, short menstrual cycles, elevated body mass index, and use of certain medications including tamoxifen and hormonal contraceptives, as well as a history of previous uterine surgery. Total laparoscopic hysterectomy is the standard definitive treatment for adenomyosis and offers advantages including reduced postoperative pain, decreased blood loss, and lower infection rates compared to other surgical approaches. Patients with histopathologically proven adenomyosis undergoing total laparoscopic hysterectomy had significantly larger uteri and higher postoperative complication rates, particularly postoperative bleeding and anemia, although serious complications such as bowel and vascular injuries remained rare at approximately 1.2% of cases. Adenomyosis was also associated with a modestly elevated risk of urinary tract and bladder injuries during hysterectomy, and severe endometriosis was significantly more prevalent in the adenomyosis group compared to controls. While definitive diagnosis requires histopathological examination following hysterectomy, advances in imaging modalities including transvaginal ultrasound and pelvic magnetic resonance imaging now enable preoperative detection of adenomyosis.

title: Incomplete obstructive diseases of the female genital tract: a classification analysis of 72 cases and a literature review
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective analysis of 72 patients classified incomplete obstructive diseases of the female genital tract into 24 distinct conditions resulting from developmental abnormalities. These malformations manifest through multiple clinical presentations including prolonged menstruation, dysmenorrhea, abnormal vaginal discharge, pelvic masses, and sexual dysfunction, with some cases complicated by severe infection and pelvic endometriosis. The study identified specific anatomical variants such as incomplete hymenal atresia, transverse and oblique vaginal septa, uterus didelphys with cervical atresia, and rudimentary horn uterus, each with characteristic complications ranging from tubo-ovarian abscess to ectopic pregnancy. Surgical interventions including hymenal incision, vaginoplasty, vaginal septum resection, cervical plasty, and rudimentary horn resection effectively treated these conditions, with 23 of 24 patients with incomplete vaginal obstruction achieving resolution of obstructive symptoms postoperatively. The findings emphasize the clinical importance of distinguishing incomplete obstructive diseases from primary dysmenorrhea and pelvic inflammatory disease to enable appropriate diagnosis and surgical management of these congenital malformations.

title: Comparison of 4DryField PH vs. seprafilm in reducing incidence of adhesion and preventing ASBO following abdominopelvic surgery: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

Post-surgical adhesions represent a major complication of abdominal and pelvic procedures, particularly laparotomies, with 68-100% of patients developing adhesions within hours following surgery. Surgical injury initiates an inflammatory cascade involving cytokine, growth factor, and cell adhesion molecule release that directly promotes adhesion formation, which can subsequently lead to serious sequelae including adhesive small bowel obstruction (ASBO), chronic pain, and infertility. This systematic review compared two anti-adhesion barriers—4DryField PH and Seprafilm—in preventing adhesion formation and ASBO following abdominopelvic surgery. 4DryField PH demonstrated superior efficacy across multiple measures, consistently reducing adhesion burden, severity, extent, and total adhesion scores in second-look studies and randomized controlled trials, particularly in endometriosis surgery populations, while also showing potential for reducing chronic pain symptoms. Seprafilm showed modest benefits in reducing postoperative small bowel obstruction, especially in laparoscopic procedures, though the evidence was less consistent across different surgical specialties including colorectal and gynecological procedures. Overall, both barriers work by creating temporary physical separations at surgically traumatized peritoneal surfaces to facilitate mesothelial healing, with 4DryField PH emerging as the more comprehensive option for adhesion reduction.

title: Constipation with megacolon or acute porphyria
summary: Acute intermittent porphyria (AIP) is a rare autosomal dominant metabolic disorder caused by hydroxymethylbilane synthase (HMBS) deficiency, which disrupts heme biosynthesis and leads to accumulation of neurotoxic precursors δ-aminolevulinic acid and porphobilinogen. This toxic accumulation triggers severe abdominal pain, gastrointestinal dysmotility, and autonomic neuropathy, with attacks frequently provoked by hormonal fluctuations, infections, medications, or fasting—making the disease particularly prevalent and symptomatic in women. Beyond acute pain management with opioids or acetaminophen, treatment strategies include heme infusion as the preferred acute therapy, high-carbohydrate supplementation to reduce precursor accumulation, and newer approaches such as givosiran (an RNA interference therapy targeting ALAS) to prevent attacks. In rare cases of severe, refractory gastrointestinal complications such as megacolon with marked colonic dilation and constipation, colectomy can provide complete resolution of abdominal pain, though histopathological examination typically reveals mucosal atrophy, vascular ectasia, and congestion from chronic disease. For patients with recurrent severe attacks, liver transplantation represents a definitive therapeutic option, while urine porphobilinogen testing and genetic analysis (identifying mutations such as HMBS exon 11 frameshift variants) are critical for diagnosis and familial screening.

title: Beyond Malignancy: Clinical Insights from Three Cases of Severe Hypercalcemia
summary:
# Summary

This case series illustrates the diverse etiologies and clinical consequences of severe hypercalcemia beyond malignancy. The three patients presented with hypercalcemia stemming from distinct mechanisms: lactation-associated hypercalcemia mediated by PTHrP secretion from mammary tissue, primary adrenal insufficiency complicated by autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 2, and primary hyperparathyroidism caused by a large parathyroid adenoma. Severe hypercalcemia produces multi-system complications including neurological manifestations (confusion, stupor), cardiac arrhythmias from QT interval shortening, acute kidney injury from volume depletion and reduced glomerular filtration, and gastrointestinal effects such as pancreatitis. Treatment approaches varied by etiology: lactation-associated hypercalcemia resolved with breastfeeding cessation, adrenal insufficiency responded to glucocorticoid and aldosterone replacement, and primary hyperparathyroidism required parathyroidectomy after initial stabilization with intravenous fluids, calcitonin, and zoledronic acid. These cases emphasize the importance of systematic evaluation using PTH measurement and appropriate biochemical testing to identify the underlying cause and guide targeted therapeutic intervention.

title: Robot-Assisted Laparoscopic Versus Open Adenomyomectomy: Comparative Surgical and Reproductive Outcomes
summary: Adenomyosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial infiltration into the myometrium, is associated with severe dysmenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, and infertility, with risk factors including early menarche, short menstrual cycles, elevated BMI, increased parity, and prior uterine surgery. This study compared robot-assisted laparoscopic adenomyomectomy (RLA) with open adenomyomectomy (OA), both uterus-sparing surgical approaches, finding that RLA resulted in significantly lower estimated blood loss (300 versus 400 mL), fewer blood transfusions (14.6% versus 42.9%), reduced intraoperative endometrial exposure (10.0% versus 47.6%), and shorter hospital stays (3 versus 5 days), despite longer operative times. The robotic approach's three-dimensional visualization and precise lesion-myometrium discrimination facilitated meticulous reconstruction while minimizing complications, leading to superior reproductive outcomes with four of six deliveries being full-term in the RLA group compared to all preterm births in the OA group. Postoperative management with levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine systems, GnRH agonists, or progestins effectively suppressed residual adenomyotic activity and prevented recurrence. These findings demonstrate that robot-assisted laparoscopic adenomyomectomy offers superior surgical and reproductive outcomes compared to open surgery while preserving uterine function in symptomatic adenomyosis patients.

title: An AI-Driven Clinical Decision Support Model Based on Anemia and Fibroid Parameters to Guide Surgical Decision-Making
summary: Uterine fibroids cause heavy menstrual bleeding that leads to iron-deficiency anemia, with both fibroid burden (number and volume) and anemia severity driving the need for surgical intervention. Machine learning models incorporating fibroid characteristics alongside iron-storage biomarkers—particularly ferritin—achieved near-perfect accuracy (98–99%) in predicting surgical necessity, substantially outperforming hemoglobin-based models which showed reduced sensitivity. The "UF number + Ferritin" and "UF Volume + Ferritin" models demonstrated the strongest predictive performance, with test accuracies of 0.99 and 0.98 respectively, suggesting that iron storage depletion represents a critical physiological threshold linked to operative necessity. Notably, hemoglobin and ferritin concentrations were significantly reduced in patients requiring surgery compared to those managed conservatively, while fibroid volume was significantly greater in the surgical cohort. This AI-driven clinical decision support algorithm achieved 98% concordance with blinded gynecologist assessments, providing an objective, highly accurate tool for guiding surgical decision-making in fibroid patients by integrating hematologic and morphologic parameters.

title: The value of coagulation index in thromboelastography for predicting early pregnancy loss in in vitro fertilization (IVF)/intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI) cycles
summary: This study demonstrates that thromboelastography (TEG)-derived coagulation parameters, particularly the coagulation index, are significant predictors of early pregnancy loss (EPL) in IVF/ICSI cycles. Multivariate analysis revealed that a coagulation index >0.75 increases EPL risk by 22.2% per unit increase, alongside established risk factors including ovulatory dysfunction, thyroid and endocrine disorders, and higher parity. The underlying mechanism involves thrombophilia-induced placental microthrombosis, which triggers trophoblast apoptosis and impairs placental development, ultimately leading to pregnancy loss. TEG provides superior assessment of global coagulation-fibrinolysis balance compared to conventional coagulation markers, identifying abnormalities in reaction time, kinetics time, alpha angle, and fibrinolytic parameters that associate with EPL. The study supports using TEG as a clinical tool for identifying hypercoagulable patients who may benefit from prophylactic low-molecular-weight heparin therapy to prevent thrombosis and improve placental blood flow. These findings suggest that coagulation status represents a modifiable risk factor in assisted reproduction, potentially improving pregnancy outcomes through targeted anticoagulation strategies.

title: Evaluating patient harm minimization during the COVID-19-driven reduction in benign gynecological care: a nationwide claims-based longitudinal study in the Netherlands
summary:
# Summary

This nationwide claims-based study examined how COVID-19 disruptions to elective gynecological care affected treatment patterns across multiple benign uterine conditions in the Netherlands. The pandemic caused significant reductions in overall patient volumes across all care pathways in 2020, with increased telephone consultations replacing in-person visits. However, the data reveal important adaptive shifts in treatment selection: while overall surgical volumes declined, there was a notable increase in less-invasive and office-based procedures such as hysteroscopy, intrauterine device insertion, and uterine artery embolization for conditions like heavy menstrual bleeding and uterine fibroids. In the prolapse pathway, surgical treatment shifted toward less morbid techniques including vaginal vault suspensions and colporrhaphy, suggesting providers preferentially selected conservative surgical options when procedures could proceed. By 2021-2022, hysteroscopic approaches gained further prominence across pathways, while medical therapies remained stable compared to pre-pandemic levels. These findings suggest that pandemic-driven care reductions led to harm minimization through preferential use of less invasive alternatives rather than universal delays in treatment.

title: Causal risk and protective factors for rheumatoid arthritis: a comprehensive mendelian randomization systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This Mendelian randomization meta-analysis systematically evaluated causal risk and protective factors for rheumatoid arthritis, identifying smoking behavior, hypothyroidism, elevated body mass index, and inflammatory cytokines (interleukins-6 and interleukins-17) as significant causative factors with odds ratios ranging from 1.39 to 2.27. Protective factors included higher cognitive function and educational attainment, as well as interleukin-1 receptor antagonist activity, each conferring approximately 18-26% risk reduction. Notably, elevated basal metabolic rate emerged as a shared causal risk factor for both seropositive and seronegative rheumatoid arthritis phenotypes, suggesting metabolic dysfunction contributes to disease pathogenesis regardless of autoantibody status. The analysis highlights thyroid function management and smoking cessation as critical, evidence-based prevention targets, while identifying conflicting bidirectional relationships for sleep deprivation and sarcopenia that warrant further investigation. RA's pathogenic effects include persistent joint inflammation, pain, swelling, deformity, and in severe cases, permanent disability. These findings provide a comprehensive causal framework for understanding rheumatoid arthritis etiology and identifying potentially modifiable risk factors for disease prevention and intervention strategies.

title: The Varied Presentation of Appendiceal Mucinous Neoplasms: A Rural Case Series
summary:
# Summary

Appendiceal mucinous neoplasms (AMNs) are rare heterogeneous tumors with rising incidence that pose significant clinical challenges due to their propensity to rupture and cause pseudomyxoma peritonei (PMP), a serious condition characterized by peritoneal dissemination of mucinous material. Low-grade AMNs can progress to invasive adenocarcinoma, and the presence of extra-appendiceal cellular mucin substantially increases PMP risk and necessitates aggressive cytoreductive surgery combined with hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy. Diagnostic computed tomography serves as the gold standard for preoperative detection, while elevated tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9, CA 125) may support diagnosis; however, inflammatory markers are non-specific. Surgical management is stratified by pathology: acellular mucin without peritoneal disease is managed by simple appendicectomy with negative margins, whereas invasive adenocarcinoma or positive margins require right hemicolectomy, and PMP necessitates comprehensive cytoreductive procedures including omentectomy and cholecystectomy. This case series underscores the importance of prompt recognition and appropriate surgical approach, as delayed diagnosis and inadvertent laparoscopic manipulation of large appendiceal masses increase rupture risk and dissemination.

title: Direct medical costs and length of stay associated with postoperative systemic infectious complications after gynecologic surgery: a propensity score–matched case–control study
summary:
# Summary

Postoperative systemic infectious complications (PSIC) following gynecologic surgery impose substantial economic and clinical burdens, independently driving an absolute increase of ¥8,960.74 in total medical costs and prolonging hospitalization by 4.20 days, with patients experiencing median stays of 9 days compared to 5 days in uninfected controls. The heightened costs reflect multifaceted resource demands including extended antimicrobial therapy, intensive monitoring for sepsis sequelae, and repeated diagnostic testing such as blood cultures and organ function assessments. Systemic infections such as bloodstream infections particularly escalate economic impact, nearly doubling total hospital costs and extending stays by more than 7.5 days in intensive care settings. Prevention strategies including appropriate antimicrobial prophylaxis, preoperative vaginal preparation, chlorhexidine bathing, and meticulous aseptic technique demonstrate effectiveness in reducing PSIC incidence and associated downstream complications. These findings underscore the critical importance of infection prevention bundles in gynecologic surgery to mitigate both the direct financial burden and extended recovery timelines that compromise patient outcomes and healthcare resource allocation.

title: Vaginal Lengthening Procedures for Noncongenital Indications
summary: This article examines surgical and medical approaches to treating vaginal shortening from both iatrogenic and disease-related causes. Vaginal length reduction results from multiple etiologies including pelvic radiation, radical hysterectomy, prolapse repair, graft-versus-host disease, and medical conditions such as lichen sclerosus and post-menopausal changes. Treatment options vary by underlying cause: dilator therapy serves as first-line management for all indications, while surgical approaches include buccal grafting, split-thickness skin grafts, pudendal thigh fasciocutaneous (Singapore) flaps, and acellular porcine biologic grafts, each with distinct advantages and complications. For adhesion-related shortening, lysis of adhesions is considered the gold standard, though lichen planus and prior pelvic radiation may limit efficacy. Long-term success across all interventions critically depends on maintaining vaginal patency through continued dilation or regular intercourse, as discontinuation leads to recurrent obliteration and shortened length. The article emphasizes that multimodal management combining appropriate surgical reconstruction with aggressive postoperative dilation protocols optimizes functional outcomes and patient satisfaction.

title: Right postoperative pleural effusion and pulmonary embolism following laparoscopic gynecological surgery: A rare case report and PRISMA-driven systematic review
journal: Journal of the Chinese Medical Association : JCMA
summary:
# Summary

This case report describes a rare postoperative complication in a 53-year-old woman with ovarian endometriosis who underwent laparoscopic bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy and developed both right massive pleural effusion and pulmonary embolism. The patient presented with hypoxia, elevated D-dimer levels, and imaging findings consistent with pulmonary embolism and lung atelectasis on postoperative day one. Multiple pathophysiological mechanisms can precipitate pleural effusion after laparoscopic gynecological surgery, including prolonged operative time, increased intraperitoneal pressure from CO2 insufflation, Trendelenburg positioning, and diaphragmatic defects. The patient was successfully treated with pleural pigtail drainage, anticoagulation therapy with rivaroxaban, and supportive care with albumin and diuretics. The systematic review identifies multiple risk factors for postoperative venous thromboembolism following gynecological surgery, including obesity, prolonged operative time, and advanced age, while emphasizing that minimally invasive surgery carries significantly lower VTE risk than open approaches. Mechanical and pharmacologic prophylaxis strategies are recommended to prevent these serious postoperative complications.

title: Chronic Endometritis and Recurrent Pregnancy Loss: A review of evidence and underlying mechanisms
summary:
# Summary

Chronic endometritis (CE) is significantly associated with recurrent pregnancy loss and represents an often-underdiagnosed contributor to reproductive failure. The pathophysiology involves dysbiosis of the endometrial microbiome, wherein pathogenic bacteria such as Streptococcus, Escherichia coli, Enterococcus faecalis, and Mycoplasma/Ureaplasma species trigger inflammatory cascades that elevate pro-inflammatory cytokines, impair embryonic implantation and pregnancy maintenance, and disrupt endometrial gene expression by downregulating receptivity and decidualization genes while upregulating anti-apoptotic markers. Diagnosis combines hysteroscopic findings (endometrial micropolyps, stromal edema, increased vascularization) with CD-138 immunohistochemical detection of plasma cells in the endometrial stroma, with plasma cell counts exceeding 4–6 cells per high-power field indicating elevated miscarriage risk. Targeted antibiotic treatment, individualized based on bacterial culture and antibiogram results, successfully resolves CE in 68.5–88.3% of cases and substantially improves reproductive outcomes, increasing live birth rates from 44% in untreated women to 85% in treated women, with normalized hysteroscopy findings following treatment associated with live birth rates reaching 78.4%.

title: Surgical Intervention of Huge Uterine Polyp and its Oncogenic Potential and Clinical Implications
summary:
# Summary

Endometrial polyps are benign overgrowths of the endometrial lining associated with abnormal uterine bleeding, infertility, and pelvic discomfort, with pathogenesis involving hormonal imbalances, chronic inflammation, and genetic factors. Giant polyps exceeding 10 cm are rare but cause severe complications including menorrhagia, anemia, urinary retention, and bladder or urethra obstruction through direct compression of adjacent pelvic structures and neurovascular compromise. Risk factors for polyp development include advanced age, postmenopausal status, and tamoxifen therapy, which promotes formation of large, vascularized polyps that increase hemorrhage risk and secondary anemia. The malignant transformation potential of endometrial polyps is associated with unopposed estrogen exposure, chronic inflammation, obesity, diabetes, and PTEN mutations. While MRI and hysteroscopy are valuable diagnostic tools for visualization, hysteroscopic polypectomy treats smaller polyps minimally invasively, whereas hysterectomy remains the definitive treatment for massive polyps, as demonstrated in three presented cases with complete symptom resolution and confirmed benign histopathology.

title: Asymptomatic Uterine Rupture at 20 Weeks of Gestation: A Case Report and Review of Literature
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: Uterine rupture is a serious obstetric complication with multiple risk factors and etiologies, most commonly occurring after previous cesarean section. Prostaglandin analogs, particularly Misoprostol used for labor induction in cases of intrauterine fetal demise or second-trimester abortion, significantly increase rupture risk, especially in patients with scarred uteri. Additional risk factors include multiparity, advanced maternal age, endometriosis, placenta previa, prior dilation and curettage, myomectomy, irradiation, and vertical hysterotomy. Second-trimester ruptures are primarily associated with trauma, induced pregnancy terminations in scarred uteri, and placenta accreta spectrum. Clinical manifestations include severe pelvic pain, vaginal bleeding, and hemodynamic instability, which necessitate prompt diagnosis via ultrasound or computed tomography. Emergency surgical intervention is required to manage this life-threatening condition.

title: Influence of hormonal status in hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia – analysis of an online patient questionnaire
summary:
# Summary

Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT) is a rare vascular disorder caused by pathogenic variants in transforming growth factor β pathway genes, characterized by mucocutaneous telangiectasia and recurrent epistaxis affecting over 90% of patients. This online questionnaire study reveals that hormonal status significantly influences HHT symptom severity, particularly epistaxis, with female patients reporting increased symptoms during puberty, menstruation, and pregnancy, especially in the second trimester. Combined estrogen and progestin therapy reduced epistaxis in 52% of treated patients, while the lactation period paradoxically improved symptoms in most women, suggesting a protective hormonal effect. Female HHT patients experience more severe epistaxis with advancing age compared to males, and many require hormone therapy for associated conditions including hypermenorrhea, dysmenorrhea, and menstrual irregularities. Notably, 40% of female respondents underwent gynecological surgery, with hysterectomy being most common, and HHT type 2 patients showed greater symptom worsening following such procedures. Multiple treatment modalities including topical nasal medications, laser therapy, and hormone-based interventions are available to manage epistaxis and improve quality of life in this patient population.

title: Gastric peroral endoscopic myotomy for compassionate treatment of symptomatic gastroparesis in patients on chronic opioids
summary: Gastroparesis, characterized by delayed gastric emptying and associated symptoms including nausea, vomiting, early satiety, bloating, and abdominal pain, is particularly challenging in patients on chronic opioids, which exacerbate the condition and reduce the efficacy of conventional treatments like prokinetics and gastric electric stimulation. This study demonstrates that gastric peroral endoscopic myotomy (G-POEM) represents an effective compassionate treatment option for opioid-dependent patients with medically refractory gastroparesis, with clinical response rates between 50-80% achieved through mechanical improvement of pyloric diameter and distensibility. Patients responding to G-POEM exhibited significantly lower baseline pyloric measurements and showed substantial post-procedure increases in pyloric diameter and distensibility index, accompanied by significant reductions in symptom severity scores, while nonresponders demonstrated no such improvements. Baseline pyloric characteristics measured by functional luminal imaging probe (FLIP) proved predictive of treatment response, with lower baseline measurements and distensibility index values correlating with clinical success. Favorable prognostic factors for G-POEM success included older age, shorter symptom duration, nondiabetic etiology, and predominant symptoms beyond abdominal pain alone. The findings establish FLIP as a valuable diagnostic tool for assessing pyloric sphincter characteristics and predicting G-POEM outcomes in this challenging patient population.

title: Clinical efficacy analysis of high-intensity focused ultrasound ablation in treating fibroadenomas of different breast gland types: a retrospective study
summary: Breast fibroadenomas are common benign tumors composed of fibrous and glandular tissue that develop in association with excessive estrogen stimulation and can cause anxiety and physical discomfort. Traditional surgical excision carries significant risks including postoperative bleeding, infection, and damage to mammary ducts, motivating the development of alternative treatment approaches. High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) has emerged as a safe and effective non-invasive percutaneous ablation technique that treats fibroadenomas while preserving breast tissue and avoiding surgical complications. The efficacy of HIFU varies by breast gland type, with fatty breast tissue showing the highest volume reduction rates (72.9%) and dense breast tissue showing the lowest (55.4%), while loose breast tissue required significantly higher surgical power for treatment. Younger patients with dense breast tissue presented distinct clinical characteristics including lower body mass index, indicating that patient and tissue factors influence treatment outcomes. Overall, HIFU represents a superior alternative to traditional surgery for fibroadenoma management across different breast gland types.

title: Phenol Neurolysis in Pain and Palliative Medicine
summary:
# Summary: Phenol Neurolysis in Pain and Palliative Medicine

Phenol is a chemical neurolytic agent increasingly used to treat refractory cancer pain and localized pain syndromes when standard analgesics prove insufficient or produce substantial side effects. At low concentrations, phenol functions as a local anesthetic, while at higher doses it causes dose-dependent cytotoxic effects through protein degradation, demyelination, and axonal edema that disrupt pain signaling for 2 to 12 months before neuronal regeneration restores function. Phenol's amphiphilic character allows rapid systemic absorption and distribution, with metabolism involving sulfotransferase, glucuronosyl transferase, and CYP2E1-mediated oxidative pathways, though systemic toxicity can produce serious adverse effects including hemolysis, cardiac arrhythmias, renal failure, and metabolic acidosis. Clinically, phenol neurolysis targets various sympathetic plexuses and ganglia to relieve visceral and cancer-related pain—including coeliac plexus blockade for upper abdominal malignancy pain, splanchnic nerve blocks for pancreatic cancer pain, and superior/inferior hypogastric plexus blocks for pelvic pain—while reducing opioid consumption and associated constipation. However, neuraxial administration carries significant risks of motor weakness, numbness, and bladder or bowel dysfunction, necessitating careful patient selection and technical precision for safe therapeutic benefit.

title: Incidence and Risk Factors of Postoperative Urinary Retention in Gynecologic Surgery: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Postoperative urinary retention (POUR) represents a significant complication following gynecologic surgery, with incidence varying substantially by procedure type—highest after pelvic organ prolapse surgery (30.9%) and sling procedures (25.3%), but lowest after minimally invasive hysterectomy (3.7%). Multiple patient and perioperative risk factors contribute to POUR, including baseline voiding dysfunction, diabetes mellitus, vaginal surgical approach, and opioid exposure, with diabetes acting through impaired parasympathetic regulation of bladder contraction via autonomic neuropathy. The pathophysiology involves multiple mechanisms including periurethral tissue edema, inflammatory responses, pelvic neural pathway disruption, and pharmacologic suppression of detrusor contractility by anesthesia and opioids. POUR can lead to serious complications including bladder overdistension, detrusor muscle injury, urinary tract infection, and delayed postoperative recovery. Preventive strategies—such as perioperative alpha-blockers, early pelvic floor muscle training, liberal voiding protocols, and minimally invasive surgical techniques—demonstrate protective effects and represent important clinical interventions to reduce POUR risk in high-risk gynecologic surgery populations.

title: Vaginal bleeding in a newborn as initial presentation of uterus didelphys
summary: Vaginal bleeding in newborn girls is typically a physiological response to maternal estrogen withdrawal, but unusually heavy bleeding can signal underlying congenital anomalies. This case report describes an infant presenting with notable vaginal hemorrhage that prompted diagnostic imaging and revealed uterus didelphys, a Müllerian anomaly characterized by complete duplication of the uterine cavity. Neonates with uterus didelphys are predisposed to heavier vaginal bleeding than typical because the presence of two separate endometrial cavities results in a relatively larger total amount of endometrial tissue responding to hormonal changes. The diagnosis of Müllerian anomalies such as uterus didelphys is conventionally made during adolescence or adulthood, but this case demonstrates that earlier detection through imaging in symptomatic neonates is clinically valuable. Early identification of such structural reproductive anomalies facilitates timely medical and surgical management and allows for better patient counseling regarding potential reproductive complications in the future.
